This double programmability allows P-term sharing across multiple outputs, yielding the highest logic density per fuse for complex multi-output functions. Older PLAs were not reprogrammable; modern PLA structures within CPLDs use EEPROM/Flash.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Output Logic Macro Cell (OLMC)" and list its three main configuration modes.*

**Model Answer:**
An **OLMC** is the configurable output stage of a GAL (and similar modern SPLDs) that allows the user to dynamically select the structure and behavior of each output pin.

**Three main configuration modes:**
1. **Combinational Mode** — output is the direct OR-gate result, available in both active-HIGH and active-LOW polarities (selected via the polarity MUX).
2. **Registered Mode** — the OR-gate output is clocked into a D flip-flop, providing sequential / synchronous logic with feedback to the AND-plane.
3. **Three-State Mode** — an output-enable product term controls a 3-state buffer, allowing the pin to act as a bi-directional I/O or to be placed in high-impedance for bus multiplexing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> (1) Students frequently forget to mark intact vs blown fuses with an explicit **"×"** symbol on the AND/OR plane diagram — this alone costs **2 marks**.
> (2) When asked for PLA implementation, students must show **P-term sharing** across outputs; failing to highlight shared terms loses **1–2 marks**.
> (3) For FPGA questions, examiners expect the answer to discuss **SRAM volatility + external boot PROM**; skipping this loses **2 marks**.
> (4) Always state the **unit** (ns, MHz, V) alongside numerical values.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a **CPLD**. Compare its architecture with that of an **FPGA** in terms of logic density, non-volatility, and typical application areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement the following Boolean function using a **PLA**:

$$
F_1(A,B,C,D) = \sum m(0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 9, 12, 13)
$$

$$
F_2(A,B,C,D) = \sum m(1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15)
$$

Identify the shared product terms and justify the PLA's advantage over using two independent PALs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CPLD Architecture (7 marks):**

A CPLD consists of multiple **Logic Array Blocks (LABs)**, each containing **16 macrocells**, interconnected through a central **Programmable Interconnect Array (PIA)**.

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
- **LAB / PLA Block** — contains a programmable AND-plane and OR-plane feeding a macrocell.
- **Macrocell** — comprises an OR gate, an EXOR gate for polarity control, a D flip-flop, and a 3-state output buffer.
- **PIA** — central switching matrix that routes signals between LABs and I/O blocks.
- **I/O Block** — provides input/output buffering, slew-rate control, and pull-up resistors.
- **Non-volatile configuration memory** (EEPROM or Flash) stores the user's design permanently.

**CPLD vs FPGA comparison table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | CPLD | FPGA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Basic building block | Macrocell (PAL-like) | Look-Up Table (LUT) + Flip-flop |
| Logic density | 100 – 100 K gates | **100 K – 10 M+ gates** |
| Non-volatile configuration | **Yes** (Flash/EEPROM) | No (SRAM-based); Flash variants exist |
| Predictable timing | **Yes** (fixed interconnect) | Variable (routing-dependent) |
| Power-on instant-on | **Yes** | No (requires bitstream load) |
| Typical applications | Glue logic, power sequencing, boot controllers | DSP, video processing, SoC prototyping, AI acceleration |

---

**(b) PLA Implementation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert minterms to product terms (2 marks):**

| Minterm | Binary $ABCD$ | Product Term |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m_0$ | 0 0 0 0 | $\overline{A}\overline{B}\overline{C}\overline{D}$ |
| $m_1$ | 0 0 0 1 | $\overline{A}\overline{B}\overline{C}D$ |
| $m_2$ | 0 0 1 0 | $\overline{A}\overline{B}C\overline{D}$ |
| $m_4$ | 0 1 0 0 | $\overline{A}B\overline{C}\overline{D}$ |
| $m_8$ | 1 0 0 0 | $A\overline{B}\overline{C}\overline{D}$ |
| $m_9$ | 1 0 0 1 | $A\overline{B}\overline{C}D$ |
| $m_{12}$ | 1 1 0 0 | $AB\overline{C}\overline{D}$ |
| $m_{13}$ | 1 1 0 1 | $AB\overline{C}D$ |
| $m_3$ | 0 0 1 1 | $\overline{A}\overline{B}CD$ |
| $m_5$ | 0 1 0 1 | $\overline{A}B\overline{C}D$ |
| $m_7$ | 0 1 1 1 | $\overline{A}BCD$ |
| $m_{11}$ | 1 0 1 1 | $A\overline{B}CD$ |
| $m_{15}$ | 1 1 1 1 | $ABCD$ |

**Step 2 — Identify shared P-terms (2 marks):**
- $P_9 = A\overline{B}\overline{C}D$ appears in both $F_1$ and $F_2$ → **shared**.
- $P_{13} = AB\overline{C}D$ appears in both $F_1$ and $F_2$ → **shared**.

**Step 3 — Express outputs (2 marks):**

$$
F_1 = m_0 + m_1 + m_2 + m_4 + m_8 + P_9 + m_{12} + P_{13}
$$

$$
F_2 = P_9 + m_3 + m_5 + m_7 + m_{11} + P_{13} + m_{15}
$$

**Step 4 — Justify PLA's advantage (1 mark):**
A PLA with P-term sharing uses only **13 distinct product lines** instead of 16 (8 + 8). If two separate PALs were used, both AND-planes would independently implement $P_9$ and $P_{13}$, doubling the silicon real-estate for those rows. The PLA's programmable OR-plane allows the shared P-terms to feed multiple outputs, saving fuses and reducing propagation delay.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an **SRAM-based FPGA** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of **LUTs, switch matrices, BRAM, and PLL blocks**. Why is an external configuration PROM necessary? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a clear architectural diagram, explain the working of an **Output Logic Macro Cell (OLMC)** in a GAL device. Describe how the polarity multiplexer and feedback path enable flexible output configuration. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SRAM-Based FPGA Architecture (7 marks):**

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
- **CLB / Logic Element (LE):** Basic logic unit containing a $k$-input LUT (typically $k=4, 6,$ or $7$), a carry chain, and a flip-flop.
- **Switch Matrix / Routing Channels:** Programmable interconnect wires and NMOS pass transistors (controlled by SRAM cells) that route signals between CLBs.
- **Block RAM (BRAM):** Dedicated 18 Kb / 36 Kb dual-port SRAM blocks for on-chip data storage, FIFOs, and lookup tables.
- **Clock Management Tiles (PLL / DLL):** Generate multiple clock frequencies from a single reference, with phase shifting and skew control.
- **Hard IP cores:** PCIe, Ethernet MAC, ADC, high-speed transceivers (SERDES).
- **I/O Blocks:** Support multiple voltage standards (LVCMOS, LVDS, HSTL) with adjustable slew rate.

**Function of key blocks (3 marks):**
- **LUT** — implements any Boolean function of $k$ variables by storing its truth table in $2^{k}$ SRAM bits. A 6-input LUT can implement any 6-input function or two independent 5-input functions.
- **Switch matrix** — provides programmable routing; the configuration bitstream controls whether each pass transistor is ON or OFF, effectively wiring up the entire logic fabric.
- **BRAM** — used for data buffering, register files, and small memory arrays in DSP pipelines.
- **PLL** — synthesizes high-frequency clocks (e.g., 100 MHz from a 50 MHz crystal) with programmable multiplication/division.

**External configuration PROM necessity (1 mark):**
SRAM cells are **volatile** — they lose their contents when power is removed. Therefore, on every power-up, the FPGA must reload its configuration bitstream from an external **non-volatile memory** (typically a SPI Flash PROM). Without this external PROM, the FPGA would be unconfigured and non-functional after every power cycle.

---

**(b) OLMC Architecture (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
Draw the OLMC with the following labelled sub-blocks:
1. **8-input OR gate** — receives P-terms from the AND-plane.
2. **Polarity Multiplexer (2:1 MUX)** — controlled by configuration bit; selects between OR-gate output and its complement, allowing the user to implement either active-HIGH or active-LOW logic without altering the AND-plane.
3. **D Flip-Flop** — optional (configuration-selected); when included, the OLMC operates in registered (sequential) mode.
4. **Output-Enable (OE) Multiplexer** — selects whether the output is always enabled, always disabled, or controlled by a product term from the AND-plane.
5. **3-State Buffer** — drives the output pin; can be placed in high-impedance state.
6. **Feedback Path** — routes the FF output (or the combinational output) back to the AND-plane, enabling the implementation of sequential circuits and iterative logic networks.

**Polarity MUX explanation (2 marks):**
The polarity MUX is a programmable XOR-like structure. When configured for active-LOW output, it negates the OR-gate result. This is crucial for Karnaugh-map-optimized implementations where the ON-set and OFF-set functions are equally valid — the designer can choose whichever yields fewer product terms.

**Feedback path explanation (2 marks):**
The feedback path allows the registered output $Q$ to be re-injected into the AND-plane input bus. This enables:
- **State machine implementation** — present state $Q$ is fed back to compute next state.
- **Latched I/O** — pin can act as both input and output in different clock phases.
- **Buried logic** — internal nodes can use FF outputs without exposing them to the package pin, freeing the pin for other uses.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- **PLD =** Programmable Logic Device; user-customized IC replacing discrete gates/ASICs.
- **Three classical SPLDs:** PROM, PAL, PLA.
- **Mnemonic:** *"PROM = Fixed AND; PAL = Fixed OR; PLA = Both Free."*
- **Minterm count** for $n$ inputs = $2^{n}$.
- **GAL** = CMOS, EEPROM-based, reprogrammable PAL with OLMC.
- **OLMC has 5 sub-blocks:** OR gate, Polarity MUX, D-FF, OE MUX, 3-state buffer.
- **CPLD:** Multiple macrocells + PIA + non-volatile Flash → instant-on, predictable timing.
- **FPGA:** LUT-based (typically 6-input), SRAM-volatile, requires external boot PROM.
- **LUT size:** $2^{k}$ bits for $k$-input LUT ($k = 4, 6, 7$ in modern FPGAs).
- **Programming techs:** Bipolar fuse (1×), Anti-fuse (1×), EPROM (UV), EEPROM/Flash (many×), SRAM (unlimited, volatile).
- **P-term sharing in PLA** saves fuses and silicon — always highlight shared terms in exam answers.
- **Hard IP cores in FPGAs:** PCIe, Ethernet MAC, ADC, SERDES, ARM Cortex, DSP slices, BRAM, PLL.
- **Carry chain** dramatically accelerates arithmetic in FPGAs (32-bit adder: ~0.1 ns/stage vs 0.5 ns).
- **Always mark fuses** with "×" on AND/OR plane diagrams; examiners deduct marks if missing.
- **Always include units** (ns, MHz, mA, V) when stating numerical values.
- **For PLA problems:** Write the SOP expansion, identify shared P-terms, and draw both AND and OR planes.
- **For FPGA problems:** Discuss volatility, bitstream loading, and the role of the external boot Flash.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — PLDs Notes.** These notes are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, **OEAET723 — Embedded Systems** syllabus, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze). For further reading, refer to standard digital design textbooks and the official KTU syllabus document available on the university website.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Off-The-Shelf Components (COTS), Memory - ROM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET723 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Commercial Off-The-Shelf Components (COTS), Memory - ROM # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Commercial Off-The-Shelf Components (COTS) ### Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf Components)** are pre-designed, pre-manufactured, and readily available hardware or software components that can be purchased from commercial vendors and integrated directly into an embedded system without requiring custom design, fabrication, or significant modification. In the context of **embedded system design**, COTS components are standardized building blocks (microcontrollers, sensors, memory ICs, communication modules, RTOS kernels, etc.) procured from the open commercial market and deployed as-is to accelerate prototyping, reduce non-recurring engineering (NRE) costs, and shorten the time-to-market for embedded products. ### Key Characteristics of COTS Components | Characteristic | Description | |---|---| | **Availability** | Ready-to-purchase from authorized distributors and vendors | | **Standardization** | Conforms to industry-standard interfaces, footprints, and protocols | | **Cost-Effectiveness** | Low per-unit cost due to mass production economies of scale | | **Documentation** | Vendor-provided datasheets, application notes, and reference designs | | **Limited Customization** | Cannot be modified at the silicon/firmware level by the end user | | **Vendor Dependency** | Long-term reliability tied to the manufacturer's product lifecycle | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of COTS like buying a pre-fabricated modular kitchen cabinet from IKEA instead of hiring a carpenter to build one from scratch.** > > The cabinet arrives ready-to-assemble with standard dimensions, a clear instruction manual, and known quality. You bolt it into your house, connect it to the plumbing, and it works. You didn't design the wood joints, you didn't cut the panels — you simply *integrated* a finished, tested component. > > Similarly, in embedded systems, an engineer can plug a COTS temperature sensor (e.g., **LM35**, **DHT22**) onto a PCB, write a few lines of driver code, and the system "just works." No ASIC fabrication, no custom silicon — just integration of tested, market-ready parts. --- ## 1.2 Memory — ROM (Read-Only Memory) ### Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** is a non-volatile semiconductor memory device that retains its stored data even when power is removed. In embedded systems, ROM is primarily used to store the firmware/boot program, lookup tables, and constant data that must persist across power cycles. Unlike RAM (Random Access Memory), ROM is **write-protected or write-restricted** under normal operating conditions, making it ideal for storing permanent program code and microcode. ### Physical / Electrical Metrics - **Non-volatile retention time:** typically **10 to 100 years** without power - **Access time:** ranges from **~25 ns (fast ROM)** to **~200 ns (legacy EPROM)** - **Density:** modern Flash ROM reaches **gigabit (Gb) scale** per IC ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **ROM is like a printed book sitting on your shelf.** > > Once the publisher prints the book (data is written during manufacturing or programming), you can read it as many times as you want — even during a power outage. However, you cannot easily rewrite the contents the way you would scribble in a notebook (RAM). Some "special editions" (EPROM/EEPROM/Flash) allow you to erase and rewrite, but it requires special effort — like reprinting specific pages. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 COTS — Operational Reasoning & Engineering Trade-offs ### Why Use COTS in Embedded Systems? The decision to use COTS components is driven by five engineering and economic factors: 1. **Reduced Time-to-Market** - Custom ASIC design cycles range from **12 to 24 months**, whereas COTS components can be sourced and integrated in **days to weeks**. - Critical in consumer electronics where product obsolescence is rapid (**18-month lifecycle**). 2. **Lower Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) Costs** - A custom ASIC tape-out costs approximately **$500,000 to $2 million**, while a COTS microcontroller costs **$1 to $50** per unit. - For low-to-medium volume production (**< 100,000 units**), COTS is overwhelmingly economical. 3. **Proven Reliability** - COTS components have been validated by thousands of customers and carry industrial certifications (e.g., **RoHS**, **AEC-Q100** for automotive). - Vendor datasheets provide MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) data. 4. **Rich Ecosystem Support** - Vendor-provided IDEs, debuggers, middleware (USB stacks, TCP/IP stacks, file systems). - Active community forums reduce debugging time. 5. **Scalability and Upgradability** - Pin-compatible and software-compatible families allow seamless migration to higher-performance variants. ### The Five-Step COTS Selection Methodology - **Step 1 — Requirements Definition:** Identify functional (logic, memory, I/O) and non-functional (power, temperature, EMI/EMC) requirements. - **Step 2 — Vendor Survey:** Shortlist vendors (Texas Instruments, STMicroelectronics, NXP, Microchip, etc.) based on datasheet availability and lifecycle status. - **Step 3 — Specification Matching:** Cross-validate electrical specs, package type, and interface compatibility against the system specification. - **Step 4 — Risk & Lifecycle Analysis:** Verify that the part is **NRND (Not Recommended for New Designs)**, **active**, or **mature** in the vendor's lifecycle roadmap. - **Step 5 — Prototyping & Validation:** Build a breadboard/evaluation board prototype and validate under real operating conditions. ### Limitations of COTS > [!WARNING] > **COTS is NOT a silver bullet.** Common pitfalls include: > - **Vendor Obsolescence:** Vendors can discontinue parts without notice, forcing costly redesigns. > - **Limited Differentiation:** Competing products use identical COTS, reducing your product's unique selling proposition (USP). > - **Security Vulnerabilities:** Pre-built firmware stacks may contain undocumented backdoors or known CVEs. > - **Over-specification:** COTS parts may include unused features, increasing BOM cost and PCB area. --- ## 2.2 ROM — Types, Internal Structure & Selection Criteria ### ROM Family Tree ROM is a broad umbrella term encompassing several technologies, each with distinct write/erase characteristics: ### 2.2.1 Mask ROM - **Programming mechanism:** Data is physically etched into the silicon during wafer fabrication using lithographic masks. - **Write capability:** **None** — permanently encoded at the factory. - **Cost profile:** Extremely low per-bit cost at high volumes (**> 100,000 units**); very high NRE (mask charges). - **Use case:** High-volume consumer electronics (e.g., character generators in legacy TV sets, game cartridges). - **Turnaround time:** **6 to 12 weeks** for mask fabrication. ### 2.2.2 PROM (Programmable ROM) - **Programming mechanism:** Each memory cell contains a fusible link (poly-silicon or nickel-chromium) that is electrically blown using a **PROM programmer** (high-current pulse of ~10–30 mA per cell). - **Write capability:** **One-time programmable (OTP)** — irreversible. - **Use case:** Low-volume prototyping, configuration storage in industrial controllers. ### 2.2.3 EPROM (Erasable PROM) - **Programming mechanism:** Data is stored as trapped charge on a **floating-gate MOSFET**. Programming uses the **hot-carrier injection** mechanism with ~12–25 V on the drain. - **Erase mechanism:** Exposed to **ultraviolet (UV) light** at **253.7 nm wavelength** for **20–30 minutes** through a quartz window on the IC package. - **Write/erase endurance:** Programming cycles ~**10 to 100 times**. - **Use case:** Early microcontroller firmware (e.g., legacy **Intel 8051** development). ### 2.2.4 EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM) - **Programming mechanism:** Uses **Fowler-Nordheim tunneling** to inject or remove electrons from the floating gate. - **Erase mechanism:** Electrical (byte-by-byte or page-by-page), no UV light needed. - **Write/erase endurance:** ~**100,000 to 1,000,000 cycles** per cell. - **Access time:** Byte write time of **~3 to 10 ms**. - **Use case:** Storing configuration parameters, calibration data, device serial numbers. ### 2.2.5 Flash Memory - **Programming mechanism:** Same floating-gate physics as EEPROM, but with a **sector/block** organization. - **Two main variants:** - **NOR Flash:** Random byte access, faster read (**~25–50 ns**), used for code storage (**XIP — Execute-In-Place**). - **NAND Flash:** Sequential page access, higher density, lower cost per bit, used for mass data storage (SD cards, SSDs). - **Write/erase endurance:** ~**10,000 to 100,000 cycles** (SLC), lower for MLC/TLC/QLC. - **Use case:** Universal memory in modern embedded systems — replaces both ROM and RAM in many designs. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Memory Capacity | $N = 2^n$ (for $n$ address lines) | Bytes / KB / MB / GB | Determines max addressable space | | Address Decoding | $\text{CS} = \overline{A_n \cdot A_{n-1} \cdots A_0}$ | Boolean logic | Chip-select generation | | Memory Access Time | $t_{ACC}$ | ns (nanoseconds) | Time from address valid to data valid | | Power Dissipation | $P = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC}$ | mW | Thermal design constraint | | Endurance | $E_{cycles}$ | cycles per cell | Number of write/erase operations | | Retention Time | $T_{ret}$ | years | Data persistence without power | | Cost per Bit | $C_{bit} = \dfrac{C_{IC}}{N_{bits}}$ | USD/bit | BOM optimization metric | | Density Scaling | $D_{Moore} = D_0 \cdot 2^{y/2}$ | transistors/mm² | Moore's Law projection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the unit alongside any numeric value in your answer. Writing "ROM capacity = 32" earns zero marks; writing "**32 KB**" earns full marks. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive ECUs:** COTS microcontrollers (e.g., NXP S32K, Infineon AURIX) running firmware from internal Flash ROM. - **IoT Edge Devices:** COTS Wi-Fi/BLE modules (ESP32, nRF52840) integrating external SPI Flash for OTA update storage. - **Aerospace & Defense:** Historically used custom rad-hard ASICs; modern shift toward **COTS-plus** (COTS components screened for radiation tolerance). - **Medical Devices:** COTS sensors + redundant ROM for storing calibration constants and FDA compliance logs. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ROM Address Decoding Logic A ROM IC with $n$ address lines can access $2^n$ unique memory locations. The following derivation shows how to design the chip-select (CS) logic for a memory-mapped ROM in an embedded system. ### Problem Setup - **Microprocessor:** 16-bit address bus ($A_{15} A_{14} \dots A_0$) - **ROM size:** $4\text{ KB} = 4 \times 1024 = 4096$ bytes $= 2^{12}$ locations - **ROM location:** Map ROM to the address range **$0000\text{H}$ to $0FFF\text{H}$** ### Step 1 — Determine Address Lines Used by ROM Since the ROM has $2^{12}$ locations, it requires the **lower 12 address lines** ($A_{11}$ to $A_0$) to address each byte internally. $$\text{ROM internal addressing} \implies n_{ROM} = 12$$ ### Step 2 — Identify the Chip-Select Lines The remaining address lines ($A_{15}$, $A_{14}$, $A_{13}$, $A_{12}$) must be decoded to generate the CS signal. $$\text{Chip-select lines} = A_{15}, A_{14}, A_{13}, A_{12}$$ ### Step 3 — Express the Target Address Range The ROM occupies **$0000\text{H}$ to $0FFF\text{H}$**: $$\begin{aligned} 0000\text{H} &= 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_2 \\ 0FFF\text{H} &= 0000\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111_2 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Derive the Boolean CS Expression For the address to fall within $0000\text{H}$–$0FFF\text{H}$, the upper four bits ($A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}$) must all be **0**. The CS logic must assert (go LOW, since most ROMs use active-low CS) when: $$CS = \overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}} \cdot \overline{A_{13}} \cdot \overline{A_{12}}$$ Or equivalently using DeMorgan's theorem: $$CS = \overline{A_{15} + A_{14} + A_{13} + A_{12}}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Memory Map Coverage The total address space covered by this single ROM mapping is: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Coverage} &= 2^{n_{CS}} \cdot 2^{n_{ROM}} \\ &= 2^4 \cdot 2^{12} \\ &= 2^{16} \\ &= 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB} \end{aligned}$$ Wait — that equals the full 16-bit address space. The reason: the CS logic is asserted only when the upper 4 bits are all zero, leaving the lower 12 bits to range over $2^{12}$ unique values, exactly mapping to the **4 KB ROM region** ($0000\text{H}$–$0FFF\text{H}$). ✓ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: COTS vs. Custom Design vs. ASIC | Criterion | COTS Component | Custom Board Design | Full ASIC | |---|---|---|---| | **NRE Cost** | **Very Low** ($0) | Medium ($5K–$50K) | Very High ($500K–$2M) | | **Per-Unit Cost (Volume)** | Medium ($1–$50) | Medium ($10–$100) | Very Low ($0.10–$5) | | **Time-to-Market** | **Weeks** | Months | 12–24 months | | **Differentiation** | Low | Medium | **Very High** | | **Volume Break-Even** | < 10K units | 10K–100K | > 100K units | | **IP Protection** | Low (vendor-owned) | Medium | **High** | | **Reliability Validation** | Vendor-certified | Self-validated | Custom qualification | | **Supply Chain Risk** | **Moderate** (obsolescence) | Low | Low | | **Flexibility** | Fixed | Moderate | **Full control** | ### Derivation — Volume Break-Even Point Let: - $C_{COTS}$ = total per-unit cost of COTS-based design (BOM + assembly + test) - $C_{ASIC}$ = per-unit cost of ASIC at production volume - $NRE_{ASIC}$ = non-recurring engineering cost of ASIC - $V$ = production volume The break-even volume $V^*$ occurs when total costs are equal: $$\begin{aligned} V \cdot C_{COTS} &= NRE_{ASIC} + V \cdot C_{ASIC} \\ V \cdot (C_{COTS} - C_{ASIC}) &= NRE_{ASIC} \\ V^* &= \dfrac{NRE_{ASIC}}{C_{COTS} - C_{ASIC}} \end{aligned}$$ **Example Numerical Evaluation:** Assume $NRE_{ASIC} = \$1{,}000{,}000$, $C_{COTS} = \$5$, $C_{ASIC} = \$2$: $$\begin{aligned} V^* &= \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{5 - 2} \\ &= \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{3} \\ &\approx 333{,}334 \text{ units} \end{aligned}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** For production volumes **below ~333K units**, COTS is more economical. Above that threshold, ASIC wins on per-unit cost despite the high NRE. --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Comparison: ROM Family Endurance & Latency | ROM Type | Program Voltage | Erase Mechanism | Endurance (cycles) | Byte Write Time | Density (bits/IC) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Mask ROM | N/A (factory) | N/A | 0 (no writes) | N/A | Up to 1 Gb | | PROM | +12 V | Fuse blow (irreversible) | **1** (OTP) | ~1 ms | 1–16 Mb | | EPROM | +12.5 V to +25 V | UV light, 20–30 min | 10–100 | ~1 ms | 1–8 Mb | | EEPROM | +5 V to +5.5 V | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | 100K–1M | 3–10 ms | 4 Kb–1 Mb | | NOR Flash | +3.3 V to +12 V | Sector/block electrical erase | 10K–100K | ~10 µs (page) | 16 Mb–2 Gb | | NAND Flash | +3.3 V to +15 V | Block electrical erase | 1K–100K | ~200 µs (page) | 1 Gb–1 Tb | > [!NOTE] > **Derivation of Memory Capacity from Address Lines:** > If a NOR Flash IC has **20 address lines**, the addressable memory space is: > $$N = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB}$$ > If the data bus is **16 bits wide**, the total storage is: > $$N_{total} = 2^{20} \times 16 \text{ bits} = 2^{24} \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ MB}$$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — COTS Integration in an Embedded System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Power Supply Unit
COTS LDO Regulator] --> B[Microcontroller
COTS MCU] C[Sensor Module
COTS Temp Sensor] --> B D[Communication Module
COTS WiFi/BLE] --> B B --> E[Memory ROM
Firmware Storage] B --> F[Memory RAM
Runtime Data] B --> G[Actuator / Output
PWM Motor Driver] H[User Interface
COTS LCD Display] --> B I[Host Development PC] -->|JTAG/SWD| B J[Vendor Documentation
Datasheets & App Notes] -.-> B K[Vendor IDE
COTS Toolchain] -.-> B style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style E fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style F fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style G fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf style H fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6 style I fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style J fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666 style K fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666 ``` ### Diagram Explanation - **Yellow Block (Microcontroller):** The COTS brain (e.g., STM32, ESP32) orchestrating all subsystems. - **Green Blocks (Sensor/Comm):** COTS input peripherals providing environmental data and wireless connectivity. - **Red Blocks (ROM/RAM):** COTS memory hierarchy storing firmware and runtime variables. - **Dashed Lines:** Represent non-physical flows — documentation, toolchains, and debug interfaces. --- ## 4.2 ROM Internal Architecture — Floating Gate Transistor Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FG [Floating Gate MOSFET Cell] FG1[Control Gate
CG] --> FG2[Floating Gate
FG - stores charge] FG2 --> FG3[Insulating Oxide Layer
SiO2 approximately 10 nm] FG3 --> FG4[Substrate Channel
P-type Si] FG5[Source S] --> FG4 FG4 --> FG6[Drain D] FG7[Tunnel Oxide
Thin region approx 5 nm] -.-> FG2 end FG --> ROM[ROM Cell Array
Nx M matrix] ROM --> ROW[Row Decoder
Word Line Driver] ROM --> COL[Column Decoder
Sense Amplifier] ROW --> OUT1[Selected Word Line WL] COL --> OUT2[Data Output DOUT] style FG1 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00 style FG2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style FG4 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf style FG7 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style ROM fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style ROW fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style COL fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 ``` ### Diagram Explanation - **Floating Gate (FG):** Stores charge representing a binary bit. Charged FG = logic **0** (high threshold voltage, transistor does not turn on); Discharged FG = logic **1**. - **Control Gate (CG):** Receives the word-line voltage during read operations. - **Tunnel Oxide:** Thin dielectric through which electrons tunnel during program/erase (Fowler-Nordheim tunneling). - **Row/Column Decoders:** Select the addressed cell; sense amplifier reads the stored charge. --- ## 4.3 ROM Family Hierarchy Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB ROM[ROM Non-Volatile Memory] --> MROM[Mask ROM
Factory Programmed] ROM --> PROM[PROM
One Time Programmable] ROM --> EPROM[EPROM
UV Erasable] ROM --> EEPROM[EEPROM
Electrically Erasable Byte Level] ROM --> FLASH[Flash Memory
Block Erase Electrically] FLASH --> NOR[NOR Flash
Random Access XIP] FLASH --> NAND[NAND Flash
Sequential Page Access] NOR --> NORAPP[Code Storage
Boot Firmware] NAND --> NANDAPP[Mass Data Storage
SSD SD Card eMMC] EEPROM --> EEPAPP[Configuration
Calibration Data] EPROM --> EPAPP[Legacy Firmware
Development Prototyping] PROM --> PROAPP[Small Volume
OTP Applications] MROM --> MRAPP[High Volume Consumer
Game Cartridges] style ROM fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:3px style FLASH fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style NOR fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style NAND fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 ``` --- ## 4.4 COTS Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[New Embedded Product Requirement] --> Q1{Need Custom
Silicon IP?} Q1 -->|Yes| ASIC[Full Custom ASIC
Volume greater than 100K] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Volume
Forecast?} Q2 -->|Less than 10K| COTS1[Pure COTS
Microcontroller Module] Q2 -->|10K to 100K| Q3{Differentiation
Required?} Q2 -->|Greater than 100K| Q4{Cost per Unit
Critical?} Q3 -->|Yes| SEMI[Semi-Custom
COTS plus FPGA] Q3 -->|No| COTS1 Q4 -->|Yes| ASIC2[Consider ASIC
or Structured ASIC] Q4 -->|No| COTS1 COTS1 --> VENDOR[Vendor Selection
TI ST NXP Microchip] SEMI --> VENDOR ASIC --> MASK[Mask ROM
Lithographic Encoding] ASIC2 --> MASK style START fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style COTS1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style SEMI fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf style ASIC fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style ASIC2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style MASK fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define COTS components. List any four advantages of using COTS components in embedded system design. **Model Answer:** **Definition:** > **Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) components** are pre-designed, pre-manufactured, and readily available hardware or software building blocks that can be purchased from commercial vendors and integrated into an embedded system without requiring custom design or fabrication. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Reduced Time-to-Market** — Pre-tested components enable rapid prototyping and product launch. 2. **Lower NRE Cost** — Eliminates expensive custom silicon design and tooling charges. 3. **Proven Reliability** — Vendor-validated parts with industrial certifications (RoHS, AEC-Q100). 4. **Rich Ecosystem Support** — Vendor-provided IDEs, debuggers, middleware, and active community forums. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Mask ROM and PROM. Mention their programming mechanisms. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Mask ROM | PROM | |---|---|---| | **Programming Mechanism** | Data encoded during wafer fabrication using lithographic masks | Data written using a PROM programmer that blows internal fusible links via high-current pulses (~10–30 mA) | | **Cost Profile** | Low per-bit cost at very high volumes; very high mask NRE | Higher per-bit cost; zero NRE for programming | | **Write Capability** | None (factory-only) | One-Time Programmable (OTP) — irreversible | | **Use Case** | High-volume consumer electronics, game cartridges | Low-volume prototyping, configuration storage | --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** Part (a) **(7 Marks)** — Explain the different types of ROM in detail with suitable diagrams. Compare their programming and erasing mechanisms. **Model Answer:** **Introduction:** Read-Only Memory (ROM) is a non-volatile semiconductor memory used to permanently store firmware and constant data in embedded systems. It retains data even without power. **1. Mask ROM:** - **Programming:** Data is encoded directly into the silicon die during semiconductor fabrication using custom photolithographic masks. Each memory cell contains either a transistor or no transistor at predefined locations. - **Erasing:** Not possible — data is permanently etched at the factory. - **Cost:** Extremely low per-bit cost for very high volumes (>100K units); very high NRE due to mask charges. - **Use Case:** Game cartridges, character ROMs in legacy displays. **2. PROM (Programmable ROM):** - **Programming:** Each cell contains a poly-silicon or nickel-chromium fusible link. A PROM programmer applies a high-current pulse (~10–30 mA) that "blows" the fuse at addresses where logic 0 is desired. - **Erasing:** Not possible — one-time programmable (OTP). - **Use Case:** Low-volume production, prototype code storage. **3. EPROM (Erasable PROM):** - **Programming:** Floating-gate MOSFET cells are programmed using the **hot-carrier injection** mechanism. A high voltage (~12–25 V) is applied to the drain to force electrons through the oxide onto the floating gate. - **Erasing:** The IC package contains a **quartz window** through which UV light at **253.7 nm** is exposed for **20–30 minutes**, discharging the floating gate. - **Endurance:** ~10–100 program/erase cycles. - **Drawback:** Entire chip must be erased; no byte-level selectivity. **4. EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM):** - **Programming & Erasing:** Uses **Fowler-Nordheim quantum tunneling**. Electrons tunnel through a thin oxide layer (~5 nm) under an electric field. Byte-level electrical erase is possible. - **Voltage:** Operates on standard +5 V or +3.3 V supplies. - **Endurance:** ~100,000 to 1,000,000 cycles. - **Drawback:** Slower byte-write time (~3–10 ms) and lower density than Flash. **5. Flash Memory:** - **Programming & Erasing:** Same floating-gate physics as EEPROM, but organized in **sector/block** units instead of byte-level. - **Two Architectures:** - **NOR Flash:** Random byte access, fast read, supports **XIP (Execute-In-Place)** — ideal for firmware storage. - **NAND Flash:** Sequential page access, higher density, lower cost per bit — ideal for mass storage. - **Endurance:** ~10,000–100,000 cycles (SLC). **Comparison Table:** | ROM Type | Program Voltage | Erase Mechanism | Endurance | |---|---|---|---| | Mask ROM | Factory only | N/A | None | | PROM | +12 V (fuse blow) | Irreversible | 1 cycle | | EPROM | +12.5 V to +25 V | UV light, 20–30 min | 10–100 | | EEPROM | +5 V to +5.5 V | Electrical (byte) | 100K–1M | | Flash | +3.3 V to +12 V | Electrical (block) | 10K–100K | --- **Part (b) (7 Marks)** — With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a floating-gate MOSFET ROM cell. How is data stored and read? **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram Description:** ``` Control Gate (CG) | | ────[ CG ]──── | | | Floating | Insulating SiO2 (~10 nm) | Gate (FG) | Thin Tunnel Oxide (~5 nm) | | ───────────── | ────[Channel]──── S D (Source) (Drain) ``` **Structure:** A floating-gate MOSFET consists of four terminals: 1. **Control Gate (CG)** — receives the word-line voltage during read/program. 2. **Floating Gate (FG)** — electrically isolated by oxide layers; stores charge representing the bit value. 3. **Source (S)** and **Drain (D)** — current flow terminals in the silicon substrate. 4. **Tunnel Oxide** — thin dielectric region enabling electron tunneling. **Data Storage Mechanism:** - A **charged floating gate** screens the control gate's electric field, raising the transistor's threshold voltage. The cell transistor cannot turn on during a read → interpreted as logic **0**. - A **discharged floating gate** leaves the threshold voltage low; the transistor turns on during a read → interpreted as logic **1**. **Programming (Write):** A high voltage (~10–12 V) applied between control gate and drain causes electrons to tunnel through the thin oxide onto the floating gate (Fowler-Nordheim tunneling or hot-carrier injection). **Erasing:** For EEPROM/Flash, a reverse-polarity high voltage pulls electrons off the floating gate back into the substrate. **Reading:** The word-line activates the control gate with a normal Vcc (~3.3 V). The sense amplifier detects whether current flows through the cell, thereby distinguishing logic 0 from logic 1. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** Part (a) **(7 Marks)** — Discuss the role of COTS components in modern embedded system design. Explain the advantages and limitations with real-world examples. **Model Answer:** **Introduction:** COTS components have become the backbone of modern embedded system design. By integrating pre-built, vendor-validated modules, engineers can focus on application-level differentiation rather than reinventing foundational building blocks. **Advantages (with Examples):** 1. **Rapid Prototyping** — A BLE-enabled IoT node can be assembled in a single afternoon using the **Nordic nRF52840** COTS SoC, with integrated radio, MCU, and Flash. 2. **Cost Efficiency** — Using the **STM32F103** COTS microcontroller (~$1 per unit) instead of a custom ASIC (~$500K NRE + per-unit cost). 3. **Proven Reliability** — Automotive ECUs built on **NXP S32K** COTS MCUs that are AEC-Q100 qualified for -40°C to +125°C operation. 4. **Rich Ecosystem** — Vendor-supplied drivers, RTOS ports (FreeRTOS, Zephyr), and middleware accelerate firmware development. **Limitations (with Examples):** 1. **Vendor Obsolescence** — Microchip and TI regularly issue **NRND (Not Recommended for New Designs)** notices; redesigns may be needed every 5–10 years. 2. **Security Vulnerabilities** — Pre-built firmware stacks have known CVEs (e.g., the **ESP-IDF Bluetooth stack vulnerabilities** disclosed in 2023). 3. **Limited Differentiation** — Two competing smartwatches using identical COTS sensors (e.g., **MAX30102** for heart rate) have similar baseline performance. 4. **Over-specification** — A COTS MCU with built-in USB, CAN, and Ethernet may be used in a project needing only UART, wasting cost and PCB area. **Engineering Conclusion:** The optimal embedded design philosophy is **"COTS-first, custom-only-where-necessary"** — leveraging COTS for non-differentiating functions (power, comms, basic compute) while investing custom engineering into application-specific IP. --- **Part (b) (7 Marks)** — Explain the concept of Memory-Mapped ROM interfacing. Derive the chip-select logic for a 4 KB ROM mapped at address $4000\text{H}$ to $4FFF\text{H}$ in a system with 16-bit address bus. **Model Answer:** **Concept of Memory-Mapped I/O:** In memory-mapped I/O, ROM/RAM occupy addresses within the processor's unified address space. The CPU accesses ROM using the same instructions (MOV, LDR) as ordinary memory. **Derivation:** **Step 1 — ROM Size in Lines:** $$\text{ROM size} = 4\text{ KB} = 2^{12} \text{ bytes} \implies n_{ROM} = 12 \text{ address lines}$$ The 12 lower address lines $A_{11}$ to $A_0$ address the internal ROM cells. **Step 2 — Target Address Range:** $$\begin{aligned} 4000\text{H} &= 0100\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_2 \\ 4FFF\text{H} &= 0100\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Identify the Chip-Select Bits:** The upper four bits ($A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}$) must equal **0100** for the address to fall in the target range: - $A_{15} = 0$ - $A_{14} = 1$ - $A_{13} = 0$ - $A_{12} = 0$ **Step 4 — Boolean CS Expression:** $$\begin{aligned} CS &= \overline{A_{15}} \cdot A_{14} \cdot \overline{A_{13}} \cdot \overline{A_{12}} \\ &= \overline{(A_{15} + \overline{A_{14}} + A_{13} + A_{12})} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Verify Coverage:** $$\text{Coverage} = 2^4 \times 2^{12} = 2^{16} = 64\text{ KB (entire address space)}$$ The CS assertion ensures only addresses with $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12} = 0100$ activate the ROM chip, mapping it exactly to $4000\text{H}$–$4FFF\text{H}$ (4 KB region). ✓ **Implementation:** This logic can be realized using a **3-to-8 line decoder** (e.g., **74HC138**) with appropriate input combinations or a simple **AND-OR gate network**. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing "ROM capacity = 32" instead of "**32 KB**" — Examiner deducts **0.5–1 mark** per occurrence. > 2. **Confusing NOR and NAND Flash:** NOR Flash supports random access and XIP; NAND Flash does NOT — this is a **frequently asked** 3-mark question. > 3. **Skipping Block Diagrams:** A "neat block diagram" is **mandatory** for any 7-mark ROM question; omitting it costs **2–3 marks**. > 4. **Writing Only Names:** For "explain types of ROM" questions, merely listing names without explaining programming/erasing mechanisms yields only **2 out of 7 marks**. > 5. **COTS Without Examples:** Generic answers lose marks; always cite at least **one vendor part number** (e.g., STM32F103, ESP32, LM35). > 6. **Mask ROM vs. PROM Confusion:** Mask ROM is factory-programmed during fabrication; PROM is user-programmed post-fabrication. Examiners **strictly penalize** this confusion. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — COTS & ROM:** > > - [x] **COTS Definition:** Pre-built, vendor-supplied components integrated as-is into embedded systems. > - [x] **Key COTS Advantages:** Lower NRE, faster time-to-market, proven reliability, rich ecosystem. > - [x] **Key COTS Limitations:** Vendor obsolescence, limited differentiation, security risks, over-specification cost. > - [x] **COTS Selection Methodology:** 5 steps — Requirements → Vendor Survey → Spec Match → Risk Analysis → Prototyping. > - [x] **Break-Even Volume Formula:** $V^* = \dfrac{NRE_{ASIC}}{C_{COTS} - C_{ASIC}}$ > - [x] **ROM Definition:** Non-volatile memory retaining data without power; primarily for firmware storage. > - [x] **Mask ROM:** Factory-programmed via lithography; no writes; high volume, high NRE. > - [x] **PROM:** One-time programmable via fuse blow; ~1 ms programming time. > - [x] **EPROM:** UV-erasable (253.7 nm, 20–30 min); 10–100 cycle endurance; requires quartz window. > - [x] **EEPROM:** Byte-level electrically erasable; Fowler-Nordheim tunneling; 100K–1M cycle endurance. > - [x] **Flash Memory:** Block-level electrical erase; NOR = XIP code storage; NAND = mass storage. > - [x] **Floating Gate:** Stores charge representing bit value; tunneling via thin oxide. > - [x] **Memory Mapping:** $N = 2^n$ locations for $n$ address lines; CS logic uses upper bits. > - [x] **Standard Unit Conventions:** Always write **KB, MB, GB, ns, µs, mA, mW** explicitly in KTU answers. > - [x] **Real-World Vendors:** TI, STMicroelectronics, NXP, Microchip, Infineon, Nordic, Espressif, Renesas. ---

topics.title: Memory According to the Type of Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET723 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory According to the Type of Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Memory According to the Type of Interface — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In an embedded system, **memory** is a fundamental subsystem that stores both program instructions and data required for the operation of the microcontroller/processor. When classified **according to the type of interface** with the processor, memory (and memory-mapped peripherals) can be categorized into two principal interface paradigms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory-Mapped Interface (MMIO):** A unified addressing scheme in which the processor treats peripheral registers as if they were ordinary memory locations. The same address bus, data bus, and read/write control signals used for memory are also used to access I/O devices. Every peripheral register occupies a unique address in the processor's logical address space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isolated (Port-Mapped) I/O Interface (PMIO):** A separate addressing scheme in which the processor maintains two independent address spaces — one for memory and a smaller, dedicated one for I/O ports. Special control lines (such as **IO/M** or **M/IO**) and dedicated instructions (**IN**, **OUT**) are used to access peripherals.

Additionally, memory devices themselves are classified by their **physical data transfer mechanism** into:

- **Parallel Interface Memory** — transfers multiple bits simultaneously across a wide data bus.
- **Serial Interface Memory** — transfers one bit at a time over a few signal lines, using protocols like **I²C**, **SPI**, or **UART**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** For ARM-based microcontrollers (the de-facto standard in this syllabus), only **Memory-Mapped I/O** is supported. PMIO is primarily a feature of legacy 8-bit/16-bit processors such as the **Intel 8085** and **Intel 8086**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

### Analogy 1: The "One Big Filing Cabinet" (Memory-Mapped I/O)

Imagine a large office building where **every drawer in a giant filing cabinet** has a unique number, ranging from `0001` to `9999`. Whether a drawer contains a company document (memory) or a coffee machine's operational log (I/O device), you access it the same way: walk up, dial the drawer number, and open it. There is **one single numbering system** for everything.

In Memory-Mapped I/O, the CPU has **one address space**, and peripheral registers live at specific addresses inside that space. Writing to address `0x4000_0008` might actually toggle an LED — but the CPU doesn't know or care; it just executes a `STR` instruction.

### Analogy 2: The "Two Separate Storage Rooms" (Port-Mapped I/O)

Now imagine the same building but with **two different storage areas**:
- **Room A (Memory Room):** Has drawers numbered `0001` to `9000`.
- **Room B (I/O Room):** Has its own independent drawers numbered `P01` to `P99`.

To access Room A, you use the standard procedure. To access Room B, you must first **declare** "I am going to Room B" (using a special signal like IO/M = 1) and then use a different procedure with **special keys** (IN/OUT instructions). The numbering systems never overlap.

### Analogy 3: The "Pipe" Metaphor for Physical Interface

- **Parallel Memory** is like a **wide garden hose** (8, 16, 32 water streams side by side) — fast but bulky and needs many connections.
- **Serial Memory** is like a **thin straw** — only one stream of water at a time, but the straw is cheap, lightweight, and easy to route.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Bus Width (n)** | **8, 16, 32, 64 bits** | Number of data lines; determines word size |
| **Address Lines (m)** | **16, 20, 24, 32** | Determines addressable memory locations |
| **Addressable Locations** | **2^m** | Maximum number of unique addresses |
| **Total Memory Capacity** | **2^m × n bits** | Maximum memory that can be addressed |
| **Access Time (t_AA)** | **ns (nanoseconds)** | Time from address valid to data valid |
| **Memory Cycle Time (t_RC)** | **ns** | Minimum time between successive accesses |
| **Bandwidth** | **MB/s or GB/s** | Data transfer rate of the interface |
| **Standard Serial Clock Speeds (SPI)** | **1 MHz – 100+ MHz** | SCK frequency for serial memories |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always mention **2^n** explicitly when describing addressable space. Most marks in derivations are awarded for correctly setting up the address-space equation.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Embedded Systems

The choice of memory interface impacts:
1. **Performance** — Parallel is faster; serial uses fewer pins.
2. **PCB Cost & Complexity** — Serial reduces routing complexity.
3. **Software Simplicity** — MMIO allows uniform C-language pointer access; PMIO requires special instructions.
4. **Scalability** — MMIO scales naturally to large address spaces; PMIO is limited to the 8/16-bit port range.
5. **Power Consumption** — Serial memories often have lower active current.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Interface Paradigms — Detailed Operational Breakdown

### A. Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO)

**How it Works (Step-by-Step):**

1. The processor places an address on the **Address Bus**.
2. The address is decoded by an external **Address Decoder** (or internal decoder on SoCs).
3. The decoder asserts the appropriate **Chip Select (CS_n)** signal for the targeted memory or peripheral register.
4. For a **read**: the processor asserts the **Output Enable (OE_n / RD_n)** line; the device drives the data bus.
5. For a **write**: the processor asserts the **Write Enable (WE_n / WR_n)** line; data on the bus is latched by the device.
6. **No special control line is needed** to distinguish between memory and I/O — both use the same RD/WR signals.

**Software View in C (ARM Cortex-M example):**
```c
#define GPIOA_ODR  (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014U)  // GPIOA Output Data Register
GPIOA_ODR |= (1 << 5);   // Set PA5 high — looks just like a memory write!
```

**Strengths:**
- Full address space available to peripherals.
- Any data-manipulation instruction (MOV, LDR, STR, ADD, etc.) can be used on I/O.
- Compiler-friendly (no special intrinsics required).
- Naturally scales to **32-bit and 64-bit** processors.

**Weaknesses:**
- Memory address space is "consumed" by peripheral registers.
- A single stray write to a peripheral address can corrupt hardware state.
- No explicit hardware distinction between RAM and I/O.

---

### B. Isolated I/O / Port-Mapped I/O (PMIO)

**How it Works (Step-by-Step):**

1. The processor places an **8-bit or 16-bit port address** on the address bus.
2. The processor asserts a **special control signal** (e.g., **IO/M = HIGH** on 8086) to indicate an I/O access (not a memory access).
3. The memory's chip-select logic **ignores** this address (it does not respond).
4. The peripheral's chip-select logic **responds** and the data is transferred.
5. **Special instructions** are used: `IN accumulator, port` and `OUT port, accumulator` on x86; `IN` and `OUT` on 8085.

**Software View in Assembly (8086 example):**
```asm
MOV DX, 0x3F8H      ; Load the COM1 port address
IN  AL, DX          ; Read the UART receive register
OUT DX, AL          ; Write to the UART transmit register
```

**Strengths:**
- The entire memory address space remains available for memory (no wastage).
- Clear hardware distinction between I/O and memory cycles (useful for bus analyzers).
- I/O address decoding is simpler (only 8 or 16 bits).

**Weaknesses:**
- Limited I/O address space (only 256 ports on 8085; 65536 on 8086).
- Cannot use memory arithmetic/logic instructions directly on I/O data.
- More complex compiler support; usually requires inline assembly.
- Slower (extra machine cycles to assert IO/M and use port-specific instructions).

---

## 2.2 Parallel vs. Serial Memory Interface — Operational Analysis

### Parallel Memory Interface

- **Data Bus Width:** 4, 8, 16, or 32 bits transferred simultaneously.
- **Pin Count:** Very high (e.g., a 32-bit wide × 24-bit address ROM requires ~60 pins).
- **Throughput:** High — full word per memory cycle.
- **Examples:** 6264 SRAM (8-bit × 8K), 27C256 EPROM, 28C256 EEPROM, parallel NOR Flash, SDRAM.

**Typical Signal Set for a Parallel Memory:**

| Signal | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **A0 … A(m-1)** | CPU → Memory | Address lines |
| **D0 … D(n-1)** | Bidirectional | Data lines |
| **CS_n (Chip Select)** | CPU → Memory | Enables the chip |
| **OE_n (Output Enable)** | CPU → Memory | Enables data output (read) |
| **WE_n (Write Enable)** | CPU → Memory | Latches data input (write) |
| **VCC, GND** | Power | Supply rails |

### Serial Memory Interface

- **Data Width:** 1 bit per clock cycle.
- **Pin Count:** Low (typically 4–6 pins: SCK, MOSI/SDA, MISO, CS_n).
- **Throughput:** Lower per-pin but with very high SCK frequencies, effective throughput can rival parallel buses.
- **Common Protocols:** **SPI** (Serial Peripheral Interface), **I²C** (Inter-Integrated Circuit), **Microwire**, **UART-based**, **One-Wire**.

**SPI Serial Memory Pinout:**

| Signal | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **SCK** | Master → Memory | Serial clock |
| **MOSI** | Master → Memory | Master Out, Slave In (write data) |
| **MISO** | Memory → Master | Master In, Slave Out (read data) |
| **CS_n / SS_n** | Master → Memory | Slave select (chip enable) |
| **WP_n, HOLD_n** | Master → Memory | Optional write-protect and bus-pause |

**I²C Serial Memory Pinout:**

| Signal | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **SCL** | Master → Memory | Serial clock |
| **SDA** | Bidirectional | Serial data (open-drain, needs pull-up) |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of addressable locations | **N = 2^m** | locations | where m = number of address lines |
| 2 | Total memory capacity (bits) | **C_bits = 2^m × n** | bits | n = data bus width |
| 3 | Total memory capacity (bytes) | **C_bytes = (2^m × n) / 8** | bytes | when n is in bits |
| 4 | Address range (hex) | **0x00000 to 0x(2^m − 1)** | hex | inclusive lower, exclusive upper |
| 5 | Number of chips needed | **K = (Required memory) / (Memory per chip)** | dimensionless | round UP to nearest integer |
| 6 | Address-line fan-out per chip | **m_chip = log2(memory per chip)** | bits | log base 2 |
| 7 | Memory access time (read) | **t_access = t_AA + t_OE** | ns | address-access + output-enable |
| 8 | Memory bandwidth (parallel) | **BW = f × n / 8** | MB/s | f = bus clock, n = bus width |
| 9 | Memory bandwidth (serial SPI) | **BW_SPI = f_SCK / 8** (approx) | MB/s | per data line, ignoring overhead |
| 10 | Address decoding: Linear | **CS_n = NOT(Ax)** | logic | one address line per chip |
| 11 | Address decoding: Full | **CS_n = NOT(decoder output)** | logic | using 74LS138 / 74LS139 |
| 12 | Memory cycle time | **t_cycle ≥ t_AA + t_DH** | ns | t_DH = data hold time |
| 13 | Wait states required | **WS = ceil(t_access / t_CPU) − 1** | integer | if memory slower than CPU |
| 14 | Energy per access | **E = V × I_active × t_access** | Joules | for low-power calculations |
| 15 | I²C pull-up resistor | **R_pullup = (VCC − V_OL) / I_OL** | Ω | typically 1 kΩ – 10 kΩ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cheat Sheet Warning:** Memorize the two equations **N = 2^m** and **C = 2^m × n** in both bits and bytes. These appear in over 70% of memory-related 14-mark questions on this topic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Memory-Interface Knowledge |
|---|---|
| **Automotive ECUs** | MMIO for register access; serial EEPROM for storing fault codes (DTCs) |
| **IoT Devices** | External SPI Flash for firmware storage (over-the-air updates) |
| **Industrial PLCs** | Parallel SRAM for fast scratchpad; I²C EEPROM for parameter retention |
| **Medical Devices** | Battery-backed SRAM (BB-SRAM) for patient data logs |
| **Consumer Electronics** | eMMC (parallel-style 8-bit interface) in smartphones |
| **Aerospace** | MRAM (serial/parallel) for radiation-hardened non-volatile storage |
| **FPGA SoCs** | MMIO bridges AXI/APB to GPIO and UART registers |

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Address Space and Memory Capacity

**Given:** A 16-bit processor with a 20-bit address bus and an 8-bit data bus (similar to the Intel 8086 in maximum mode).

**Step 1 — Identify the address lines (m) and data lines (n).**

From the problem statement, the processor has m = **20** address lines and n = **8** data lines.

**Step 2 — Compute the number of addressable byte locations.**

$$N = 2^m$$

$$N = 2^{20}$$

$$N = 1\,048\,576 \text{ locations}$$

This is exactly **1 Megabyte (1 MiB)** of addressable memory space.

**Step 3 — Compute the total memory capacity in bits.**

$$C_{\text{bits}} = 2^m \times n$$

$$C_{\text{bits}} = 2^{20} \times 8$$

$$C_{\text{bits}} = 8\,388\,608 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes.**

$$C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{2^m \times n}{8} = \frac{2^{20} \times 8}{8} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 5 — Express the address range.**

$$\text{Address range: } 0x00000 \text{ to } 0xFFFFE \text{ (in word terms)}$$
$$\text{Address range: } 0x00000 \text{ to } 0xFFFFF \text{ (in byte terms — 20-bit max)}$$

> **Conclusion:** The 8086 in maximum mode can address **1 MB = 2^20 bytes = 8 Mbits** of memory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Memory Chips Required

**Given:** System requires **64 KB of ROM** and **32 KB of RAM**. Available chips: ROM = **8 KB × 8-bit**, RAM = **16 KB × 8-bit**. Address bus = 16 bits.

**Step 1 — Calculate the number of ROM chips needed.**

$$\text{ROM chips} = \frac{\text{Required ROM}}{\text{ROM per chip}} = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{8 \text{ KB}} = 8 \text{ chips}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the number of RAM chips needed.**

$$\text{RAM chips} = \frac{\text{Required RAM}}{\text{RAM per chip}} = \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ KB}} = 2 \text{ chips}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the address lines per chip (for ROM).**

$$m_{\text{ROM chip}} = \log_2(8 \text{ KB} \times 8) = \log_2(65\,536) = 16 \text{ bits}$$

Wait — let me be precise. 8 KB = 8 × 1024 = 8192 bytes. So:

$$m_{\text{ROM chip}} = \log_2(8192) = 13 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the address lines per chip (for RAM).**

$$\text{16 KB} = 16 \times 1024 = 16\,384 \text{ bytes}$$

$$m_{\text{RAM chip}} = \log_2(16\,384) = 14 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 5 — Determine the higher-order address lines for chip-select decoding.**

For 8 ROM chips, the 8 ROM chips are organized as **8 banks of 8 KB each** = 64 KB. To select 1 of 8 chips, we need:

$$d = \log_2(8) = 3 \text{ decoder inputs}$$

The total address bus is 16 lines, and 13 are used by the ROM chip itself, leaving 16 − 13 = **3** high-order lines for the ROM decoder. ✓ Consistent.

For 2 RAM chips (16 KB each), the 2 RAM chips form **2 banks of 16 KB each** = 32 KB. To select 1 of 2, we need 1 line. The RAM chip uses 14 address lines; 16 − 14 = **2** high-order lines remain, which is more than enough (one can be tied directly as CS, the other unused or used for ROM banking).

> **Conclusion:** The system needs **8 ROM chips** and **2 RAM chips**, with a **3-to-8 decoder** (e.g., 74LS138) for ROM chip-select and a single inverter/AND-gate combination for RAM chip-select.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Memory Access Time and Wait States

**Given:** CPU clock period **t_CPU = 100 ns** (i.e., 10 MHz). Memory access time **t_AA = 250 ns**.

**Step 1 — Determine how many CPU clock cycles elapse in one memory access.**

$$\text{Cycles} = \frac{t_{AA}}{t_{CPU}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Round up to the next integer (memory must complete a full cycle).**

$$\text{Integer cycles} = \lceil 2.5 \rceil = 3 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the number of wait states required.**

$$WS = \text{Integer cycles} - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ wait states}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective memory cycle time with wait states.**

$$t_{\text{cycle,eff}} = (1 + WS) \times t_{CPU} = (1 + 2) \times 100 = 300 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the throughput penalty.**

$$\text{No-wait throughput} = \frac{n}{t_{CPU}} = \frac{8 \text{ bits}}{100 \text{ ns}} = 80 \text{ Mbps}$$

$$\text{With 2 wait states} = \frac{8 \text{ bits}}{300 \text{ ns}} \approx 26.67 \text{ Mbps}$$

$$\text{Penalty} = 1 - \frac{26.67}{80} = 0.667 = 66.7\% \text{ slower}$$

> **Conclusion:** Slow memory forces **2 wait states**, reducing bus throughput by approximately **two-thirds**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — SPI Serial Memory Effective Bandwidth

**Given:** SPI mode 0, SCK = 50 MHz, 8-bit words. Overhead: 1 byte of command + 3 bytes of address per read page = 4 bytes overhead per 256-byte page.

**Step 1 — Compute raw bit rate.**

$$f_{\text{SCK}} = 50 \text{ MHz} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw byte rate.**

$$R_{\text{raw}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{8} = 6.25 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective throughput including overhead.**

For every 256 bytes of payload, 4 bytes of overhead are sent, so:

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6 \times 256}{8 \times (256 + 4)} = \frac{12.8 \times 10^9}{2080} \approx 6.154 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the overhead percentage.**

$$\text{Overhead} = \frac{4}{256 + 4} \times 100 \approx 1.54\%$$

> **Conclusion:** For large page reads, the SPI interface achieves ~6.15 MB/s, only ~1.54% lower than the raw rate.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — MMIO vs PMIO vs Parallel vs Serial

| Parameter | Memory-Mapped I/O | Port-Mapped I/O | Parallel Memory | Serial Memory |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | Shared with memory | Separate (8/16-bit) | Same address bus | Same address bus (mapped) |
| **Special Instructions?** | **No** | **Yes** (IN/OUT) | No (normal LD/ST) | No (normal LD/ST after init) |
| **Pin Count** | High (full address + data) | High (separate strobe) | Very high (n + m) | Very low (2–6 pins) |
| **Speed** | Medium-Fast | Fast (single-cycle IN/OUT) | Very fast | Slow–Medium |
| **Software Complexity** | Low (C pointers) | High (assembly needed) | Low | Medium (driver code) |
| **Power** | High during access | High during access | High | Low |
| **Scalability** | Excellent (32/64-bit) | Poor (8/16-bit only) | Poor (pin-limited) | Excellent |
| **Example Processor** | ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V | Intel 8085, 8086 | 8051 ext. bus | AVR (internal), STM32 SPI |
| **Common Use** | Modern MCUs/SoCs | Legacy 8/16-bit | External SRAM/ROM | EEPROM, Flash, FRAM |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Address Decoder Design Using 74LS138

**Given:** 16-bit address bus (A15…A0). We need to decode:
- ROM at `0x0000 – 0x1FFF` (8 KB)
- RAM at `0x2000 – 0x3FFF` (8 KB)

**Step 1 — Write the binary address ranges.**

ROM: `0 0000 0000 0000 0000` to `0 0001 1111 1111 1111`
RAM: `0 0010 0000 0000 0000` to `0 0011 1111 1111 1111`

**Step 2 — Identify the high-order decoding bits.**

A15, A14, A13, A12, A11 vary across these ranges. The lower 11 bits (A10…A0) are the "don't care" within each 8 KB block.

**Step 3 — Map to 74LS138 inputs (3-to-8 decoder).**

The 74LS138 has inputs C, B, A (active-high, inverted internally). For the ROM at `0x0000–0x1FFF`, A15 = 0, A14 = 0, A13 = 0 or 1. The first two 8 KB blocks correspond to Y0 and Y1. We can combine them using:

$$\text{CS\_ROM} = \overline{Y_0 \cdot Y_1} \quad \text{(using AND + invert)}$$

A cleaner method is to wire:
- A15 → G2A (active low enable)
- A14 → G2B (active low enable)
- A13 → C
- A12 → B
- A11 → A
- Y0 → CS_ROM
- Y1 → CS_RAM (with appropriate gating)

> **Conclusion:** The 74LS138 with A15, A14 tied low enables the decoder; A13, A12, A11 select one of 8 outputs; Y0 selects ROM, Y1 selects RAM, etc.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — CPU ↔ Memory Interface (MMIO & PMIO)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuA[CPU Core]
    addrBus[(Address Bus\nA0..Am-1)]
    dataBus[(Data Bus\nD0..Dn-1)]
    ctrlMM[Control Signals\nRD_n, WR_n]
    ctrlPM[Special Control\nIO_M_n]
    decoder[Address Decoder\n74LS138 / Internal]
    memA[Memory Chip A\nROM]
    memB[Memory Chip B\nRAM]
    periA[Peripheral A\nUART Registers]
    periB[Peripheral B\nGPIO Registers]

    cpuA -->|drives| addrBus
    cpuA -->|drives| dataBus
    cpuA -->|asserts| ctrlMM
    cpuA -->|MMIO: tied LOW\nPMIO: toggles| ctrlPM
    addrBus --> decoder
    ctrlMM --> decoder
    ctrlPM --> decoder
    decoder -->|CS_n| memA
    decoder -->|CS_n| memB
    decoder -->|CS_n| periA
    decoder -->|CS_n| periB
    memA <-->|bidirectional| dataBus
    memB <-->|bidirectional| dataBus
    periA <-->|bidirectional| dataBus
    periB <-->|bidirectional| dataBus
```

**Interpretation:**
- The same address bus, data bus, and RD/WR lines serve **all devices**.
- The decoder activates exactly one CS_n per cycle.
- In **MMIO mode**, `IO_M_n` is always LOW (or unused).
- In **PMIO mode**, `IO_M_n` is asserted only for port access, and the memory's CS logic blocks memory from responding.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Read Cycle Sequence (Parallel Memory)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([CPU initiates READ]) --> a1[Place address on Address Bus]
    a1 --> a2[Decoder asserts CS_n for target device]
    a2 --> a3[CPU asserts OE_n / RD_n]
    a3 --> a4{Memory access time\nelapsed?}
    a4 -->|No| a5[CPU inserts WAIT state]
    a5 --> a4
    a4 -->|Yes| a6[Target device drives Data Bus]
    a6 --> a7[CPU latches data]
    a7 --> a8[CPU deasserts RD_n and CS_n]
    a8 --> endR([Read complete])
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Serial Memory (SPI) Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    master[Master Controller\nMCU]
    sck[SCK Line]
    mosi[MOSI Line]
    miso[MISO Line]
    csn[CS_n Line]
    sMem[Serial SPI Memory\nFlash or FRAM]

    master -->|clock| sck
    master -->|command and address| mosi
    sMem -->|data out| miso
    master -->|select| csn
    sck --> sMem
    mosi --> sMem
    miso --> master
    csn --> sMem
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Interface Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is pin count\ncritically limited?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is data throughput\nrequired > 1 MB/s?}
    q1 -->|No| q3[Use Parallel Memory Interface\nwith MMIO]
    q2 -->|Yes| q4[Use Quad SPI or\nOctal SPI Flash]
    q2 -->|No| q5[Use Standard SPI\nor I2C EEPROM]
    q3 --> q6{Is processor\n8/16-bit legacy?}
    q6 -->|Yes| q7{Need separate\nI/O address space?}
    q6 -->|No| q8[Use 32-bit MMIO\nARM Cortex-M or RISC-V]
    q7 -->|Yes| q9[Use PMIO with\nIN and OUT instructions]
    q7 -->|No| q10[Use MMIO only\nsoftware-driven]
```

---

## 4.5 Address Space Partitioning Diagram (MMIO Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph addrSpace[32-bit Address Space 0x00000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF]
        dir1[0x00000000 - 0x1FFFFFFF\nFLASH Memory 512 MB]
        dir2[0x20000000 - 0x3FFFFFFF\nSRAM 512 MB]
        dir3[0x40000000 - 0x5FFFFFFF\nPeripherals 512 MB]
        dir4[0x60000000 - 0x7FFFFFFF\nExternal SRAM 512 MB]
        dir5[0x80000000 - 0x9FFFFFFF\nExternal PSRAM 512 MB]
        dir6[0xE0000000 - 0xFFFFFFFF\nSystem Control 512 MB]
    end
```

> **KTU Interpretation:** In ARM Cortex-M4 (e.g., STM32F407), this exact memory map is used. Code at `0x0000_0000` (FLASH) writes to a peripheral at `0x4002_0014` (GPIOA_ODR) using **the same LDR/STR instructions** — that is MMIO in action.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Memory-Mapped I/O and Isolated I/O with respect to address space, instructions used, and a real processor example for each.**

**Model Answer (Expected ~3-mark depth):**

| Aspect | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | Memory and I/O share the **same** logical address space. | Memory and I/O have **separate** address spaces. |
| **Instructions Used** | Normal memory instructions (e.g., `LDR`, `STR` on ARM; `MOV` to memory on x86). | Special I/O instructions (`IN`, `OUT` on 8085/8086). |
| **Processor Examples** | ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, 8051 (variant), MIPS. | Intel 8085, 8086 (in minimum mode with `IO/M` pin). |
| **Address Range** | Full 2^n range. | Limited (e.g., 8-bit = 256 ports on 8085; 16-bit = 65536 ports on 8086). |

**Conclusion:** Modern embedded systems overwhelmingly use MMIO due to compiler friendliness and unified programming; PMIO survives mainly in legacy 8/16-bit designs.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three advantages and three disadvantages of using a serial memory interface (e.g., SPI Flash) over a parallel memory interface in embedded systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of Serial Memory Interface:**
1. **Reduced pin count** — typically 4–6 pins (SCK, MOSI, MISO, CS) vs. dozens for parallel.
2. **Lower PCB complexity and cost** — fewer traces, smaller packages (SOIC-8 vs. TSOP-48).
3. **Lower power consumption** — fewer switching lines and lower drive strength needed.
4. (Bonus) Easier routing on multi-layer boards; supports longer cable lengths.

**Disadvantages of Serial Memory Interface:**
1. **Lower data throughput** — 1 bit per SCK cycle vs. n bits per cycle in parallel.
2. **More complex driver software** — requires bit-banging or peripheral configuration, command/address sequencing.
3. **Latency overhead** — command and address bytes must be sent before data, increasing access time for small reads.
4. (Bonus) Limited random-access performance — page-based reads can be slow.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module 2 typically carries one 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternative questions (A and B) are provided below; in the actual exam, you would answer **one** of the two.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Memory-Mapped I/O** scheme used in ARM-based embedded systems. Describe the role of the address decoder and the read/write control signals.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 16-bit processor has a 20-bit address bus and uses 8 KB × 8-bit ROM chips and 16 KB × 8-bit RAM chips. The system requires **64 KB of ROM** and **32 KB of RAM**. Design the memory map, compute the **number of chips needed**, the **number of address lines per chip**, and the **chip-select decoder logic** for both ROM and RAM.

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a) — Memory-Mapped I/O in ARM

**Definition:** Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO) is a scheme in which peripheral control and status registers are assigned addresses in the same logical address space as program memory. The processor accesses them using the same bus cycles as ordinary memory.

**Block Diagram (Conceptual):**

```
        +----------------------+
        |       ARM CPU        |
        |   (Cortex-M core)    |
        +----+-----+-----+-----+
             |     |     |
             A     D     RnW
             |     |     |
        =====|=====|=====|======  AHB / APB Bus
             |     |     |
   +---------+     |     +---------+
   |               |               |
+--+---+    +-----+----+     +-----+----+
| ROM  |    |   RAM    |     |  UART    |
| CS_n |    |   CS_n   |     |  CS_n    |
+------+    +----------+     +----------+
```

**Role of the Address Decoder:**

- The decoder (e.g., a 74LS138, or an internal SoC decoder) takes the high-order address lines as inputs.
- It activates exactly one **CS_n** (Chip Select) line per bus cycle.
- The lower-order address lines go directly to the device's internal memory array (to select a byte/word within the device).

**Read/Write Control:**

- **Read cycle:** CPU drives `RnW = 1` (or `OE_n` low) → device outputs data on the data bus.
- **Write cycle:** CPU drives `RnW = 0` (or `WE_n` low) → device latches data from the data bus.

**Key Advantage on ARM:** Since all registers (memory and peripherals) are addressable using normal `LDR` and `STR` instructions, C code can manipulate hardware using simple pointers, e.g., `*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014 = 0x20;`

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b) — Memory Design

**Step 1 — Number of chips needed.**

$$\text{ROM chips} = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{8 \text{ KB}} = 8$$

$$\text{RAM chips} = \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ KB}} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Address lines per chip.**

For ROM (8 KB = 8192 bytes):
$$m_{\text{ROM}} = \log_2(8192) = 13 \text{ lines}$$

For RAM (16 KB = 16384 bytes):
$$m_{\text{RAM}} = \log_2(16384) = 14 \text{ lines}$$

**Step 3 — System address map (lower 64 KB region assumed):**

| Device | Range (low-order 16 bits) | Size | Decoder Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| ROM bank 0 | 0x0000 – 0x1FFF | 8 KB | Y0 |
| ROM bank 1 | 0x2000 – 0x3FFF | 8 KB | Y1 |
| ROM bank 2 | 0x4000 – 0x5FFF | 8 KB | Y2 |
| ROM bank 3 | 0x6000 – 0x7FFF | 8 KB | Y3 |
| ROM bank 4 | 0x8000 – 0x9FFF | 8 KB | Y4 |
| ROM bank 5 | 0xA000 – 0xBFFF | 8 KB | Y5 |
| ROM bank 6 | 0xC000 – 0xDFFF | 8 KB | Y6 |
| ROM bank 7 | 0xE000 – 0xFFFF | 8 KB | Y7 |

**Wait — this conflicts with RAM!** If the system uses the full 64 KB for ROM in the lower region, RAM must go elsewhere. **Correction (realistic design):** Allocate ROM to lower 32 KB and RAM to upper 32 KB:

- ROM (4 × 8 KB = 32 KB): `0x0000 – 0x7FFF` → Y0, Y1, Y2, Y3.
- RAM (2 × 16 KB = 32 KB): `0x8000 – 0xBFFF` and `0xC000 – 0xFFFF` → Y4, Y5.

But the question says **64 KB ROM and 32 KB RAM** = 96 KB total, exceeding the 16-bit 64 KB limit. **We must assume a 20-bit address bus (1 MB space)**, with ROM in the lower 64 KB and RAM in the next 32 KB.

**Revised Map (using 20-bit address bus):**

| Device | Range | Size |
|---|---|---|
| ROM (8 × 8 KB) | 0x00000 – 0x0FFFF | 64 KB |
| RAM (2 × 16 KB) | 0x10000 – 0x17FFF | 32 KB |

**Step 4 — Decoder design.**

Use a 74LS138 decoder:
- Inputs C, B, A ← A16, A15, A14
- Enables: G1 = +5V, G2A_n = A19·A18·A17, G2B_n = GND
- Outputs: Y0, Y1, Y2, Y3 → 4 ROM chip groups (with sub-decoding for 8 chips)
- Y4 → RAM bank 0; Y5 → RAM bank 1

For 8 ROM chips, use a **sub-decoder** (74LS138) with A13, A12, A11 selecting one of 8 ROMs, and the main decoder's Y0 enabling the sub-decoder.

**Conclusion:** The system needs **8 ROM chips** and **2 RAM chips**, requiring a **two-level decoder hierarchy** with address lines A19…A11 used for chip selection and A12…A0 (or A10…A0 within each chip) for byte selection.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Memory-Mapped I/O** and **Isolated I/O** in detail. State at least **four points of comparison** and explain which scheme is used in modern ARM-based microcontrollers, with justification.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **SPI protocol** used to interface a serial Flash memory with an MCU. Draw the signal timing diagram of a single-byte read operation and compute the **effective bandwidth** for reading 1 KB of data with SPI clock = 25 MHz, command byte = 1, address bytes = 3, and dummy byte = 1.

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a) — MMIO vs. PMIO

| # | Comparison Point | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Address Space** | Unified — peripherals live in the same address space as memory. | Separate — dedicated I/O address space (8 or 16 bits). |
| 2 | **Instructions** | Normal load/store instructions work on both memory and I/O. | Special `IN` and `OUT` instructions. |
| 3 | **Control Signals** | Only RD_n and WR_n are needed. | Additional IO/M_n signal distinguishes the two. |
| 4 | **Address Range for I/O** | Full 2^n range. | Limited to 256 (8-bit) or 65536 (16-bit) ports. |
| 5 | **Compiler Support** | Excellent (C pointers work). | Requires inline assembly. |
| 6 | **Decoding Complexity** | Single decoder. | Two decoders (memory + I/O). |
| 7 | **Examples** | ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, MIPS. | Intel 8085, 8086. |

**Modern ARM Choice:** All ARM Cortex-M and Cortex-A processors use **MMIO exclusively**. Justification:
1. Simplified ISA — no special I/O instructions needed.
2. Compiler-friendly — C code can directly access registers.
3. Scalable to large address spaces (32/64-bit).
4. Reduces hardware complexity (no separate IO/M pin).

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b) — SPI Serial Flash Read

**SPI Protocol Basics:**
- Four signals: **SCK** (clock), **MOSI** (master out), **MISO** (master in), **CS_n** (chip select).
- Master generates SCK and initiates transfers; slave responds.
- Most significant bit (MSB) is transmitted first by convention.
- Four SPI modes (0, 1, 2, 3) defined by CPOL and CPHA.

**Read Operation (single-byte, simplified):**
1. Master pulls CS_n LOW.
2. Master sends 1 command byte (e.g., `0x03` for Read Data) on MOSI.
3. Master sends 3 address bytes on MOSI.
4. Master sends 1 dummy byte on MOSI (or captures one on MISO for latency).
5. Master clocks out data bytes on MISO.
6. Master pulls CS_n HIGH to terminate.

**Bandwidth Calculation:**

Given:
- SPI clock: f_SCK = 25 MHz
- Command bytes: 1
- Address bytes: 3
- Dummy bytes: 1
- Payload: 1 KB = 1024 bytes
- Total bits transmitted per page:

$$N_{\text{bits}} = 8 \times (1 + 3 + 1 + 1024) = 8 \times 1029 = 8232 \text{ bits}$$

Time to transfer:

$$T = \frac{N_{\text{bits}}}{f_{\text{SCK}}} = \frac{8232}{25 \times 10^6} = 329.28 \text{ μs}$$

Effective bandwidth:

$$BW = \frac{1024 \text{ bytes}}{329.28 \text{ μs}} = \frac{8192 \text{ bits}}{329.28 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} \approx 24.88 \text{ Mbps} \approx 3.11 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Conclusion:** The effective SPI bandwidth of 3.11 MB/s is only slightly less than the raw rate of 25/8 = 3.125 MB/s, with an overhead of approximately 0.5%.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the data bus width (n) in capacity calculations.** The most common error: writing `C = 2^m` for a 16-bit system. The correct form is `C = 2^m × n`.
>
> 2. **Confusing bits and bytes.** A 16 KB chip stores `16 × 1024 × 8 = 131,072` bits, not 16,384 bits.
>
> 3. **Using PMIO on ARM.** If the question specifies ARM Cortex-M, you MUST use MMIO. PMIO is not supported on ARM.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the chip-select logic.** Memory design questions require explicit CS_n generation; a partial decode answer is incomplete and will lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **SPI timing diagram mistakes.** Always show CS_n going LOW before SCK starts and going HIGH after the last bit. MISO/MOSI bits must be shown changing on the correct SCK edge (mode-dependent).
>
