> 6. **Not stating SPI mode.** In a 14-mark question, always specify **CPOL and CPHA** for the timing diagram (Mode 0: CPOL=0, CPHA=0 is most common).
>
> 7. **Mixing up I²C and SPI.** I²C uses 2 wires (SCL, SDA) with open-drain + pull-ups; SPI uses 4+ wires with push-pull. Examiners deduct marks for confusing the two.
>
> 8. **Skipping the address-decoding analysis.** Many students compute the number of chips but do not show how the CS_n signals are generated. Always include the decoder logic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory According to Type of Interface**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO):** Peripherals share the memory address space; same instructions for both.
- ✅ **Isolated/Port-Mapped I/O (PMIO):** Separate I/O address space; uses `IN`/`OUT` instructions.
- ✅ **Parallel Memory:** Multiple bits transferred simultaneously on a wide data bus.
- ✅ **Serial Memory:** Bits transferred one at a time; uses SPI, I²C, Microwire, etc.

### 🔑 Must-Know Formulas
- ✅ Number of addressable locations: **N = 2^m** (m = address lines)
- ✅ Total memory capacity in bits: **C = 2^m × n** (n = data lines)
- ✅ Total memory capacity in bytes: **C_bytes = (2^m × n) / 8**
- ✅ Number of chips: **K = ⌈Required memory / Memory per chip⌉**
- ✅ Address lines per chip: **m_chip = log₂(memory per chip in bytes)**
- ✅ Decoder inputs needed: **d = log₂(number of chips)**
- ✅ Memory bandwidth (parallel): **BW = f × n / 8**
- ✅ Wait states: **WS = ⌈t_access / t_CPU⌉ − 1**

### 🔑 Must-Know Processors
- ✅ **ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, MIPS** → MMIO only.
- ✅ **Intel 8085, 8086** → Both MMIO and PMIO supported (8086 supports both, 8085 is PMIO-only).

### 🔑 Must-Know Serial Protocols
- ✅ **SPI:** 4-wire, full-duplex, push-pull, fast (MHz–100s of MHz), MSB-first.
- ✅ **I²C:** 2-wire, half-duplex, open-drain, slower (100 kHz, 400 kHz, 1 MHz, 3.4 MHz), addressable bus.
- ✅ **UART:** Asynchronous, 2-wire (TX, RX), no clock, baud-rate-based.
- ✅ **One-Wire:** 1-wire plus ground, very low speed, used by Maxim/Dallas iButtons.

### 🔑 Must-Know Address Decoding Methods
- ✅ **Linear decoding:** One address line directly drives CS_n. Simple but causes **address aliasing**.
- ✅ **Full decoding:** All address lines are decoded uniquely. No aliasing. Uses 74LS138.
- ✅ **Partial decoding:** Some high-order lines are ignored. Causes mirroring.

### 🔑 Must-Know Memory Signals
- ✅ **CS_n** (Chip Select, active LOW) — selects the device.
- ✅ **OE_n** (Output Enable, active LOW) — enables the output drivers for read.
- ✅ **WE_n** (Write Enable, active LOW) — latches input data for write.
- ✅ **SCK, MOSI, MISO, CS_n** — SPI quartet.

### 🔑 Must-Know Memory Devices
- ✅ **6264** — 8 KB × 8 static RAM (SRAM), parallel.
- ✅ **27C256** — 32 KB × 8 EPROM, parallel.
- ✅ **28C64/256** — EEPROM, parallel.
- ✅ **25xxx series** — SPI Flash (e.g., W25Q64 = 64 Mbit SPI NOR Flash).
- ✅ **24xxx series** — I²C EEPROM (e.g., AT24C256 = 256 Kbit I²C EEPROM).
- ✅ **FRAM** (e.g., Cypress FM25V02) — serial non-volatile, very fast, high endurance.

### 🔑 Exam Pattern Reminders
- ✅ For 3-mark questions: 2–3 crisp points with one example each.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions: Always include a **diagram**, a **table of comparison**, a **step-by-step derivation**, and a **concluding statement**.
- ✅ Always specify **units** (ns, MHz, MB/s, bits) — marks are deducted for missing units.
- ✅ When asked to "design," include the decoder logic, not just chip counts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Selection for Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET723 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Memory Selection for Embedded Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Memory Selection for Embedded Systems** is the systematic engineering process of identifying, evaluating, and choosing the most appropriate memory devices (ROM, RAM, Flash, EEPROM, etc.) for a given embedded application based on a weighted trade-off between **functional requirements**, **performance metrics**, **power budget**, **physical constraints**, and **economic cost**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the selection process operates across three hierarchical layers: 1. **On-Chip Memory** (Registers, Cache, Embedded SRAM/ROM inside the MCU/MPU) 2. **External Primary Memory** (Off-chip SRAM, SDRAM, NOR Flash) 3. **Secondary/Mass Storage** (NAND Flash, EEPROM, SD cards, eMMC) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):** > Memory selection involves matching the application's data/instruction footprint, speed, volatility, and power profile with the right mix of memory technologies. Unlike general-purpose computing, embedded memory design is **resource-constrained** — there is no scope for "over-provisioning." --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Kitchen Pantry Imagine you are designing a **smart refrigerator** (the embedded system). You have three types of storage: | Memory Type | Refrigerator Analogy | Character | |---|---|---| | **CPU Registers** | The chef's hands (currently chopping) | Fastest, smallest, most expensive | | **Cache SRAM (On-chip)** | The countertop spice rack | Very fast, very small | | **Main RAM** | The kitchen shelves | Fast, medium size, volatile (loses food on power off) | | **Flash/ROM** | The deep freezer | Non-volatile, large, slow to access | | **Secondary Storage (SD/eMMC)** | The grocery warehouse | Largest, slowest, cheapest | A chef (CPU) needs the **right ingredient in the right place at the right time**. If garlic (frequently used variable) is kept in the warehouse (Flash), the dish will be unbearably slow. If saffron (rarely used) occupies the countertop (Cache), it wastes precious space. **Memory selection is the art of placing each data item in its optimal pantry location.** --- ## 1.3 Classification of Embedded Memory (Foundational Vocabulary) ### A. By Volatility - **Volatile Memory**: Loses data when power is removed. Examples: SRAM, DRAM, SDRAM. - **Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)**: Retains data without power. Examples: Mask ROM, Flash, EEPROM, FRAM. ### B. By Access Pattern - **Random Access Memory (RAM)**: Any location can be accessed in equal time. Used for working data. - **Sequential Access Memory**: Access time depends on physical position (e.g., NAND Flash page read). - **Direct Access**: A hybrid (e.g., magnetic disk in legacy designs). ### C. By Write Capability - **Read-Only (ROM, NOR Flash in execute-in-place mode)**: Writes are rare or impossible during operation. - **Read/Write (RAM, NAND Flash, EEPROM)**: Symmetric read/write. ### D. By Physical Location - **Internal/On-Chip Memory**: Embedded inside the microcontroller (e.g., STM32 has 512 KB internal Flash). - **External/Off-Chip Memory**: Connected via memory bus (FSMC, EBI, Quad-SPI). > [!NOTE] > **Volatility** is the single most important decision driver. If the firmware must survive a power cycle, **at least one NVM device is mandatory** for boot code and persistent configuration. --- ## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary) | Term | Formal Definition | Unit | |---|---|---| | **Density (Capacity)** | Total number of bits a memory chip can store | Bits, Bytes, KB, MB, GB | | **Access Time ($t_{acc}$)** | Time elapsed from address stable → data valid | **nanoseconds (ns)** | | **Cycle Time ($t_{cyc}$)** | Minimum interval between two consecutive memory operations | **ns** | | **Bandwidth (BW)** | Maximum rate of data transfer | **MB/s**, **GB/s** | | **Endurance** | Number of write/erase cycles before failure | **Cycles** (e.g., $10^5$, $10^6$) | | **Data Retention** | Duration data persists without power | **Years** (typically 10–20) | | **Power Consumption** | Active + standby current draw | **mW**, **µW** | | **Footprint** | PCB area consumed by the package | **mm²** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (KTU's Most Tested Concept) Embedded systems follow a strict **Memory Hierarchy** where speed and cost are inversely proportional to capacity. ``` ▲ Speed (Fastest) │ │ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ CPU Regs │ ~1 ns, bytes, $$$/bit │ ├──────────────┤ │ │ L1 Cache │ ~1-3 ns, KB │ ├──────────────┤ │ │ L2 / SRAM │ ~5-10 ns, 100s of KB │ ├──────────────┤ │ │ Main RAM │ ~50-70 ns, MB │ ├──────────────┤ │ │ NOR Flash │ ~70-100 ns, MB (XIP) │ ├──────────────┤ │ │ NAND Flash │ ~25 µs/page, GB │ ├──────────────┤ │ │ SD/eMMC │ ms latency, GB-TB │ └──────────────┘ │ ▼ Capacity (Largest) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does the hierarchy exist?** > - **Speed**: Smaller memories are physically closer to the CPU and have shorter word-lines/bit-lines. > - **Economics**: A 32 KB SRAM cell costs 100× more per bit than a 4 GB NAND Flash. > - **Power**: A larger memory's sense amplifiers consume more dynamic switching power. --- ## 2.2 Operational Logic: How Memory is Selected (The 7-Step Decision Process) ### Step 1 — Determine Application Class - **Bare-metal firmware device** (e.g., washing machine) → Tiny footprint, internal Flash + 4 KB RAM suffices. - **RTOS-based device** (e.g., industrial PLC) → Needs external SDRAM for task stacks. - **Linux-based gateway** (e.g., IoT router) → Requires DDR3 + eMMC + NOR boot Flash. ### Step 2 — Quantify Memory Footprint Estimate **three** memory requirements: - **Code (.text) size** → Stored in ROM/Flash - **Read-Only Data (.rodata)** → Constants, lookup tables → ROM/Flash - **Read-Write Data (.data + .bss + Heap + Stack)** → RAM > **Rule of Thumb (KTU 2024)**: Always provision **30–50 % headroom** above the compiled binary size for future firmware updates (DFU/OTA). ### Step 3 — Determine Volatility Requirements - Boot loader & firmware → **Non-Volatile** (Flash/ROM) - Sensor samples → Volatile OK if data is ephemeral - User configuration (Wi-Fi creds) → **Non-Volatile** (EEPROM/Flash sector) ### Step 4 — Establish Performance Envelope - CPU clock $f_{CPU}$ (e.g., 72 MHz for STM32F1) - Required throughput = (Instructions per cycle × Code size) / Acceptable execution time - If $t_{cyc}$ of memory > $1/f_{CPU}$, **wait states** must be inserted → slows the CPU. ### Step 5 — Evaluate Power Budget - Battery-powered IoT sensor: choose **FRAM** or **MRAM** over Flash (Flash erase currents can spike to **15 mA**, killing coin-cell budgets). - Mains-powered gateway: Flash is acceptable. ### Step 6 — Apply Physical & Interface Constraints - Package size (BGA vs TSOP vs WLCSP) - Bus width (8/16/32-bit) - Interface protocol (parallel FSMC, SPI, Quad-SPI, Octal-SPI, HyperBus) ### Step 7 — Run Cost-Per-Bit Optimization $$ \text{Cost-per-bit} = \frac{\text{Unit Price (₹ or \$)}}{\text{Density (bits)}} $$ Choose the **lowest cost-per-bit** that still meets all upper-layer constraints. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This!) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and divide symbols use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C = 2^{n} \times w$ | Memory Capacity from address lines ($n$) and data width ($w$) | bits | | 2 | $C_{bytes} = 2^{n} \times \dfrac{w}{8}$ | Same, in bytes | Bytes | | 3 | $A_{range} = [A_{min}, \ A_{max}]$ | Address range = $[BaseAddr, \ BaseAddr + 2^{n} - 1]$ | Hex / Dec | | 4 | $BW = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | Bandwidth = Data transferred ÷ Transfer time | MB/s | | 5 | $BW = f_{bus} \times w_{bus} \times \frac{N_{transfers}}{cycle}$ | Practical bus bandwidth | MB/s | | 6 | $N_{ws} = \left\lceil \dfrac{t_{cyc}^{mem}}{T_{CPU}} \right\rceil - 1$ | Number of wait states | Integer | | 7 | $E_{total} = V \times I_{active} \times t_{active} + V \times I_{stdby} \times t_{stdby}$ | Total energy over an operation cycle | Joules | | 8 | $\eta_{mem} = \dfrac{t_{access}}{t_{cycle}} \times 100\%$ | Memory utilization efficiency | % | | 9 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic power (CMOS memory) | Watts | | 10 | $\text{Latency}_{avg} = \sum p_i \cdot L_i$ | Average memory access latency (weighted by hit rate) | ns | **Where:** - $n$ = Number of address lines - $w$ = Data bus width in bits - $T_{CPU} = 1/f_{CPU}$ = CPU clock period - $\alpha$ = Switching activity factor (0 to 1) - $C_L$ = Load capacitance - $p_i$ = Probability of accessing memory level $i$ - $L_i$ = Latency of level $i$ --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Typical Memory Stack | |---|---| | **Wearable fitness band** | MCU internal Flash (256 KB) + internal SRAM (32 KB) + SPI Flash for logs (16 MB) | | **Automotive ECU (AUTOSAR)** | External NOR Flash (4 MB for XIP) + external SRAM (256 KB) + EEPROM (4 KB for CAN IDs) | | **Smartphone SoC** | L1 cache (64 KB) + L2 (1 MB) + LPDDR4X (8 GB) + UFS 3.1 (256 GB) | | **Satellite (CubeSat)** | MRAM (radiation-hardened) + SRAM (with EDAC) — Flash avoided due to SEE sensitivity | | **Smart meter** | Internal Flash + external SPI Flash (tamper-evident log storage for 10 years) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The selection of **radiation-hardened MRAM** in satellites versus **commodity NAND Flash** in smartphones is driven entirely by the operating environment — the same logical "memory function" can be implemented with radically different hardware. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Memory Capacity from Address Lines **Given:** A memory chip has $n$ address pins and a data bus of $w$ bits. **Goal:** Derive the total storage capacity in bits and bytes. ### Step 1 — Count Distinct Addressable Locations Each address pin can be either 0 or 1. With $n$ independent address lines, the number of unique addresses is given by the counting principle of combinatorics: $$ N_{locations} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times 2 \cdots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} = 2^{n} $$ **Textual logic:** A single address line ($n=1$) yields 2 locations (0 and 1). Adding a second line doubles it to 4 locations. Each new line **doubles** the address space. ### Step 2 — Multiply by Word Width Each address returns a datum of $w$ bits. Therefore, the **total bit capacity** is: $$ C_{bits} = N_{locations} \times w = 2^{n} \times w $$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Bytes Since 1 Byte = 8 bits: $$ C_{bytes} = \frac{C_{bits}}{8} = 2^{n} \times \frac{w}{8} $$ **Numerical Example (KTU 2024 typical problem):** A memory chip has 16 address lines and an 8-bit data bus. $$ C_{bits} = 2^{16} \times 8 = 65536 \times 8 = 524288 \text{ bits} $$ $$ C_{bytes} = 2^{16} \times \frac{8}{8} = 65536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Address Range Calculation **Given:** A 256 KB SRAM is mapped starting at base address `0x60000000` in the address space. **Goal:** Determine the upper-bound address of this memory block. ### Step 1 — Convert Capacity to Bytes $$ 256 \text{ KB} = 256 \times 1024 = 262144 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Hexadecimal Compute $262144$ in hex: - $262144 = 0x40000$ ### Step 3 — Compute the End Address $$ A_{end} = A_{base} + (C_{bytes} - 1) $$ **Why subtract 1?** Because if the memory holds 262144 bytes, the addresses are `0` through `262143` (a count of 262144). The **last byte** sits at offset `C - 1`. $$ A_{end} = \text{0x60000000} + \text{0x40000} - 1 = \text{0x6003FFFF} $$ ### Step 4 — Express the Address Range $$ A_{range} = [\text{0x60000000}, \ \text{0x6003FFFF}] $$ **Verification:** $$ \text{Range size} = \text{0x6003FFFF} - \text{0x60000000} + 1 = \text{0x40000} = 262144 \text{ bytes} \ \checkmark $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wait State Calculation **Given:** - CPU clock frequency $f_{CPU} = 50$ MHz - External memory access time $t_{cyc}^{mem} = 65$ ns **Goal:** Find the number of wait states required. ### Step 1 — Compute CPU Clock Period $$ T_{CPU} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \text{ ns} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Number of CPU Cycles Needed $$ N_{cycles} = \frac{t_{cyc}^{mem}}{T_{CPU}} = \frac{65 \text{ ns}}{20 \text{ ns}} = 3.25 \text{ cycles} $$ ### Step 3 — Round Up to Integer Since fractional cycles are impossible, we must **ceiling** the value: $$ N_{cycles}^{int} = \lceil 3.25 \rceil = 4 \text{ cycles} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Wait States The first cycle is the actual access. The remaining cycles are "wait" states: $$ N_{ws} = N_{cycles}^{int} - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ wait states} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Impact:** 3 wait states mean the CPU is **idle for 60 ns** during every memory access. If the CPU fetches 1 instruction per cycle, performance is degraded by a factor of 4×. This is why on-chip SRAM (single-cycle) is preferred for time-critical code. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Memory Bandwidth (Sequential Burst) **Given:** - Memory operates at $f_{bus} = 100$ MHz - Bus width = 16 bits = 2 Bytes - Burst length = 4 transfers/cycle - 100% bus utilization **Goal:** Find peak bandwidth in MB/s. ### Step 1 — Bytes per Transfer $$ B_{trans} = \frac{16 \text{ bits}}{8} = 2 \text{ Bytes} $$ ### Step 2 — Transfers per Second $$ N_{trans/sec} = f_{bus} \times N_{transfers/cycle} = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 4 = 400 \times 10^{6} \text{ transfers/s} $$ ### Step 3 — Bandwidth $$ BW = N_{trans/sec} \times B_{trans} = 400 \times 10^{6} \times 2 = 800 \times 10^{6} \text{ Bytes/s} $$ $$ BW = 800 \text{ MB/s} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Embedded Memory Technologies | Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash | SRAM | DRAM | EEPROM | FRAM | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Volatility** | Non-Volatile | Non-Volatile | Volatile | Volatile | Non-Volatile | Non-Volatile | | **Read Speed** | ~70 ns (fast) | ~25 µs (slow) | ~5 ns (very fast) | ~50 ns (moderate) | ~50 ns | ~50 ns | | **Write Speed** | ~1 s/page (slow) | ~200 µs/page | ~5 ns (fast) | ~50 ns | ~5 ms (very slow) | ~50 ns (fast) | | **Erase Granularity** | Sector (64–256 KB) | Block (128–512 KB) | Byte (no erase needed) | Byte | Byte | Byte | | **Endurance (cycles)** | $10^{4}$–$10^{5}$ | $10^{3}$–$10^{5}$ | Unlimited (reads) | Unlimited (reads) | $10^{6}$ | $10^{14}$ (huge!) | | **Density** | MB (typical) | GB (typical) | KB–MB | MB–GB | KB | KB–MB | | **Power (Active)** | ~15 mA (erase spike) | ~15 mA | ~10 mA | ~100 mA (refresh) | ~5 mA | ~5 mA | | **Cost per bit** | High | Very Low | Very High | Low | Very High | Very High | | **XIP Support** | **Yes** (random read) | No (page-based) | Yes (natively) | Yes | Yes (slow) | Yes | | **Best Use** | Boot code, firmware | Mass storage, logs | Cache, stack | Main RAM | Config bytes | Tamper-proof logs | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Figure 1: Memory Selection Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Define Application Requirements] --> B{Volatile or Persistent Data?} B -- Persistent --> C[Firmware Size? User Data?] B -- Ephemeral --> D[Working Data Size?] C -- Firmware <= Internal Flash --> E[Use Internal Flash for Boot + App] C -- Firmware > Internal Flash --> F[Add External NOR Flash with FSMC/SPI] D -- < 64 KB --> G[Use Internal SRAM] D -- 64 KB to 1 MB --> H[Add External SRAM via Bus] D -- > 1 MB --> I[Add External SDRAM DDR] E --> J{Power Budget Critical?} F --> J G --> J H --> J I --> J J -- Yes Battery Powered --> K[Consider FRAM or MRAM] J -- No Mains Powered --> L[Standard Flash is OK] K --> M[End: Final BOM] L --> M ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (A, B, C… M). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as standalone identifiers. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric without markdown bold/italic. --- ## 4.2 Figure 2: Memory Hierarchy in a Typical ARM Cortex-M4 Embedded System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CoreUnit[CPU Core] R0[Registers R0-R15] R1[NVIC Vector Table in Flash] end subgraph OnChip[On-Chip Memory] S1[L1 Instruction Cache 4KB] S2[Internal SRAM 256KB] S3[Internal Flash 1MB] end subgraph OffChip[Off-Chip Memory via FSMC Quad-SPI] T1[External NOR Flash 16MB] T2[External SDRAM 32MB] T3[SPI EEPROM 4KB] end subgraph Mass[Mass Storage] U1[SD Card via SDMMC] U2[eMMC via SDMMC] end R0 -.fastest.-> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> T1 T1 --> T2 T2 --> T3 T3 --> U1 U1 --> U2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** Edges represent a **speed gradient** — moving downward means **slower but larger**. The CPU rarely accesses mass storage directly; instead, the DMA controller or filesystem driver shuttles data into external SDRAM where the CPU can process it at speed. --- ## 4.3 Figure 3: Memory Mapping Block Architecture (Address Decoder View) ```mermaid flowchart LR CPU[CPU with Address Bus] --> DEC[Address Decoder
74HC138 or CPLD] DEC --> CS1[CS0: Internal Flash 0x00000000-0x000FFFFF] DEC --> CS2[CS1: External SRAM 0x60000000-0x6003FFFF] DEC --> CS3[CS2: External NOR 0x64000000-0x64FFFFFF] DEC --> CS4[CS3: EEPROM 0x68000000-0x68000FFF] CS1 --> I1[Code .text segment] CS2 --> I2[Stack and Heap] CS3 --> I3[Bootloader and OTA staging] CS4 --> I4[Device Configuration] ``` **Textual Explanation:** The address decoder (often a 3-to-8 line decoder like 74HC138) examines the high-order address bits and activates exactly **one** Chip Select (CS) line. Each peripheral gets a unique, non-overlapping window in the linear address space. This is the **physical embodiment** of memory selection — the CPU sees a unified 4 GB address space, but the decoder routes each access to the correct physical chip. --- ## 4.4 Figure 4: Volatility Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD P[New Data Item to Store] --> Q{Must survive power-off?} Q -- Yes --> R{How often written?} Q -- No --> S{Random or sequential access?} R -- Rarely boot once --> T[Mask ROM or OTP] R -- Occasionally few KB --> U[EEPROM or Data Flash] R -- Often big blocks --> V[External NAND Flash] R -- Extremely often byte level --> W[FRAM or MRAM] R -- Never after manufacture --> X[Mask ROM] S -- Random small --> Y[SRAM] S -- Sequential massive --> Z[DRAM SDRAM with refresh] S -- Both --> AA[DDR SDRAM] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer / Definition Type) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile memory. Give two examples of each and state one embedded application where non-volatile memory is mandatory.** **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):** | Aspect | Volatile Memory | Non-Volatile Memory | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Loses stored data when power is removed | Retains data even after power-off | | **Examples** | SRAM, DRAM, SDRAM | Flash, EEPROM, Mask ROM, FRAM | | **Speed** | Generally faster read/write | Slower write (especially Flash) | | **Use Case** | Temporary working memory (stack, heap) | Firmware, configuration, logs | **Mandatory NVM Application:** A digital door lock must remember the user PIN and last lock state even when the battery is removed. Hence **non-volatile memory (typically internal Flash or external EEPROM)** is mandatory. *(1 mark for differentiation table, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for application + justification.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **List any four parameters used for selecting a memory device for an embedded system. Briefly explain how "endurance" affects the choice of Flash memory in wearable devices.** **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):** **Four Selection Parameters (1 mark):** 1. **Density (Capacity)** – must hold code + data. 2. **Access Time** – must not starve the CPU. 3. **Power Consumption** – critical for battery devices. 4. **Endurance** – number of write/erase cycles supported. **Endurance in Wearables (2 marks):** Wearables continuously log sensor data (heart rate, steps, SpO₂) and may write to Flash every second. If the device has only $10^{4}$ write cycles per sector and writes once per second to the same sector, the sector will fail in approximately **2.7 hours** ($10^{4} / 3600$). This is unacceptable. Therefore, wearables use either: - **Wear-leveling algorithms** to distribute writes across sectors, or - **FRAM** (with $10^{14}$ endurance) for high-frequency log buffers. --- ## 📗 PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Full Descriptive Type) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full. --- ### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the different types of memory used in embedded systems with suitable examples. Discuss the role of each in the memory hierarchy.** **Model Solution:** **1. On-Chip Registers (1.5 marks)** - Flip-flops inside the CPU core (R0–R15 in ARM). - Access time: **1 CPU cycle** (~10 ns at 100 MHz). - Role: Hold operands, addresses, and the program counter. The **fastest** level of the hierarchy. **2. On-Chip Cache Memory (1 mark)** - Small, fast SRAM storing recently used instructions/data. - Hit rate typically **> 90 %**, reducing external memory accesses. - Role: Bridge between CPU speed and external memory speed. **3. Main RAM – SRAM vs DRAM (1.5 marks)** - **SRAM**: 6 transistors per cell, no refresh, fast (~5 ns), used for cache and small embedded systems. - **DRAM**: 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per cell, needs refresh every **64 ms**, denser, cheaper. - Role: Holds stack, heap, and global variables at runtime. **4. Non-Volatile Memory – Flash & ROM (2 marks)** - **Mask ROM**: Programmed at fabrication, cheapest per bit for high volumes. - **NOR Flash**: Random read access, supports **Execute-In-Place (XIP)**. Used for boot code. - **NAND Flash**: Page-based, high density, used for mass storage in SD cards, eMMC. - **EEPROM**: Byte-level erase, used for small configuration data (e.g., calibration constants). **5. Secondary / Mass Storage (1 mark)** - SD cards, eMMC, USB drives. - Capacity in GB/TB, access time in ms. - Role: Long-term data storage (logs, media, firmware updates). **Concluding remark (optional for full marks):** > The hierarchy ensures that the **most frequently accessed** data sits **closest to the CPU**, balancing the competing demands of speed, cost, and capacity. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A memory chip has 14 address lines and an 8-bit data bus. Calculate its capacity, address range when mapped at base address `0x20000000`, and determine the number of wait states required if the CPU runs at 25 MHz and the memory access time is 100 ns.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Capacity Calculation (2 marks)** $$ C_{bits} = 2^{n} \times w = 2^{14} \times 8 = 16384 \times 8 = 131072 \text{ bits} $$ $$ C_{bytes} = 2^{14} \times \frac{8}{8} = 16384 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ KB} $$ **Step 2 — Address Range (2 marks)** Capacity in hex: $16384 = 0x4000$ $$ A_{end} = 0x20000000 + 0x4000 - 1 = 0x20003FFF $$ $$ \boxed{A_{range} = [\text{0x20000000}, \ \text{0x20003FFF}]} $$ **Step 3 — Wait States (3 marks)** $$ T_{CPU} = \frac{1}{25 \times 10^{6}} = 40 \text{ ns} $$ $$ N_{cycles} = \frac{t_{acc}}{T_{CPU}} = \frac{100 \text{ ns}}{40 \text{ ns}} = 2.5 \text{ cycles} $$ $$ N_{cycles}^{int} = \lceil 2.5 \rceil = 3 $$ $$ N_{ws} = 3 - 1 = \boxed{2 \text{ wait states}} $$ **Final Answer:** Capacity = **16 KB**, Range = **`0x20000000` – `0x20003FFF`**, Wait States = **2**. --- ### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Discuss the criteria for selecting memory in an embedded system. Explain with a real-world example (e.g., IoT wearable or automotive ECU).** **Model Solution:** **The 7 Selection Criteria (4 marks):** 1. **Capacity** – Total bytes needed for code + data + future OTA updates. 2. **Performance (Access Time / Bandwidth)** – Must keep up with CPU; no wait states if possible. 3. **Volatility** – NVM for firmware and persistent config. 4. **Power** – Sleep current must be < 10 µA for battery devices. 5. **Endurance** – Number of write cycles over the product lifetime. 6. **Physical Size & Interface** – Package footprint and bus type (SPI, Quad-SPI, parallel). 7. **Cost** – Cost-per-bit at the BOM level. **Real-World Example: Fitness Wearable (3 marks)** A fitness band running FreeRTOS on an ARM Cortex-M4 needs: - **Internal Flash 1 MB**: Stores bootloader (32 KB), application code (256 KB), and OTA staging area (512 KB). - **Internal SRAM 256 KB**: Holds task stacks, FreeRTOS heap, sensor buffers. - **External SPI Flash 16 MB**: Non-volatile storage for step logs (10 years of data). - **Optional FRAM 64 KB**: Tamper-evident calibration log with $10^{14}$ endurance. **Why these choices?** - SRAM instead of DRAM → no refresh, lower power. - NOR Flash for code → supports XIP, fast boot. - NAND-style SPI Flash for mass storage → high density, low cost. - FRAM only for high-write-rate parameters → avoids Flash wear-out. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Compare NOR Flash and NAND Flash in terms of architecture, access method, speed, endurance, and application. Which is suitable for boot code storage in an automotive ECU? Justify.** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash | |---|---|---| | **Cell Architecture** | Parallel connection of cells, similar to NOR gate | Series connection (NAND string of 32–64 cells) | | **Access Method** | Random byte/word access (XIP) | Page-based (2 KB–16 KB pages) | | **Read Speed** | ~70 ns (fast) | ~25 µs first byte, then sequential | | **Write Speed** | ~1 s to erase a sector | ~200 µs per page (faster) | | **Endurance** | $10^{4}$–$10^{5}$ cycles | $10^{3}$–$10^{5}$ cycles | | **Bad Blocks** | None (always good) | Possible (managed by FTL/ECC) | | **Cost per bit** | High | Very Low | | **Application** | Boot code, firmware, BIOS | SSD, SD cards, eMMC, USB drives | **Suitability for Automotive ECU Boot Code (3 marks):** **NOR Flash is the correct choice** for the following reasons: 1. **Execute-In-Place (XIP):** The ECU's bootloader must run directly from Flash to save RAM. NOR's random access allows the CPU to fetch any instruction in constant time. 2. **Deterministic Boot Time:** Automotive systems require boot in **< 100 ms**. NAND's variable latency and bad-block scanning make deterministic boot impossible without external RAM staging. 3. **Reliability:** Bad-block management (FTL) in NAND adds complexity and is unsuitable for the safety-critical AUTOSAR boot sequence. 4. **Endurance:** Boot code is written **once per vehicle life**, so NAND's slightly lower endurance is irrelevant; the choice is driven by access pattern, not endurance. > **Conclusion:** NOR Flash is mandatory for automotive ECU boot storage. NAND Flash is reserved for infotainment mass storage (maps, media) where its density and cost advantages shine. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Based on KTU 2024 Valuation Key Patterns):** > > 1. **Forgetting to subtract 1** when computing the end address. Range is `[Base, Base + Size - 1]`, **not** `[Base, Base + Size]`. (–1 to –2 marks) > 2. **Confusing `2^14` with `14 × 2`**. Students often write 28 instead of 16384 locations. (–2 marks) > 3. **Not rounding up** the wait-state fraction. If $t_{acc} = 100$ ns and $T_{CPU} = 40$ ns, students write 2.5 cycles instead of 3 cycles. (–1 mark) > 4. **Mixing up NOR vs NAND** characteristics — e.g., claiming NAND supports XIP, or NOR has higher density. (–2 marks) > 5. **Writing "Flash" generically** without specifying NOR or NAND — examiners will not award full marks. > 6. **Ignoring headroom** — students compute exact code size but forget 30–50 % OTA headroom, losing practical-design marks. > 7. **Skipping units** in numerical answers. Always write `ns`, `MHz`, `KB`, `MB/s`. (–0.5 to –1 mark per missing unit) --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullets)** - ✅ **Memory Selection** = a multi-constraint optimization problem balancing **capacity, speed, power, volatility, endurance, cost, and footprint**. - ✅ **Hierarchy (top → bottom):** CPU Registers → Cache → SRAM → DRAM/SDRAM → NOR Flash → NAND Flash → Mass Storage. - ✅ **NOR Flash = XIP-capable, random access, used for BOOT code.** - ✅ **NAND Flash = page-based, high density, used for MASS STORAGE.** - ✅ **SRAM = fast, no refresh, expensive → cache, stack.** - ✅ **DRAM = needs refresh every ~64 ms, denser, cheaper → main RAM.** - ✅ **FRAM = high endurance ($10^{14}$), non-volatile, low power → tamper logs, metering.** - ✅ **Capacity formula:** $C = 2^{n} \times w$ (bits); $C = 2^{n} \times (w/8)$ (bytes). - ✅ **End address:** $A_{end} = A_{base} + C - 1$. - ✅ **Wait States:** $N_{ws} = \lceil t_{acc} / T_{CPU} \rceil - 1$. - ✅ **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_{bus} \times w_{bus} \times N_{transfers/cycle}$. - ✅ **Volatility rule:** Firmware & configuration ⇒ **Non-Volatile**. Stack/heap ⇒ **Volatile**. - ✅ **Endurance rule:** High write frequency ⇒ **FRAM or MRAM** (avoid Flash). - ✅ **Cost-per-bit rule:** Pick the cheapest memory whose performance/voltage/endurance still meets spec. - ✅ **Always provision 30–50 % memory headroom** for OTA/DFU updates. - ✅ **For safety-critical boot, NOR Flash is non-negotiable** (deterministic latency, XIP, no bad-block management). > **Final Mantra for KTU Viva:** > *"In embedded systems, memory is not a single device — it is a carefully orchestrated hierarchy. Selecting it is an act of engineering trade-off, not feature-checking."*

topics.title: Introduction to Sensors and Actuators for Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET723 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Sensors and Actuators for Embedded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor?

A **sensor** is an electronic, electromechanical, or electro-chemical device that detects physical, chemical, or biological quantities from the environment and converts them into an electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or digital output) that can be read by a microcontroller or processing unit.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a sensor is the *input subsystem* of any embedded system — it is the bridge between the physical world (analog, continuous) and the digital processing core (binary, discrete).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> A *transducer* is a generic device that converts one form of energy into another. A *sensor* is a specific type of transducer that converts a physical stimulus (light, temperature, pressure) into an electrical signal. All sensors are transducers, but not all transducers are sensors.

## 1.2 What is an Actuator?

An **actuator** is a device that converts an electrical control signal from a processor into a physical action — mechanical motion, heat, light, sound, or fluid flow. It is the *output subsystem* of an embedded system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Sense–Process–Actuate Loop:**
> Every embedded system follows the **Sense → Process → Actuate** loop. Sensors perform the *Sense* stage, the MCU performs the *Process* stage, and actuators perform the *Actuate* stage. This closed-loop paradigm is the backbone of IoT, robotics, automotive, and industrial automation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the **human body** as a biological embedded system:

| Human Body | Embedded System Equivalent | Function |
|------------|---------------------------|----------|
| Eyes, ears, skin, nose | **Sensors** (LDR, microphone, thermistor, gas sensor) | Detect environmental changes |
| Brain and nervous system | **Microcontroller / Processor** | Decision-making and processing |
| Muscles and hands | **Actuators** (DC motor, relay, speaker) | Produce physical action |

When you touch a hot pan (sensed by skin thermoreceptors), your brain processes the danger signal and your hand muscles (actuators) immediately retract. An embedded system performs the exact same sequence, but with silicon and copper.

## 1.4 Classification at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Classification Box:**
>
> **Sensors can be classified by:**
> 1. **Power Source:** Active (self-generating) vs. Passive (require external excitation)
> 2. **Output type:** Analog vs. Digital
> 3. **Physical quantity sensed:** Temperature, Light, Pressure, Motion, Chemical, Position, Humidity
>
> **Actuators can be classified by:**
> 1. **Energy type:** Electrical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Thermal, Mechanical
> 2. **Motion type:** Linear vs. Rotary
> 3. **Output type:** Continuous vs. Discrete

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Operational Principles

A sensor operates on a fundamental physical or chemical principle. When a physical measurand (the quantity being measured) interacts with the sensing element, an electrical property changes proportionally. The processor then reads this change to infer the original physical quantity.

### 2.1.1 Active vs. Passive Sensors

**Active Sensors (Self-Generating):**
- Generate their own electrical output directly from the physical stimulus.
- No external power (excitation voltage) is required for the sensing element.
- Examples: **Thermocouple** (Seebeck effect — temperature difference generates voltage), **Piezoelectric sensor** (mechanical stress generates charge), **Photovoltaic solar cell** (light generates voltage).

**Passive Sensors:**
- Require an external excitation voltage or current to operate.
- The output is a *variation* in an electrical parameter (resistance, capacitance, inductance) caused by the measurand.
- Examples: **Thermistor** (resistance varies with temperature), **LDR / Photoresistor** (resistance varies with light), **Potentiometer** (resistance varies with position), **Capacitive humidity sensor**.

### 2.1.2 Analog vs. Digital Sensors

| Type | Output | Example | MCU Interface |
|------|--------|---------|---------------|
| **Analog Sensor** | Continuous voltage/current proportional to measurand | LM35 temperature sensor, TMP36 | Requires **ADC** |
| **Digital Sensor** | Discrete binary pulses or serial data | DHT11, DS18B20, MPU6050 | Direct GPIO, I²C, SPI, UART |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:**
> Examiners frequently test whether students understand that an analog sensor like LM35 **cannot be connected directly** to a digital GPIO pin. The path is: Sensor → Signal Conditioning → **ADC** → MCU. Forgetting the ADC stage is a common 2-mark deduction.

## 2.2 Actuator Operational Principles

Actuators essentially perform the *reverse* operation of a sensor. They take an electrical command signal and convert it into physical work. Most actuators operate on one of the following principles:

1. **Electromagnetic principle:** Current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field produces force (F = BIL).
2. **Electrostatic principle:** Charged plates attract (F = ½ ε₀ A V² / d²).
3. **Piezoelectric principle:** Applied voltage causes material deformation.
4. **Thermal expansion:** Resistive heating causes material expansion.
5. **Hydraulic/Pneumatic:** Pressurized fluid/gas drives mechanical motion.

### 2.2.1 Common Actuators in Embedded Systems

- **DC Motor:** Rotary motion; speed controlled via PWM.
- **Stepper Motor:** Precise angular steps; used in CNC, printers, robotics.
- **Servo Motor:** Closed-loop position control (0°–180° typical hobby servo).
- **Relay:** Electromagnetic switch for high-voltage/high-current loads.
- **Solenoid:** Linear motion from electromagnetic push/pull.
- **Buzzer / Speaker:** Electrical signal → sound waves.
- **LED / LCD:** Electrical signal → light.
- **Heater / Peltier module:** Electrical signal → heat.

## 2.3 The Signal Conditioning Stage

Raw sensor outputs are rarely MCU-ready. They typically suffer from:
- Low amplitude (millivolts)
- High output impedance
- Noise contamination
- Non-linearity
- Offset voltage

**Signal conditioning** refers to the analog front-end processing that prepares the sensor signal for the ADC. It typically includes:

1. **Amplification** — using op-amps (inverting, non-inverting, instrumentation).
2. **Filtering** — low-pass (anti-aliasing), high-pass (DC removal), band-pass.
3. **Level shifting** — to match the ADC's input voltage range (e.g., 0–3.3 V).
4. **Linearization** — software lookup tables or analog compensation.
5. **Isolation** — optocouplers, transformers to protect the MCU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist Criterion (Anti-aliasing rule):**
> The sampling frequency $f_s$ of the ADC must be **at least twice** the highest frequency $f_{max}$ present in the input signal. In practice, engineers use $f_s \geq 5 \times f_{max}$. Failure to satisfy this causes *aliasing*, a destructive distortion.

## 2.4 ADC Resolution and Quantization

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** converts a continuous analog voltage into a discrete digital code. The resolution determines the smallest voltage step the ADC can distinguish.

**Key formulas:**

For an $n$-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{ref}$:

$$\text{Step Size } (\Delta V) = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

$$\text{Digital Output } (D) = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor$$

$$\text{Number of discrete levels} = 2^n$$

For a DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter) — used to drive analog actuators:

$$V_{out} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

where $D$ is the decimal equivalent of the digital input code.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| Step size (ADC) | $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$ | Volts | n = number of bits |
| Total levels (ADC) | $L = 2^n$ | — | e.g., 10-bit ADC → 1024 levels |
| ADC output code | $D = \lfloor V_{in} \cdot 2^n / V_{ref} \rfloor$ | Decimal integer | 0 to $2^n - 1$ |
| DAC output | $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{ref} / 2^n$ | Volts | D from 0 to $2^n - 1$ |
| Nyquist rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | Anti-aliasing condition |
| Sensor sensitivity | $S = \Delta V_{out} / \Delta x$ | V/unit | Output change per unit measurand |
| Force on conductor | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | Newtons | DC motor / voice coil basis |
| DC Motor speed | $N \propto V_{applied}$ | RPM | Linear in supply voltage |
| Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Ohms | Beta equation (Steinhart-Hart is more accurate) |
| Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V \approx S \cdot \Delta T$ | Volts | S ≈ 40 µV/°C for K-type |
| Piezoelectric | $V = g \cdot t \cdot \sigma$ | Volts | g = voltage coefficient, σ = stress |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These formulas are the foundation for designing any data acquisition system — from a medical ECG monitor (sensing microvolt cardiac signals) to an automotive engine controller (sensing pressure, temperature, and crankshaft position). Every IoT device, smartphone, and industrial PLC embeds this sensor-ADC-MCU-DAC-actuator chain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Output Code and Resolution

**Given:**
- $n$-bit ADC
- Reference voltage $V_{ref}$
- Analog input voltage $V_{in}$
- The ADC divides the reference range into $2^n$ equal intervals.

**Derivation:**

An $n$-bit ADC has $2^n$ distinct output codes ranging from $0$ to $2^n - 1$. The full-scale range $V_{ref}$ is divided into $2^n$ equal intervals called *Least Significant Bit (LSB)* intervals, also known as the *step size* $\Delta V$.

Therefore, by definition of equal division:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{L} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

For an arbitrary input voltage $V_{in}$, the digital output code $D$ (decimal) is the integer number of steps $\Delta V$ that fit into $V_{in}$:

$$D = \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref} / 2^n} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}}$$

Since digital codes are integers, we apply the floor function to round down to the nearest valid code:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor$$

Maximum code occurs when $V_{in}$ is just below $V_{ref}$:

$$D_{max} = 2^n - 1$$

Minimum code is when $V_{in} = 0$:

$$D_{min} = 0$$

This derivation is the *backbone* of all microcontroller ADC programming.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — ADC Conversion

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question Type):**

A 10-bit ADC has a reference voltage of **3.3 V**. The analog input voltage is **1.25 V**. Calculate:
1. The step size (resolution) of the ADC.
2. The decimal digital output code.
3. The percentage of full-scale represented by the input.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$n = 10, \quad V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}, \quad V_{in} = 1.25 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the step size:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{2^{10}} = \frac{3.3}{1024}$$

$$\Delta V = 0.003223 \text{ V} \approx 3.223 \text{ mV}$$

This means the ADC can distinguish voltage changes as small as **3.223 mV**.

**Step 3 — Compute the decimal output code:**

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.25 \cdot 1024}{3.3} \right\rfloor$$

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{1280}{3.3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 387.878... \right\rfloor$$

$$D = 387$$

**Step 4 — Convert to binary (KTU examiners love this):**

$$387_{10} = 256 + 128 + 2 + 1 = 2^8 + 2^7 + 2^1 + 2^0$$

$$387_{10} = 110000011_{2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute percentage of full-scale:**

$$\% \text{ FS} = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 100 = \frac{1.25}{3.3} \times 100 = 37.88\%$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — DAC Reconstruction

**Problem Statement:**

A 8-bit DAC receives a digital input code of **0xC0** (hexadecimal). $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V. Find the analog output voltage.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert hex to decimal:**

$$0xC0 = (12 \times 16) + 0 = 192$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DAC formula:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{192 \cdot 5.0}{2^8} = \frac{960}{256}$$

$$V_{out} = 3.75 \text{ V}$$

This represents $\frac{192}{255} \times 100 = 75.29\%$ of full-scale.

## 3.4 Sensor Sensitivity Derivation

**Sensitivity** $S$ of a sensor is defined as the ratio of the change in output to the change in the input measurand:

$$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

where $y$ is the electrical output (V or mA) and $x$ is the physical input (°C, lux, N, etc.).

**Example — LM35 Temperature Sensor:**

The LM35 datasheet specifies $S = 10 \text{ mV/°C}$ with $V_{ref} = 5$ V on a 10-bit ADC.

For a temperature of $T = 50$ °C:

$$V_{out} = S \cdot T = 0.010 \cdot 50 = 0.50 \text{ V}$$

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{0.50 \cdot 1024}{5.0} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 102.4 \right\rfloor = 102$$

$$\text{Binary: } 102_{10} = 01100110_2$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Decision Table

This matrix helps in selecting the right sensor for a typical KTU-style design problem:

| Application | Physical Quantity | Recommended Sensor | Output Type | MCU Interface |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| Room thermometer | Temperature | LM35 / DS18B20 | Analog / Digital | ADC / 1-Wire |
| Light-follower robot | Light intensity | LDR (GL5528) | Analog resistance | ADC via voltage divider |
| Object detection (short range) | Distance | IR proximity sensor | Analog / Digital | ADC / GPIO |
| Precise distance measurement | Distance | HC-SR04 Ultrasonic | Digital pulse | GPIO with timer |
| Motion / orientation | Acceleration | MPU6050 | Digital (I²C) | I²C bus |
| Gas leak detector | Gas concentration | MQ-2 / MQ-135 | Analog resistance | ADC |
| Soil moisture | Water content | Resistive probe | Analog resistance | ADC |
| Heart rate | Pulse | IR reflectance sensor | Analog | ADC + filtering |
| Position sensing | Angle | Rotary encoder / Potentiometer | Digital pulses / Analog | Timer / ADC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete **Sense → Process → Actuate** signal chain in an embedded system, including all signal-conditioning stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env[Physical Environment] -->|stimulus| sensorBlock
    subgraph sensorBlock[Sensor Stage]
        s1[Sensing Element] -->|raw signal| s2[Transduction Mechanism]
        s2 -->|low-level electrical signal| s3[Sensor Output]
    end
    s3 -->|millivolts, noisy| scBlock
    subgraph scBlock[Signal Conditioning Stage]
        sc1[Amplifier Op-Amp] -->|amplified| sc2[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
        sc2 -->|clean bandlimited| sc3[Level Shifter to 0 to 3V3]
    end
    sc3 -->|conditioned analog| adcBlock
    subgraph adcBlock[ADC Conversion Stage]
        a1[Sample and Hold] -->|held voltage| a2[Quantizer N-Bit]
        a2 -->|digital code D| a3[Encoder to Binary]
    end
    a3 -->|parallel or serial bus| mcuBlock
    subgraph mcuBlock[Processing Stage]
        m1[Microcontroller CPU] -->|executes algorithm| m2[Firmware Logic]
        m2 -->|control decision| m3[Output Decision Register]
    end
    m3 -->|control code| dacBlock
    subgraph dacBlock[DAC or PWM Stage]
        d1[PWM Generator] -->|pulse train| d2[Low Pass Filter]
        d2 -->|reconstructed analog| d3[Power Amplifier]
    end
    d3 -->|drive signal| actBlock
    subgraph actBlock[Actuator Stage]
        ac1[Actuator Driver] -->|actuation energy| ac2[Physical Action Output]
    end
    ac2 -->|physical effect| physicalOutput[Motor Motion, LED Light, Buzzer Sound, Heat]
```

## 4.2 Sensor Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensors[Sensor Classification] --> power[By Power Source]
    sensors --> output[By Output Type]
    sensors --> measurand[By Measurand Type]
    power --> active[Active Self Generating]
    power --> passive[Passive Externally Excited]
    active --> actEx1[Thermocouple]
    active --> actEx2[Piezoelectric Sensor]
    active --> actEx3[Photovoltaic Cell]
    passive --> pasEx1[Thermistor]
    passive --> pasEx2[LDR Photoresistor]
    passive --> pasEx3[Potentiometer]
    output --> analog[Analog Continuous Output]
    output --> digital[Digital Pulse or Serial Output]
    analog --> anEx1[LM35 Temperature]
    analog --> anEx2[MPX Pressure Sensor]
    digital --> diEx1[DHT11 Humidity]
    digital --> diEx2[DS18B20 Temperature]
    digital --> diEx3[MPU6050 IMU I2C]
    measurand --> thermal[Thermal]
    measurand --> mechanical[Mechanical]
    measurand --> optical[Optical]
    measurand --> chemical[Chemical]
    measurand --> magnetic[Magnetic]
    thermal --> thEx1[Thermistor, RTD, Thermocouple]
    mechanical --> meEx1[Strain Gauge, Accelerometer, Gyro]
    optical --> opEx1[LDR, Photodiode, IR Sensor]
    chemical --> chEx1[MQ Gas Sensor, pH Probe]
    magnetic --> mgEx1[Halleffect Sensor, Reed Switch]
```

## 4.3 Actuator Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    actuators[Actuator Classification] --> motion[Motion Type]
    actuators --> energy[Energy Type]
    motion --> rotary[Rotary Motion]
    motion --> linear[Linear Motion]
    rotary --> rotEx1[DC Motor]
    rotary --> rotEx2[Stepper Motor]
    rotary --> rotEx3[Servo Motor]
    linear --> linEx1[Solenoid]
    linear --> linEx2[Linear Actuator]
    energy --> elec[Electromagnetic]
    energy --> piezo[Piezoelectric]
    energy --> thermal[Thermal]
    energy --> fluidic[Hydraulic or Pneumatic]
    elec --> elEx1[Relay, Speaker, DC Motor]
    piezo --> piEx1[Piezo Buzzer]
    thermal --> thEx1[Heater, Peltier Cooler]
    fluidic --> flEx1[Pneumatic Cylinder]
```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Embedded Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference Setpoint] -->|desired value| ctrl[Controller MCU]
    ctrl -->|command signal| plant[Actuator and Plant]
    plant -->|physical action| proc[Physical Process]
    proc -->|physical effect| fbSensor[Feedback Sensor]
    fbSensor -->|measured value| sigCond[Signal Conditioning]
    sigCond -->|conditioned analog| adcFB[ADC]
    adcFB -->|digital feedback| ctrl
    ctrl -->|error driven output| plant
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> The closed-loop diagram above represents the architecture of nearly every modern embedded control system — from a **cruise controller** in a car (where the feedback sensor is a wheel-speed sensor) to a **smart thermostat** (where the feedback sensor is a thermistor). The MCU continuously computes the *error* = *setpoint* − *measured value* and drives the actuator to minimize that error. This is the foundation of **PID control**, which is covered in detail in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between active and passive sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Power source | Self-generating; no external excitation needed | Requires external excitation voltage/current |
| Working principle | Converts physical energy directly to electrical energy | Modifies an externally supplied electrical signal |
| Example 1 | Thermocouple (Seebeck effect) | Thermistor |
| Example 2 | Piezoelectric sensor | LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) |

*(Full 3 marks awarded for clear two-point differentiation and one example each.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is an ADC? Why is signal conditioning necessary before feeding a sensor output to an MCU?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is an electronic circuit that converts a continuous analog voltage into a discrete digital code that can be processed by a microcontroller.

Signal conditioning is necessary because raw sensor outputs are typically:
1. **Too small in amplitude** (millivolts) — must be amplified to ADC input range.
2. **Noisy** — must be filtered to satisfy the Nyquist criterion and prevent aliasing.
3. **Off-scale or offset** — must be level-shifted to match 0–V_ref range of the ADC.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for ADC definition, 2 marks for the three reasons above.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the classification of sensors in detail with suitable examples for each category. Discuss the working principle of a thermistor and an LDR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8-bit ADC has a reference voltage of 5 V. The analog input voltage is 2.4 V. Calculate the step size, the decimal digital output code, and the binary equivalent. Also find the percentage of full-scale. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Classification of sensors (3 marks):**

Sensors can be classified based on multiple criteria:

**(i) By Power Source:**
- **Active sensors:** Self-generating. Examples: Thermocouple, piezoelectric sensor, photovoltaic cell.
- **Passive sensors:** Require external excitation. Examples: Thermistor, LDR, strain gauge, potentiometer.

**(ii) By Output Type:**
- **Analog sensors:** Output is a continuous voltage/current proportional to measurand. Example: LM35.
- **Digital sensors:** Output is a digital code, pulse train, or serial data. Example: DS18B20 (1-Wire), DHT11 (single-wire protocol).

**(iii) By Measurand:**
- Temperature (Thermistor, RTD, Thermocouple, LM35)
- Light (LDR, Photodiode, Phototransistor)
- Pressure (Strain gauge, piezoelectric pressure sensor)
- Motion (Accelerometer, Gyroscope)
- Chemical (Gas sensor, pH sensor)

**Working principle of a Thermistor (2 marks):**

A thermistor is a *passive* temperature-sensitive resistor made of semiconductor material. Its resistance changes significantly with temperature. There are two types:

- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **decreases** as temperature increases.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **increases** as temperature increases.

The resistance-temperature relationship for an NTC thermistor is:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

where $R_0$ is resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually 25 °C = 298.15 K), $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, and $B$ is the beta constant (typically 3000–5000 K).

The thermistor is typically connected in a **voltage divider** with a fixed resistor $R$, and the output voltage is fed to an ADC.

**Working principle of an LDR (2 marks):**

A Light Dependent Resistor (LDR), also called a photoresistor, is a passive sensor whose resistance changes with incident light intensity.

- In **darkness**, resistance is very high (MΩ range).
- In **bright light**, resistance is very low (a few hundred ohms).

The material used is **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)**. When photons strike the CdS surface, electrons gain energy and jump from the valence band to the conduction band, increasing conductivity. A voltage divider with a fixed resistor converts this resistance change into a voltage that an ADC can read.

#### Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**Given:** $n = 8$, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $V_{in} = 2.4$ V

**Step 1 — Step size (2 marks):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V} \approx 19.53 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 — Decimal output code (2 marks):**

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2.4 \cdot 256}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{614.4}{5} \right\rfloor$$

$$D = \left\lfloor 122.88 \right\rfloor = 122$$

**Step 3 — Binary conversion (2 marks):**

$$122_{10} = 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 2 = 2^6 + 2^5 + 2^4 + 2^3 + 2^1$$

$$122_{10} = 01111010_2$$

**Step 4 — Percentage of full-scale (1 mark):**

$$\% \text{ FS} = \frac{2.4}{5} \times 100 = 48\%$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the signal chain of a typical embedded measurement system from physical quantity to digital display. Identify and justify each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a DC motor and a stepper motor as actuators. Compare them with respect to precision, control complexity, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Block diagram and explanation (5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation):**

The typical signal chain of an embedded measurement system contains the following stages in sequence:

**1. Sensor:** Detects the physical quantity (temperature, pressure, light, etc.) and converts it into a (usually small) electrical signal. Example: LM35 for temperature.

**2. Signal Conditioning Stage:**
- **Amplifier:** Boosts the weak sensor signal (often a few mV) to a usable voltage range.
- **Filter:** A low-pass anti-aliasing filter (typically RC or active op-amp filter) removes high-frequency noise. The cutoff frequency must satisfy $f_c \leq f_s / 2$ (Nyquist criterion).
- **Level Shifter:** Adjusts the voltage range to match the ADC input (e.g., 0–3.3 V for most modern MCUs).

**3. Sample-and-Hold:** Holds the analog voltage constant during the conversion process to avoid errors due to input fluctuations.

**4. ADC:** Quantizes the analog voltage into a discrete $n$-bit digital code. Resolution $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$.

**5. Microcontroller:** Executes the firmware algorithm — performs linearization, calibration, computation, and decision-making.

**6. Display / Communication:** Outputs the result via LCD, 7-segment display, UART, I²C, SPI, or wireless (Bluetooth/Wi-Fi) to a remote receiver.

**Justification (2 marks):** Each block is necessary because raw sensor signals are weak, noisy, and incompatible with digital logic. The amplifier boosts them, the filter prevents aliasing, the ADC digitizes them, and the MCU processes them into meaningful engineering units.

#### Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**DC Motor Working (3 marks):**

A DC motor consists of a stator (permanent magnet or field winding) and a rotor (armature winding). When DC current is supplied to the armature through brushes and a commutator, it experiences a force according to:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$

This force produces a torque:

$$\tau = K_t \cdot I_a$$

where $K_t$ is the torque constant and $I_a$ is the armature current. The rotor rotates continuously as long as power is supplied. Speed is approximately proportional to the applied voltage, making it easy to control via PWM (Pulse Width Modulation). DC motors cannot hold a precise position without a feedback encoder.

**Stepper Motor Working (3 marks):**

A stepper motor has multiple stator windings (phases) and a toothed rotor (permanent magnet or variable reluctance). Energizing the windings in a specific sequence produces a magnetic field that pulls the rotor to discrete angular positions called *steps*. Common step angles are 1.8° (200 steps/rev) and 0.9° (400 steps/rev).

Stepper motors are driven by a sequence of digital pulses; each pulse advances the rotor by one step. They can *hold* a position without feedback, making them open-loop precise.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | DC Motor | Stepper Motor |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| Precision | Low (open loop); needs encoder for precision | High (open loop, ~1.8° per step) |
| Control Complexity | Simple (PWM + H-bridge) | Moderate (sequence driver + driver IC) |
| Holding Torque at Rest | None (free-wheel) | High (locks at last step) |
| Typical Applications | Fans, wheels, pumps, conveyor belts | CNC, 3D printers, robotics, camera gimbals |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units in numerical answers:** Writing "step size = 0.0195" without "Volts" loses 0.5 marks immediately.
> 2. **Applying wrong ADC formula:** Some students write $D = V_{in} / \Delta V$ (which is correct) but mistakenly write $D = V_{in} \cdot \Delta V$ — always divide, never multiply.
> 3. **Confusing sensor and transducer:** A *transducer* is the umbrella term; a *sensor* is an input transducer; an *actuator* is an output transducer. Mixing up these definitions costs a full 2-mark definition question.
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows showing signal flow:** Examiners expect clearly labeled arrows (e.g., "analog voltage", "digital code", "control signal"). An unlabeled block diagram earns only partial credit.
> 5. **Skipping the floor function in ADC:** The digital output must be an integer; writing $D = 122.88$ instead of $D = 122$ loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing NTC and PTC thermistor behavior:** NTC = resistance falls with temperature; PTC = resistance rises with temperature. This is a tested concept.
> 7. **Omitting the Nyquist criterion when discussing signal conditioning:** Always mention that filtering must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to prevent aliasing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sensor:** Input device that converts a physical stimulus into an electrical signal.
- **Actuator:** Output device that converts an electrical signal into a physical action.
- **Active sensor:** Self-generating (e.g., thermocouple, piezoelectric). No external power needed.
- **Passive sensor:** Requires external excitation (e.g., thermistor, LDR, strain gauge).
- **Analog sensor** → needs **ADC** → MCU. **Digital sensor** → directly to MCU via GPIO / I²C / SPI / UART.
- **ADC step size:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$. A 10-bit ADC with 3.3 V reference has step size ≈ 3.22 mV.
- **DAC output:** $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{ref} / 2^n$.
- **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing. Anti-aliasing filter is mandatory.
- **Signal conditioning chain:** Amplify → Filter → Level Shift → Sample-and-Hold → ADC.
- **Thermistor:** NTC = resistance ↓ with temperature; PTC = resistance ↑ with temperature. Beta equation: $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$.
- **LDR (CdS):** Resistance ↓ as light intensity ↑. Used in automatic street lights.
- **Thermocouple:** Active sensor based on Seebeck effect; output ≈ 40 µV/°C for K-type.
- **DC Motor:** Continuous rotation, speed ∝ voltage, controlled via PWM.
- **Stepper Motor:** Discrete angular steps, open-loop position control, holds position at rest.
- **Servo Motor:** Closed-loop position control (0°–180°) using internal potentiometer feedback.
- **Relay:** Electromagnetic switch for switching AC/high-power loads from a low-power MCU pin.
- **Sense–Process–Actuate loop** is the universal paradigm of every embedded system.
- **Closed-loop control** uses feedback sensors to continuously correct errors; the basis of PID control.
- Always show **units** in numerical answers and **arrows + labels** in block diagrams to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RTOS Based Embedded System Design: Operating System Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET723 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RTOS Based Embedded System Design: Operating System Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Operating System (OS) in an Embedded Context?

An **Operating System (OS)** is system software that acts as an interface between the user/application program and the underlying hardware. In an **embedded system**, the OS manages hardware resources (CPU, memory, I/O devices) and provides services such as task scheduling, inter-task communication, interrupt handling, and memory management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> In embedded systems, the OS is typically a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** — a specialized operating system designed to handle tasks with strict **timing constraints** (deadlines), where correctness depends not only on the logical result but also on the **time** at which the result is produced.

---

## 1.2 Why do we need an OS in Embedded Systems?

In simple embedded applications (e.g., a digital thermometer), a **super-loop** or **bare-metal** approach is sufficient:

```text
while(1) {
    read_sensor();
    process_data();
    display_result();
}
```

However, when an embedded system must handle **multiple concurrent activities** (e.g., a washing machine controlling motor, water level sensor, user interface, and buzzer simultaneously), managing them with a super-loop becomes impractical. This is where an **RTOS** is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen:
> - **No OS (Bare-metal):** One chef cooks one dish at a time. Dishes are queued. If a dish needs urgent attention (a customer is waiting), the chef cannot interrupt — the customer must wait.
> - **With RTOS:** Multiple chefs work in parallel. A head chef (the **scheduler**) assigns tasks based on priority and deadlines. Urgent orders (high-priority tasks) are handled immediately, ensuring every dish is served on time.
> - The **head chef = Scheduler**, **cooks = Tasks**, **shared tools (stove, oven) = Shared Resources**, **recipes = Tasks Code**, **service contract = Deadlines**.

---

## 1.3 General Purpose OS (GPOS) vs Real-Time OS (RTOS)

| Feature | **GPOS** (e.g., Windows, Linux) | **RTOS** (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Throughput / Fairness | Determinism / Timeliness |
| Scheduling | Time-sharing, fair | Priority-based, preemptive |
| Response Time | Variable (best-effort) | Bounded (worst-case guaranteed) |
| Kernel Size | Large (MBs) | Small (KBs) |
| Latency | Non-deterministic | Microsecond-level deterministic |
| Use Case | Desktops, servers | Embedded controllers, IoT, automotive |
| Deadline Awareness | No | **Yes — Hard / Firm / Soft** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Terminology — Three Classes of Real-Time Systems:**
> 1. **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline is a **system failure** (e.g., airbag deployment, anti-lock braking).
> 2. **Firm Real-Time:** Late results are useless but not catastrophic (e.g., video frame rendering — dropping a frame is acceptable).
> 3. **Soft Real-Time:** Late results degrade quality but system continues (e.g., audio playback, keypad response).

---

## 1.4 Core Components of an RTOS Kernel

An RTOS kernel typically provides these fundamental services:

1. **Task/Thread Management** — Creation, deletion, scheduling of tasks
2. **Scheduler** — Decides which task runs at any given instant
3. **Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** — Mechanisms like queues, mailboxes, pipes
4. **Synchronization Primitives** — Semaphores, mutexes, event flags
5. **Memory Management** — Static allocation, dynamic pools
6. **Interrupt Handling** — Coordination between ISRs and tasks
7. **Time Management** — System tick, delays, timeouts

---

## 1.5 Important Terminology Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions for KTU:**
> - **Task:** An independent thread of execution with its own stack and context.
> - **Kernel:** The core of the OS that runs in privileged mode and provides essential services.
> - **Scheduler:** The component of the kernel that decides task execution order.
> - **Context Switch:** Saving the state of a running task and loading the state of another — the foundation of multitasking.
> - **Determinism:** The guarantee that the system will respond within a known, bounded time.
> - **Jitter:** The variation in the time between successive executions of a periodic task.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RTOS Kernel — Architecture Overview

The **kernel** is the heart of the RTOS. It runs continuously and is invoked whenever a task makes a system call, an interrupt occurs, or a scheduling decision is required.

There are two primary architectural models:

### A. Monolithic Kernel
- All OS services (scheduling, file system, network, drivers) run in **kernel space**.
- Faster communication between components (no mode switches).
- Example: Traditional RTOS like **VxWorks**.

### B. Microkernel
- Only essential services (scheduler, IPC) run in kernel space.
- Other services run as user-space tasks.
- More modular and reliable; slightly higher overhead.
- Example: **QNX Neutrino**, **MINIX**.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, the focus is on understanding how the scheduler, tasks, and IPC interact — the architecture type is secondary.

---

## 2.2 Task States and Transitions

A task in an RTOS exists in one of several states. The most common state diagram includes:

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| **Ready** | Task is ready to run but waiting for CPU |
| **Running** | Task is currently executing on the CPU |
| **Blocked / Waiting** | Task is waiting for an event (semaphore, queue, delay) |
| **Suspended** | Task is explicitly suspended by another task or itself |
| **Terminated / Deleted** | Task has finished execution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition Rules:**
> - `Ready → Running`: When scheduler dispatches it
> - `Running → Ready`: When preempted by a higher-priority task
> - `Running → Blocked`: When it requests a resource that is unavailable
> - `Blocked → Ready`: When the awaited event occurs
> - Any state → Suspended: Explicit API call

---

## 2.3 Scheduling Algorithms — The Core of RTOS

### 2.3.1 Preemptive Priority-Based Scheduling (Most Common in RTOS)

Each task has a **static or dynamic priority**. The scheduler always runs the **highest-priority Ready task**. If a higher-priority task becomes Ready, it **immediately preempts** the currently running task.

**Advantages:**
- Excellent real-time response for high-priority tasks
- Predictable behavior

**Disadvantages:**
- **Priority Inversion** problem (covered below)
- Lower-priority tasks may starve (solution: aging)

### 2.3.2 Round-Robin Scheduling

Used when multiple tasks share the **same priority level**. Each task gets a **time slice (quantum)** of the CPU. When the quantum expires, the scheduler moves to the next ready task of equal priority.

```
Task A | Task B | Task C | Task A | Task B | Task C | ...
|<--- Quantum Q --->|
```

### 2.3.3 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)

A **fixed-priority preemptive** algorithm used for **periodic tasks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rate Monotonic Priority Assignment Rule:**
> The task with the **shorter period (higher frequency)** is assigned the **higher priority**.

### 2.3.4 Earliest Deadline First (EDF)

A **dynamic-priority** algorithm. The task whose deadline is **closest** is given the highest priority at every scheduling point. Optimal for uniprocessor systems but has higher runtime overhead.

---

## 2.4 The Critical Concept of Priority Inversion

**Priority Inversion** occurs when a **higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task**, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of two tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic Analogy — The Mars Pathfinder Bug (1997):**
> A low-priority meteorological task held a shared bus semaphore. A medium-priority communication task ran, preventing the low-priority task from releasing the semaphore. The high-priority bus management task was starved — causing system resets. NASA solved it by enabling **priority inheritance** in VxWorks.

### Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

When a high-priority task waits for a resource held by a lower-priority task, the lower-priority task **temporarily inherits** the higher priority until it releases the resource.

---

## 2.5 Inter-Task Communication (ITC) Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Use Case | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Semaphore (Binary)** | Mutual exclusion, signaling | No ownership concept |
| **Mutex** | Mutual exclusion | Has ownership (task that locks must unlock) |
| **Counting Semaphore** | Resource counting (e.g., 3 serial ports) | Tracks count |
| **Message Queue** | Passing data between tasks | FIFO buffer of fixed-size messages |
| **Mailbox** | Single-message passing | Typically 1-slot queue |
| **Event Flags** | Synchronizing on multiple conditions | Bitwise operations |
| **Pipes** | Stream-oriented byte transfer | For larger data |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for RTOS Calculations:**

| Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{response} = T_{context\_switch} + T_{ISR} + T_{service}$ | Total interrupt response time | $\mu s$ |
| $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | **CPU Utilization** for $n$ tasks ($C_i$ = execution time, $T_i$ = period) | dimensionless |
| $U_{bound} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | **Liu & Layland Bound** for RMS schedulability | dimensionless |
| $Jitter = T_{actual} - T_{ideal}$ | Variation in periodic execution timing | $\mu s$ |
| $T_{deadline} = T_{arrival} + T_{latency\_max}$ | Hard real-time deadline calculation | $\mu s$ |
| $T_{worst\_case} = T_{preemption\_max} + T_{execution\_max}$ | Worst-case task completion time | $\mu s$ |
| $\text{Throughput} = \frac{N_{tasks}}{T_{total}}$ | Number of tasks completed per unit time | tasks/sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Liu & Layland Theorem (1973):**
> For $n$ periodic tasks scheduled under RMS on a uniprocessor, a **necessary** (but not sufficient) condition for schedulability is:
> $$U \le n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$
> - For $n = 1$: $U_{bound} = 1.0$
> - For $n = 2$: $U_{bound} \approx 0.828$
> - For $n = 3$: $U_{bound} \approx 0.779$
> - As $n \to \infty$: $U_{bound} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$
>
> A **sufficient condition** (exact test for harmonic periods) is $U \le 1.0$.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

RTOS concepts are foundational in:
- **Automotive ECU systems** (AUTOSAR on OSEK RTOS)
- **Aerospace flight control** (RTEMS, VxWorks)
- **Medical devices** (pacemakers, infusion pumps)
- **Industrial robotics and PLCs**
- **Consumer IoT** (Amazon FreeRTOS on ESP32)
- **Mobile baseband processors** (DSP RTOS)
