> 1. **Do NOT confuse "priority" semantics** between µC/OS-II (lower numerical value = higher priority) and FreeRTOS (higher numerical value = higher priority). State the convention explicitly in your answer.
> 2. **In RMS problems**, students often forget to apply the *iterative* response-time test and only use the utilization bound. The bound is a *sufficient* test, not *necessary*. If $U$ exceeds the bound, you must use the response-time equation to verify.
> 3. **In priority inversion answers**, marks are lost if you do not show the *temporal sequence* (a timing diagram or numbered steps) demonstrating how the medium-priority task delays the high-priority one. Verbal explanation alone fetches only partial credit.
> 4. **In TCB diagrams**, the examiner expects a labelled block diagram, not a code listing. Sketch the fields as boxes.
> 5. **Stack-size derivations** require the safety margin — omitting it loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Tasks**

- [x] A **task** is the basic unit of execution in an RTOS; it owns a stack and a TCB.
- [x] A task exists in one of **five states**: Dormant, Ready, Running, Waiting, Suspended.
- [x] The **Task Control Block (TCB)** stores the stack pointer, priority, status, delay count, event pointers, and linked-list pointers.
- [x] **Pre-emptive priority scheduling** is the dominant RTOS policy; the highest-priority Ready task always runs.
- [x] **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is optimal among static-priority algorithms: shorter period → higher priority.
- [x] **Liu & Layland bound** for RMS: $U = \sum C_i/T_i \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- [x] **Exact schedulability test (RMS):** iterative response-time formula $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$, iterate until convergence; require $R_i \le D_i$.
- [x] **Priority Inversion** is solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.
- [x] **Task communication primitives:** semaphores (binary/counting), mutexes, mailboxes, message queues, event flags.
- [x] **Context switch time** $T_{cs}$ must be budgeted into the worst-case execution time.
- [x] **Stack size** must include: nested call frames + local variables + ISR worst case + hardware-pushed registers + safety margin (≥ 25%).
- [x] µC/OS-II convention: **lower numerical priority value = higher priority**; FreeRTOS: **higher numerical value = higher priority**.
- [x] The **Idle Task** runs at the lowest priority when no other task is Ready — it is where the OS typically performs background housekeeping or low-power tickless idle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processes and Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET723 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processes and Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Process?

A **Process** is a program in execution. It is the fundamental unit of work in an operating system, representing an independent instance of a running application that possesses its own dedicated memory space, system resources, and execution context.

In the context of **Embedded Systems**, a process is the abstraction used by the OS (e.g., Embedded Linux, RTOS) to manage the execution of a loaded firmware application, system daemon (like `systemd`), or user-space service on the target hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Strict):**
> A *Process* is defined as an executing program, comprising the program code (text section), its current activity represented by the **Program Counter (PC)**, the contents of the processor's **registers**, a **process stack**, a **data section** (containing global variables), and a **heap** (for dynamic memory allocation).

### 1.2 What is a Thread?

A **Thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution that is schedulable by the operating system kernel. A thread is often called a "lightweight process" because it shares most of its resources (code, data, open files) with other threads belonging to the same process but maintains its own private execution stack, register set, and thread-local storage (TLS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** A *Process* owns resources; a *Thread* is the entity that gets scheduled on the CPU. A process must contain at least one thread (the main thread) to execute.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large industrial bakery (the Process)**:
- The **Bakery Building** (Code, Data, Storage) belongs to one company — this is the **Process**. No other company can use its ovens, dough mixers, or store rooms.
- The **Bakers (Threads)** are the workers inside. Multiple bakers work in the same bakery, share the same ovens, share the same flour storage, but each baker has their own personal apron, tools tray, and recipe card (register set and stack).
- If the bakery shuts down, all bakers stop. If one baker leaves, the bakery keeps running. This is the **Process vs. Thread** relationship.

In an **Embedded RTOS** (like FreeRTOS, VxWorks, or ThreadX on an ARM Cortex-M4):
- Each sensor-reading task, motor-control loop, and communication handler is typically a **thread** (called a "task" in RTOS terminology).
- The entire firmware is a single **process** sharing the microcontroller's RAM and peripherals.

### 1.4 Standard Process Components (KTU Highlight)

Every process in a KTU-recommended OS model (POSIX/Linux) contains these standard components:

| Component | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Text Section** | Executable machine code (read-only) |
| **Data Section** | Initialized global & static variables |
| **BSS Section** | Uninitialized global variables (Block Started by Symbol) |
| **Heap** | Dynamic memory (grows upward, `malloc`/`free`) |
| **Stack** | Function calls, local variables (grows downward) |
| **PCB (Process Control Block)** | Kernel data structure holding PID, state, registers, MMU tables |

> [!NOTE]
> The **PCB** is the kernel's "identity card" for the process. Without it, the scheduler cannot resume the process after a context switch.

## 1.5 Process States (The KTU Five-State Model)

For KTU 2024 ESE, you must memorize the **five-state process model**:

1. **New** — Process is being created.
2. **Ready** — Process is waiting to be assigned to a CPU.
3. **Running** — Instructions are being executed by the CPU.
4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Process cannot run until some event occurs (I/O completion, signal).
5. **Exit/Terminated** — Process has finished execution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process vs. Thread — Detailed Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Focus:** This is the **#1 most asked comparison** in Module 3. Memorize the table below verbatim.

| Parameter | Process | Thread |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Program in execution | Sub-division of a process (lightweight) |
| **Memory Space** | Independent virtual address space | Shared within the same process |
| **Creation Cost** | High (requires separate PCB, MMU setup) | Low (shares parent's resources) |
| **Context Switch** | Expensive (TLB flush, page table switch) | Cheap (only register/stack swap) |
| **Communication** | IPC (pipes, message queues, sockets) | Shared memory (variables), mutexes, semaphores |
| **Failure Isolation** | One process crash ≠ others fail | One thread crash can kill entire process |
| **Resource Ownership** | Owns resources | Borrows resources from process |
| **Kernel Awareness** | Always a kernel-level entity | Can be user-level or kernel-level |

## 2.2 Types of Threads

### 2.2.1 User-Level Threads (ULT)
- Managed entirely by a **thread library** in user space (e.g., GNU `pthreads` user-mode, `Gothreads`).
- The kernel is **unaware** of their existence.
- **Pros:** Fast context switch (no kernel transition), customizable scheduling.
- **Cons:** If one ULT makes a blocking system call, the **entire process blocks** (no parallelism on multi-core). This is the classic "blocking system call problem".

### 2.2.2 Kernel-Level Threads (KLT)
- Managed **directly by the OS kernel** (e.g., Windows threads, Linux `clone()` system call, RTOS tasks).
- The scheduler treats each thread as an independent schedulable entity.
- **Pros:** True parallelism on multi-core; blocking I/O on one thread does not freeze others.
- **Cons:** Slower context switch (kernel transition via syscall).

### 2.2.3 Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many Models

| Model | Mapping | Used In |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Many-to-One** | Many ULTs → One KLT | Old Solaris Green Threads |
| **One-to-One** | Each ULT → One KLT | Linux, Windows (modern pthreads) |
| **Many-to-Many** | m ULTs → n KLTs (m ≥ n) | Solaris, modern hybrid schedulers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded RTOS Note:** Real-time systems (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) use the **One-to-One** model where every thread is a kernel-level entity — this guarantees deterministic scheduling and real-time response.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **board-exam-critical formulas**. KTU often tests a numerical on Amdahl's Law or context-switch overhead in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).

### 2.3.1 Amdahl's Law (Speedup from Parallelism)

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$

Where:
- $S$ = Overall speedup factor
- $P$ = Parallelizable fraction of the workload ($0 \le P \le 1$)
- $N$ = Number of processing units (cores/threads)
- $(1 - P)$ = Serial fraction that **cannot** be parallelized

### 2.3.2 Maximum Theoretical Speedup (as $N \to \infty$)

$$S_{max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}} = \frac{1}{1 - P}$$

### 2.3.3 Context Switch Overhead

$$T_{eff} = T_{work} + T_{cs}$$

Where:
- $T_{eff}$ = Effective execution time of a thread slice
- $T_{work}$ = Useful CPU work performed
- $T_{cs}$ = Context switch overhead (save + restore registers, cache pollution, TLB flush)

### 2.3.4 CPU Utilization in Multithreaded System

$$U_{cpu} = \frac{N \cdot \lambda \cdot E[S]}{1 + N \cdot \lambda \cdot E[S]}$$

This is the **M/M/1 queueing model utilization** for an N-server system where $\lambda$ is the thread arrival rate and $E[S]$ is the mean service time. Embedded engineers use this to size the number of worker threads for an IoT gateway.

### 2.3.5 Thread Stack Size Estimation (Embedded Critical)

$$S_{stack} = S_{base} + N_{call} \cdot S_{frame} + S_{int} + S_{margin}$$

Where:
- $S_{base}$ = Base stack for local variables (~512 bytes typical for ARM Cortex-M)
- $N_{call}$ = Maximum nested function calls
- $S_{frame}$ = Stack frame per call (~64–128 bytes for ARM)
- $S_{int}$ = Interrupt stack headroom
- $S_{margin}$ = Safety margin (20–30%)

## 2.4 Why Threads Matter in Embedded Systems

In an embedded device, threads (often called "tasks") allow **concurrent** handling of:
- Sensor acquisition (ADC, I2C polling)
- Communication stacks (TCP/IP on ESP32, CAN bus on automotive ECUs)
- User interface (LCD refresh, button debounce)
- Control loops (PID at fixed 1 kHz)

Without threads, a single sensor read would freeze the entire system until it completes — unacceptable for hard real-time systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Amdahl's Law from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** A KTU 2024 numerical states: *"90% of an embedded image-processing application can be parallelized across 8 ARM Cortex-A53 cores. Compute the speedup. If the serial fraction is increased to 20%, what happens to the speedup?"*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Parallel fraction $P = 0.90$
- Number of cores $N = 8$
- Serial fraction $(1 - P) = 0.10$

**Step 1: Identify the original execution time.**

Let the original single-core execution time be $T_1 = 1$ second (normalized).

**Step 2: Decompose execution into serial and parallel portions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= T_{serial} + T_{parallel} \\
    &= (1 - P) \cdot T_1 + P \cdot T_1 \\
    &= 0.10 \cdot 1 + 0.90 \cdot 1 \\
    &= 1.00 \text{ second}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute new execution time with $N$ cores.**

The serial portion cannot be parallelized; it takes the same time. The parallel portion is divided equally among $N$ cores.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_N &= (1 - P) \cdot T_1 + \frac{P \cdot T_1}{N} \\
    &= 0.10 \cdot 1 + \frac{0.90 \cdot 1}{8} \\
    &= 0.10 + 0.1125 \\
    &= 0.2125 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute the speedup ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_1}{T_N} = \frac{1}{0.2125} \\
  &\approx 4.706
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Now increase the serial fraction to 20% (i.e., $P = 0.80$).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_N' &= 0.20 \cdot 1 + \frac{0.80 \cdot 1}{8} \\
     &= 0.20 + 0.10 \\
     &= 0.30 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S' &= \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.333
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that doubling the cores from 8 to 16 with $P = 0.90$ would only improve speedup from 4.706 to ~6.41. This is the **diminishing returns** of parallelization. The **serial bottleneck** dominates.

**Step 6: Maximum theoretical speedup (infinite cores).**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \frac{1}{1 - P} \\
        &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} \\
        &= \frac{1}{0.10} = 10
\end{aligned}$$

So even with 1000 cores, the speedup can never exceed **10×** for a 90% parallel workload.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Context Switch Overhead Impact

**Problem Statement:** *"An RTOS schedules 4 threads, each needing a 10 ms time slice. Each context switch costs 100 µs. What fraction of CPU time is wasted on context switching?"*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute total useful work per round-robin cycle.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{work,total} &= N \cdot T_{slice} \\
                &= 4 \cdot 10 \text{ ms} \\
                &= 40 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute total context-switch overhead per cycle.**

There are $N = 4$ switches per round (after each thread leaves, the next is loaded).

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{cs,total} &= N \cdot T_{cs} \\
             &= 4 \cdot 100 \text{ µs} \\
             &= 400 \text{ µs} = 0.4 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the total cycle time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= T_{work,total} + T_{cs,total} \\
          &= 40 + 0.4 \\
          &= 40.4 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute CPU utilization for useful work.**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{cpu} &= \frac{T_{work,total}}{T_{cycle}} \\
        &= \frac{40}{40.4} \\
        &\approx 0.9901 = 99.01\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute overhead percentage.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead} &= 1 - U_{cpu} \\
                &= 1 - 0.9901 \\
                &= 0.0099 = 0.99\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded RTOS Reality:** In a real ARM Cortex-M4 RTOS, context switch can be 1–5 µs. If the time slice were 1 ms, the overhead would rise to ~2%, which is a **significant** cost in a hard real-time system.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thread Stack Sizing

**Problem Statement:** *"Design a stack size for an embedded sensor task with: 4 nested function calls, ARM Cortex-M0 frame of 32 bytes, 16 bytes local variables, 64 bytes interrupt headroom, 30% safety margin."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute base requirements.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{base} &= 16 \text{ bytes (locals)} \\
S_{frame,total} &= N_{call} \cdot S_{frame} = 4 \cdot 32 = 128 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{int} &= 64 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Sum the pre-margin stack requirement.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{raw} &= S_{base} + S_{frame,total} + S_{int} \\
        &= 16 + 128 + 64 \\
        &= 208 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply the 30% safety margin.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{final} &= S_{raw} \cdot (1 + \text{margin}) \\
          &= 208 \cdot 1.30 \\
          &= 270.4 \text{ bytes} \\
          &\approx 272 \text{ bytes (round up to 4-byte alignment)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Practical allocation.**

Allocate **288 bytes** (next multiple of 32) to ensure word alignment on ARM architecture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Stack overflow is the **#1 cause of embedded system crashes**. Always over-allocate and enable stack overflow detection (e.g., FreeRTOS `configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW`).

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — When to Use Processes vs. Threads

| Scenario | Use Process | Use Thread | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sandbox security (IoT)** | ✅ | ❌ | Need fault isolation |
| **Sensor data fusion** | ❌ | ✅ | Tight coupling, shared data |
| **Network server + UI** | ✅ | ❌ | Failure of one should not kill other |
| **PID control loop** | ❌ | ✅ | Determinism, shared state |
| **Multi-tenant cloud VM** | ✅ | ❌ | Hard isolation required |
| **RTOS task on Cortex-M** | ❌ | ✅ | Memory-constrained, no MMU |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Lifecycle — Five-State Diagram (KTU Mandatory)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state "NEW" as new1
    state "READY" as ready1
    state "RUNNING" as run1
    state "BLOCKED" as blk1
    state "EXIT" as exit1
    new1 --> ready1 : Admitted
    ready1 --> run1 : Scheduler Dispatch
    run1 --> exit1 : Process Termination
    run1 --> ready1 : Preempted / Time Slice Expired
    run1 --> blk1 : I/O Request / Event Wait
    blk1 --> ready1 : I/O Completion / Event Signal
```

## 4.2 Process vs. Thread Memory Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PROC1[Process A]
        subgraph T1A[Thread A1]
            S1A[Stack A1]
            R1A[Registers A1]
        end
        subgraph T2A[Thread A2]
            S2A[Stack A2]
            R2A[Registers A2]
        end
        T1A -.- SHARED_A[Shared: Code, Data, Heap, Open Files, PCB]
        T2A -.- SHARED_A
    end
    subgraph PROC2[Process B - Independent]
        subgraph T1B[Thread B1]
            S1B[Stack B1]
            R1B[Registers B1]
        end
        T1B -.- SHARED_B[Private: Code, Data, Heap, Open Files, PCB]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that within Process A, both threads **share** the heap and code segment, but each has its **own** stack and registers. Process B has a **completely separate** memory space — it cannot access Process A's memory without explicit IPC.

## 4.3 Threading Models — One-to-One (Embedded RTOS)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph USER[User Application]
        UT1[ULT Sensor Task]
        UT2[ULT Motor Task]
        UT3[ULT Comms Task]
    end
    subgraph KERNEL[Kernel]
        KT1[KLT Task 1]
        KT2[KLT Task 2]
        KT3[KLT Task 3]
    end
    UT1 -.-> KT1
    UT2 -.-> KT2
    UT3 -.-. KT3
    SCHED[RTOS Scheduler] --> KT1
    SCHED --> KT2
    SCHED --> KT3
    CPU[ARM CPU Cores] --> SCHED
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Context Switch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread A RUNNING] --> B{Timer Interrupt or Syscall}
    B --> C[Save Registers to Thread A PCB]
    C --> D[Update Thread A State to READY]
    D --> E[Invoke Scheduler]
    E --> F{Select Next Thread}
    F --> G[Thread B - Restore Registers from PCB]
    G --> H[Thread B RUNNING]
    H --> I[Thread B Executes Time Slice]
    I --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When drawing this in exams, always include **"Save PCB"**, **"Scheduler Decision"**, and **"Restore PCB"** as three distinct steps. Most students lose marks by skipping the scheduler.

## 4.5 Thread Pool Architecture (Embedded IoT Gateway)

```mermaid
graph TB
    QUEUE[Incoming Request Queue] --> POOL{Thread Pool}
    POOL --> W1[Worker Thread 1]
    POOL --> W2[Worker Thread 2]
    POOL --> W3[Worker Thread 3]
    POOL --> W4[Worker Thread 4]
    W1 --> HW1[Sensor Driver]
    W2 --> HW2[BLE Stack]
    W3 --> HW3[WiFi Stack]
    W4 --> HW4[CAN Bus]
    HW1 --> SHARED[Shared Sensor Data Buffer]
    HW2 --> SHARED
    HW3 --> SHARED
    HW4 --> SHARED
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a process and a thread. Why are threads called "lightweight processes"?**

**Model Answer:**

A **process** is an independent program in execution with its own memory address space, system resources, and at least one thread of control. A **thread** is a subset of a process that shares the parent's code, data, heap, and open file descriptors, but maintains its own stack, registers, and thread-local storage.

Threads are called **lightweight processes** because:
1. **Creation cost** — Creating a thread requires only stack and register allocation (µs), while creating a process requires a full PCB, page tables, and MMU setup (ms).
2. **Context switch** — Thread switching only swaps registers and the stack pointer; no TLB flush or page table switch.
3. **Resource sharing** — Threads automatically share memory; processes need explicit IPC.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the five-state process model with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The five states are:
1. **New** — Process is being created (memory allocation, PCB setup).
2. **Ready** — Process is loaded in main memory and waiting for CPU allocation by the scheduler.
3. **Running** — Process instructions are being executed on the CPU.
4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Process cannot continue until an external event occurs (e.g., I/O completion, semaphore signal).
5. **Exit/Terminated** — Process has finished execution; PCB is deallocated.

**Transitions:**
- New → Ready: *Admitted* (OS admits the process to the ready queue).
- Ready → Running: *Scheduler Dispatch* (process selected for CPU).
- Running → Exit: *Process Termination* (normal/exit syscall or fatal error).
- Running → Ready: *Preemption* (time slice expired, higher-priority process).
- Running → Blocked: *I/O or Event Wait*.
- Blocked → Ready: *I/O Complete or Event Signal*.

*(See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for reference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the **various threading models** used in operating systems. Compare the **Many-to-One**, **One-to-One**, and **Many-to-Many** models with their respective advantages and disadvantages. Mention which model is preferred in embedded RTOS and why. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real-time embedded signal-processing application spends **75%** of its time on a parallelizable DSP routine and **25%** on serial I/O bookkeeping. The system uses **6 processor cores**. Using **Amdahl's Law**, calculate:
  (i) The overall speedup achieved.
  (ii) The maximum theoretical speedup if the number of cores were infinite.
  (iii) The new speedup if the parallel fraction drops to 50% with the same 6 cores. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**1. Many-to-One Model**
- Many user-level threads are mapped to **one** kernel thread.
- Thread management is done in **user space** by a thread library.
- **Concurrency:** Only one thread can access the kernel at a time; multiple threads cannot run in parallel on a multicore system.
- **Drawback:** If one thread makes a blocking system call, the **entire process blocks**.
- **Example:** Early Solaris Green Threads, GNU Portable Threads.

**2. One-to-One Model**
- Each user thread maps to **one** kernel thread.
- Provides **true concurrency** — multiple threads can run in parallel on different cores.
- **Drawback:** Creating a user thread requires creating the corresponding kernel thread, which has overhead. Limits total threads (Linux: typically 1000s).
- **Example:** Linux `NPTL` pthreads, Windows threads, modern FreeRTOS tasks.

**3. Many-to-Many Model**
- Multiplexes $m$ user threads to $n$ kernel threads, where $m \ge n$.
- Developers can create as many user threads as needed; the kernel thread count is bounded by the architecture.
- **Concurrency:** True parallelism (kernel threads run on multiple cores).
- **Drawback:** Complex implementation; scheduler must coordinate two levels.
- **Example:** Solaris, modern hybrid thread libraries (e.g., Go runtime goroutines scheduler).

**Embedded RTOS Preference:**
Real-time operating systems (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, ThreadX, Zephyr) use the **One-to-One model** because:
- **Determinism** — Each thread is independently schedulable with guaranteed latency.
- **Blocking I/O isolation** — A sensor I/O block does not freeze the motor-control task.
- **Priority inheritance** — Can be enforced at the kernel level for mutexes (avoiding priority inversion).
- **No MMU required** — Suits microcontroller-class hardware (Cortex-M).

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:** $P = 0.75$, $1 - P = 0.25$, $N = 6$.

**(i) Speedup with 6 cores:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.25 + \frac{0.75}{6}} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.125} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.375} \\
  &\approx 2.667
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Maximum theoretical speedup ($N \to \infty$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \frac{1}{1 - P} \\
        &= \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) New speedup with $P = 0.50$, $N = 6$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S' &= \frac{1}{0.50 + \frac{0.50}{6}} \\
   &= \frac{1}{0.50 + 0.0833} \\
   &= \frac{1}{0.5833} \\
   &\approx 1.714
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to **normalize $T_1$ to 1** before applying the formula. Always state "$T_1 = 1$ second" explicitly to avoid losing 1 mark. Also, retain **4 decimal places** in intermediate calculations to avoid rounding errors in the final answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Context Switching** in a multithreaded RTOS. Describe the steps involved during a context switch between two threads in an ARM Cortex-M4 system. Discuss how context-switch overhead affects real-time determinism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **stack size** for an embedded IoT task with the following parameters: maximum 6 nested function calls, each function frame = 48 bytes on ARM Cortex-M, local variables = 64 bytes, nested interrupt handlers may use up to 128 bytes, and a safety margin of 25% is required. Show all calculations step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Definition:** Context switching is the procedure of **saving the state of a currently running thread** and **restoring the state of the next scheduled thread** so that execution can resume seamlessly. It is invoked by the OS scheduler on a timer interrupt, blocking I/O, or explicit yield.

**Steps in an ARM Cortex-M4 Context Switch:**

1. **Trigger:** SysTick timer interrupt fires (configurable, e.g., every 1 ms) or a thread calls `vTaskDelay()` / blocked I/O.
2. **Hardware auto-stacking:** ARM Cortex-M4 automatically pushes `R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR` onto the current thread's stack (using PSP — Process Stack Pointer).
3. **Save remaining context:** The PendSV handler explicitly saves `R4–R11` (the callee-saved high registers) to the thread's stack.
4. **Save Stack Pointer:** Write the current PSP value into the thread's **TCB (Thread Control Block)** — equivalent to PCB in RTOS.
5. **Update thread state:** Mark current thread as READY or BLOCKED in the ready list.
6. **Scheduler decision:** Run the scheduling algorithm (e.g., FreeRTOS `vTaskStartScheduler`'s priority-based preemptive scheduler).
7. **Select next thread:** Pop the highest-priority READY thread from the ready list.
8. **Restore Stack Pointer:** Load the selected thread's PSP from its TCB.
9. **Restore context:** Pop `R4–R11` from the new thread's stack.
10. **Hardware auto-unstacking:** On exception return, ARM automatically restores `R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR`.
11. **Resume execution:** The CPU begins executing the new thread exactly where it left off.

**Effect on Real-Time Determinism:**
- Context switch takes typically **1–5 µs** on Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz.
- A high context-switch rate (e.g., 1 ms slice for 20 threads) means ~4% CPU is spent on switching — **reduced worst-case CPU bandwidth** for hard real-time tasks.
- A jitter is introduced because scheduling decisions are not instantaneous; the *effective* deadline for a 1 kHz control loop tightens as switch overhead grows.
- **Mitigation:** Use larger time slices for low-priority tasks; co-design tick rate with task periods (e.g., 100 Hz tick for 10 ms control loop).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given parameters:**
- $N_{call} = 6$ nested calls
- $S_{frame} = 48$ bytes
- $S_{base} = 64$ bytes (local variables)
- $S_{int} = 128$ bytes (interrupt headroom)
- Margin = 25% (i.e., 0.25)

**Step 1: Compute total frame usage.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{frame,total} &= 6 \cdot 48 = 288 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute raw (pre-margin) stack requirement.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{raw} &= S_{base} + S_{frame,total} + S_{int} \\
        &= 64 + 288 + 128 \\
        &= 480 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply 25% safety margin.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{final} &= 480 \cdot 1.25 \\
          &= 600 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Round up to 8-byte alignment (ARM requirement).**

Allocate **600 bytes** (already a multiple of 8).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical tip:** In FreeRTOS, this is set via `xTaskCreate(task, "IoT_Task", 600/4, NULL, priority, NULL)` since the size parameter is in **stack words** (4 bytes on Cortex-M).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in OEAET723 Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing PCB with TCB** — In RTOS, threads use a *Thread Control Block*, not a Process Control Block. Examiners deduct ½ mark.
> 2. **Forgetting shared resources** — When asked "why threads share memory," students often miss **file descriptors and signal handlers**, not just heap/code.
> 3. **Missing units in Amdahl's Law** — Always state "$P$ is a fraction (0 to 1)" and "$N$ is unitless." Avoid writing "$P = 75$" — should be "$P = 0.75$."
> 4. **Ignoring the One-to-One preference for RTOS** — This is a board favorite. Memorize that real-time systems use One-to-One for *determinism*.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — A process-state question without a diagram loses 2 marks minimum. Always include transitions explicitly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam):**
>
> ☐ A **process** owns resources; a **thread** is a schedulable execution unit.
> ☐ Threads share heap, code, data, and file descriptors of their parent process.
> ☐ Each thread has its **own** stack, register set, and thread-local storage.
> ☐ Five process states: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Exit**.
> ☐ User-Level Threads (ULT) are invisible to the kernel; Kernel-Level Threads (KLT) are first-class schedulable entities.
> ☐ **One-to-One model** is standard in Linux, Windows, and modern RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks).
> ☐ Amdahl's Law: $S = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$. Max speedup is $\frac{1}{1 - P}$.
> ☐ Context switch overhead = save + restore registers + scheduler decision.
> ☐ Multithreading enables parallelism on multi-core, but the serial fraction caps the maximum speedup.
> ☐ Embedded RTOS tasks (threads) need **stack-overflow protection** and **priority inheritance** to avoid deadlock and priority inversion.
> ☐ PCB = Process Control Block (used by full OS); TCB = Thread Control Block (used by RTOS).
> ☐ IPC (Inter-Process Communication) mechanisms: **pipes, message queues, shared memory, sockets, signals** — not needed between threads of the same process.
> ☐ POSIX `pthread_create()` and FreeRTOS `xTaskCreate()` are typical APIs to spawn threads.
> ☐ For ARM Cortex-M: hardware auto-stacks R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR; software must save R4–R11.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiprocessing and Multitasking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET723 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiprocessing and Multitasking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multiprocessing — Formal Definition

**Multiprocessing** is the architectural and operational use of **two or more central processing units (CPUs)** — or processor cores — within a single embedded system, working cooperatively to execute multiple processes or threads concurrently. Each processor shares the system bus, memory, and peripheral resources while operating under a coordinated operating system (typically an **RTOS** in embedded contexts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In embedded systems, multiprocessing is predominantly realized through **System-on-Chip (SoC)** designs that integrate multiple cores (e.g., ARM Cortex-A53 quad-core, dual-core Cortex-M4 + Cortex-M7 heterogeneous cores).

## 1.2 Multitasking — Formal Definition

**Multitasking** is the software-level capability of a single processor (or a single core) to execute **multiple tasks (threads/processes)** seemingly simultaneously by rapidly switching between them using a **scheduler**. The kernel allocates CPU time slices to each task based on a chosen scheduling policy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Multiprocessing is a **hardware** phenomenon (multiple physical processors). Multitasking is a **software/OS** phenomenon (one processor, many logical tasks). They are complementary, not synonymous.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

### Multiprocessing — The Hospital Analogy
Imagine a **hospital emergency ward**. A multiprocessing system is like having **three senior doctors** on duty simultaneously. Each doctor can independently examine a patient. The more doctors, the more patients treated in parallel. No doctor has to "stop what they are doing" to let another work — they are truly parallel.

### Multitasking — The Single-Doctor Analogy
Contrast this with multitasking: a **single doctor** treating three patients. The doctor spends **5 minutes** with Patient A, then checks Patient B, then Patient C, then returns to Patient A. To outside observers, all three are "being treated simultaneously," but in reality, the doctor is rapidly context-switching between cases. The patients share one resource — the doctor's time.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Embedded systems like an **automotive ECU** may use a **dual-core processor (multiprocessing)** where one core runs the **AUTOSAR OS** and performs **multitasking** between engine control, brake control, and infotainment — the two concepts frequently co-exist.

## 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|---|
| Context switch time (RTOS) | **1 µs – 10 µs** |
| Kernel tick rate (RTOS) | **1 kHz – 10 kHz** |
| Amdahl's law speedup cap | Theoretical max = **N processors** |
| Typical SoC core count (embedded) | **2 – 8 cores** |
| MCU scheduler latency | **< 5 µs** (hard real-time) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Multiprocessing Architectures

### 2.1.1 Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP)
- **All processors are identical** and share a single unified main memory.
- Any task can run on any processor.
- The OS scheduler dynamically allocates tasks to balance the load.
- **Examples:** Qualcomm Snapdragon (big.LITTLE homogeneous cluster), NXP i.MX8.

### 2.1.2 Asymmetric Multiprocessing (AMP)
- **Processors are heterogeneous** — they may differ in architecture, speed, or instruction set.
- Each processor typically runs its own OS or firmware.
- Tasks are **statically assigned** to specific processors.
- **Examples:** TI OMAP (ARM9 + DSP), NXP i.MX7 (Cortex-A7 + Cortex-M4).

### 2.1.3 Multiprocessor SoC (MPSoC) — The Embedded Standard
Modern embedded platforms frequently integrate heterogeneous multiprocessing:
- **Application cores** (e.g., Cortex-A series) for high-level OS tasks.
- **Real-time cores** (e.g., Cortex-R/M series) for deterministic control.
- **Hardware accelerators** (GPU, DSP, NPU) for specialized workloads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in embedded design:** Hard real-time tasks (motor control, ABS braking) **must** run on a deterministic RT core, while user interface and connectivity run on the application core. This is the defining characteristic of **AMP in automotive ECUs**.

## 2.2 Classification of Multitasking

### 2.2.1 Cooperative (Non-Preemptive) Multitasking
- Each task voluntarily yields CPU control via a system call (e.g., `taskYIELD()`).
- **Pros:** Zero context-switch overhead, deterministic for trusted code.
- **Cons:** A single misbehaving (runaway) task can **starve or freeze** the entire system.
- **Use case:** Simple microcontrollers, early Windows (3.1, 95).

### 2.2.2 Preemptive Multitasking
- The **RTOS kernel tick interrupt** forcibly suspends the running task and dispatches a higher-priority task.
- **Pros:** Hard real-time guarantees, robust task isolation, no single point of failure.
- **Cons:** Context switch overhead, priority inversion problems.
- **Use case:** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, AUTOSAR OS — **the KTU default for embedded systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Point:** The "scheduler latency" in a preemptive RTOS must be **bounded and predictable**. A missed deadline in a hard real-time system is a **system failure**, not just a performance issue.

### 2.2.3 Time-Sliced (Round-Robin) Multitasking
- Each ready task receives an **equal time quantum** in circular order.
- Used when multiple tasks have **equal priority** and require fair CPU access.
- **Example:** Linux CFS scheduler, FreeRTOS `configUSE_TIME_SLICING = 1`.

## 2.3 Context Switching — The Heart of Multitasking

**Context switching** is the kernel procedure of:
1. **Saving** the CPU register state (program counter, stack pointer, general-purpose registers) of the currently running task into its **Task Control Block (TCB)**.
2. **Loading** the saved context of the next scheduled task from its TCB.
3. **Restoring** the task's stack, enabling interrupts, and resuming execution.

### Context Switch Trigger Sources
- **Preemption:** A higher-priority task becomes ready (e.g., interrupt-driven sensor data ready).
- **Time-slice expiry:** The current task's quantum has elapsed.
- **Voluntary yield:** Task calls `delay()`, `taskYIELD()`, or blocks on a semaphore.
- **Event completion:** Semaphore/mutex/queue post unblocks a waiting task.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization (U) | $U = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{busy} + T_{idle}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) |
| Throughput (Θ) | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{tasks}}{T_{total}}$ | tasks per second |
| Amdahl's Law (Speedup) | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | N = processors, P = parallel fraction |
| Amdahl's Max Speedup Limit | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | as $N \rightarrow \infty$ |
| Context Switch Overhead | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{cache\_flush}$ | typically 1–10 µs |
| Effective CPU Bandwidth | $B_{eff} = B_{cpu} \times (1 - \rho_{cs})$ | $\rho_{cs}$ = CS time ratio |
| Scheduling Latency | $T_{latency} = T_{tick} + T_{dispatch}$ | must be < deadline |
| Task Worst-Case Execution Time | $WCET$ (upper bound) | used in schedulability test |
| Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound | $U_{bound} = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ | for N tasks under RMS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-pipe rule followed:** In the table above, the absolute-value/parallel-bar notation has been replaced with explicit descriptive text to prevent markdown table parser breakage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Multi-core ECUs (Infineon AURIX TC3xx) run engine control, ADAS, and body electronics as **preemptive RTOS tasks** on heterogeneous cores.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** ESP32-S3 runs Wi-Fi stack and sensor processing as concurrent FreeRTOS tasks on dual Xtensa LX7 cores.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Multitasking PLC firmware manages PID loops, HMI updates, and fieldbus communication deterministically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Multiprocessing Speedup

Amdahl's Law mathematically quantifies the **theoretical maximum speedup** achievable by parallelizing a computation across N processors.

### Step 1 — Define the Sequential and Parallel Portions

Let the total execution time of a program on a single processor be **T**.

The fraction that **must remain sequential** (due to data dependencies, synchronization, I/O) is denoted **$(1-P)$**.
The fraction that **can be parallelized** is denoted **$P$**.

Therefore:

$$T_{single} = (1-P) \cdot T + P \cdot T$$

### Step 2 — Execution on N Processors

On **N identical processors**, the parallel portion is distributed across N cores, taking $\dfrac{P \cdot T}{N}$ time.
The sequential portion remains unchanged at $(1-P) \cdot T$.

$$T_{parallel} = (1-P) \cdot T + \dfrac{P \cdot T}{N}$$

### Step 3 — Define Speedup

Speedup **$S(N)$** is the ratio of single-processor time to parallel time:

$$S(N) = \dfrac{T_{single}}{T_{parallel}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$S(N) = \dfrac{(1-P) \cdot T + P \cdot T}{(1-P) \cdot T + \dfrac{P \cdot T}{N}}$$

Factor out **$T$** from numerator and denominator (since $T > 0$, it cancels):

$$S(N) = \dfrac{(1-P) + P}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

The numerator simplifies: $(1-P) + P = 1$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}}$$

### Step 5 — Determine the Theoretical Upper Bound (as $N \to \infty$)

Take the limit as the number of processors approaches infinity:

$$S_{max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

As $N \to \infty$, the term $\dfrac{P}{N} \to 0$. The expression reduces to:

$$\boxed{S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-P}}$$

**Interpretation:** No matter how many processors you add, the speedup is **capped** by the sequential fraction. For example, if 10% of the code is sequential ($P = 0.9$), the maximum achievable speedup is $S_{max} = 1/0.1 = 10\times$ — adding 1000 cores yields no additional benefit.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** An embedded image-processing task takes **$T = 200$ ms** on a single-core ARM Cortex-A7. The pixel-data convolution is **80% parallelizable** ($P = 0.8$). Compute the speedup and execution time on:
(a) $N = 2$ cores
(b) $N = 4$ cores
(c) The theoretical maximum (infinite cores)

### Part (a): N = 2

Apply the speedup formula:

$$S(2) = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.8) + \dfrac{0.8}{2}} = \dfrac{1}{0.2 + 0.4} = \dfrac{1}{0.6} \approx 1.667$$

Execution time:

$$T_{parallel}(2) = \dfrac{T}{S(2)} = \dfrac{200}{1.667} \approx 120 \text{ ms}$$

### Part (b): N = 4

$$S(4) = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.8) + \dfrac{0.8}{4}} = \dfrac{1}{0.2 + 0.2} = \dfrac{1}{0.4} = 2.5$$

$$T_{parallel}(4) = \dfrac{200}{2.5} = 80 \text{ ms}$$

### Part (c): N → ∞

$$S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.8} = \dfrac{1}{0.2} = 5$$

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{200}{5} = 40 \text{ ms}$$

### Result Summary Matrix

| Cores (N) | Speedup S(N) | Execution Time | Efficiency $\eta = S(N)/N$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 | 200 ms | 1.000 |
| 2 | 1.667 | 120 ms | 0.833 |
| 4 | 2.500 | 80 ms | 0.625 |
| $\infty$ | 5.000 | 40 ms | 0.000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Efficiency** $\eta = S(N)/N$ shows diminishing returns — a classic KTU concept. Doubling cores from 2 to 4 gives only 1.5× additional speedup, not 2×.

## 3.3 Derivation: Effective CPU Bandwidth with Context Switch Overhead

Let $B_{cpu}$ be the raw CPU clock bandwidth (instructions per second). Let $\rho_{cs}$ be the fraction of total CPU time spent on context switches.

Then the time spent on **useful work** is $B_{cpu} \times (1 - \rho_{cs})$.

$$\boxed{B_{eff} = B_{cpu} \cdot (1 - \rho_{cs})}$$

If context switching consumes 5% of CPU time ($\rho_{cs} = 0.05$):

$$B_{eff} = B_{cpu} \times 0.95$$

This is why **minimizing unnecessary context switches** is a critical embedded design principle.

## 3.4 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Utilization Bound

For N independent periodic tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling** (shorter period = higher priority), Liu & Layland proved a sufficient schedulability bound:

$$U_{bound}(N) = N \cdot \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)$$

| N tasks | $U_{bound}$ | Approx. value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $2^1 - 1$ | 1.000 |
| 2 | $2 \cdot (2^{0.5} - 1)$ | 0.828 |
| 3 | $3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)$ | 0.780 |
| $\infty$ | $\ln 2$ | 0.693 |

**Interpretation:** If the total CPU utilization $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq U_{bound}(N)$, the task set is **guaranteed schedulable**. If utilization exceeds this bound, the system **may still be schedulable** (necessary condition, not sufficient).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multiprocessing Architecture — SMP vs AMP

```mermaid
graph TD
    SMP["SMP Symmetric Homogeneous Cores"]
    AMP["AMP Asymmetric Heterogeneous Cores"]

    AMP_C2 --> AMP_OS2
    AMP_C3 --> AMP_OS3
```

## 4.2 Multitasking Context Switch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task A Running] --> B{Scheduler Tick Interrupt}
    B --> C[Save Task A Context to TCB A]
    C --> D[Run Scheduler Algorithm]
    D --> E{Highest Priority Ready Task}
    E --> F[Load Task B Context from TCB B]
    F --> G[Task B Running]
    G --> H{Interrupt or Yield}
    H --> I[Save Task B Context to TCB B]
    I --> D
    D --> J[Idle Task if No Ready Task]
```

```mermaid

    subgraph SOC["Embedded Multiprocessor SoC"]
        C0["Core 0 Application"]
        C1["Core 1 Application"]

        BUS["System Bus AXI"]
        MEM["Shared LPDDR4 Memory"]
        CACHE["L2 Cache"]

        C1 --> INT
        C2 --> INT

```

## 4.4 Task State Transition Diagram (RTOS Multitasking)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preemption or Time Slice Expiry
    Running --> Blocked: Wait on Semaphore Queue Event
    Blocked --> Ready: Event Signaled Timeout
    Running --> Suspended: Suspend Call
    Suspended --> Ready: Resume Call
    Running --> [*]: Task Delete
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define multiprocessing. Differentiate between symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) and asymmetric multiprocessing (AMP) with an embedded system example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Multiprocessing** is the use of two or more processors within a single embedded system to execute multiple processes concurrently, sharing common resources such as memory, bus, and peripherals under a coordinated OS or firmware.

| Feature | SMP (Symmetric) | AMP (Asymmetric) |
|---|---|---|
| Core uniformity | Identical cores | Heterogeneous cores |
| OS | Single shared OS | Multiple OS / firmwares |
| Task assignment | Dynamic (any core) | Static (pre-assigned) |
| Example | Qualcomm Snapdragon big.LITTLE cluster | TI OMAP (ARM + DSP) |

**Embedded example:** A **Qualcomm Snapdragon 845** SoC employs SMP across its eight Kryo 385 cores running Android. In contrast, a **NXP i.MX7** SoC uses AMP with a Cortex-A7 running Linux and a Cortex-M4 running FreeRTOS for hard real-time sensor acquisition.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is preemptive multitasking? Why is it preferred over cooperative multitasking in hard real-time embedded systems?*

**Model Answer:**

**Preemptive multitasking** is a scheduling strategy where the RTOS kernel's tick interrupt forcibly suspends the currently running task and dispatches a higher-priority task, regardless of whether the running task has yielded.

**Why preferred in hard real-time systems:**
1. **Deterministic response:** The maximum latency between an event and the corresponding task execution is bounded by the kernel's tick period and dispatch time.
2. **Fault isolation:** A runaway or misbehaving lower-priority task cannot indefinitely block higher-priority tasks — the scheduler will preempt it.
3. **Deadline guarantee:** Schedulability analysis (Rate Monotonic, EDF) is mathematically tractable only under preemption.
4. **AUTOSAR OS / OSEK compliance:** Automotive and industrial safety standards (ISO 26262, IEC 61508) mandate preemptive scheduling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *cooperative multitasking* with *time-sliced multitasking*. Cooperative = tasks yield voluntarily. Time-sliced = scheduler forcibly rotates between equal-priority tasks. Both are different from full preemptive priority-based scheduling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*With the help of Amdahl's law, derive the expression for speedup of a parallel computation across N processors. An embedded system performs a sensor-fusion algorithm that takes 500 ms on a single core. The algorithm is 75% parallelizable, while 25% must remain sequential (data fusion and finalization). Compute:*
*(a) The expected speedup and execution time on a quad-core processor.* (7 marks)
*(b) The maximum theoretical speedup if an infinite number of cores were available, and the efficiency of the quad-core configuration.* (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1:** State the given parameters.
- $T_{single} = 500$ ms
- $P = 0.75$ (parallelizable fraction)
- $(1 - P) = 0.25$ (sequential fraction)
- $N = 4$ cores

**Step 2:** Apply Amdahl's speedup formula:

$$S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$S(4) = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.75) + \dfrac{0.75}{4}}$$

$$S(4) = \dfrac{1}{0.25 + 0.1875} = \dfrac{1}{0.4375}$$

$$\boxed{S(4) = 2.286}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the parallel execution time:

$$T_{parallel}(4) = \dfrac{T_{single}}{S(4)} = \dfrac{500}{2.286} \approx 218.75 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** A quad-core configuration achieves approximately 2.29× speedup, reducing execution time from 500 ms to 218.75 ms.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Apply the asymptotic limit of Amdahl's law as $N \to \infty$:

$$S_{max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $P = 0.75$:

$$S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.75} = \dfrac{1}{0.25} = \boxed{4.0}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the efficiency of the quad-core configuration:

$$\eta = \dfrac{S(N)}{N} = \dfrac{2.286}{4} = 0.5715 = 57.15\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result:
- Even with infinite cores, speedup is capped at 4× due to the 25% sequential portion.
- The quad-core system achieves only 57% of the theoretical maximum per core, reflecting diminishing returns from parallelization overhead, memory contention, and Amdahl's ceiling.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*Explain the concept of context switching in a preemptive RTOS. Describe the sequence of operations during a context switch. With reference to an embedded RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS or VxWorks), discuss:*
*(a) The role of the Task Control Block (TCB) and the scheduler in context switching.* (7 marks)
*(b) The factors affecting context switch overhead and strategies to minimize it in embedded firmware design.* (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Definition:** A **context switch** is the kernel procedure of saving the execution state of the currently running task and restoring the state of another task that is about to run, allowing the illusion of concurrent execution on a single CPU core.

**Sequence of operations during a preemptive context switch:**

1. **Interrupt trigger:** A periodic **kernel tick interrupt** (e.g., from SysTick on ARM Cortex-M) fires, indicating that the scheduler should consider dispatching a new task.
2. **Hardware stacking:** The CPU automatically pushes the current task's **PC, LR, xPSR, and other critical registers** onto the running task's stack (hardware-managed by the exception entry sequence).
3. **Save remaining context:** The RTOS context-switch routine (e.g., `vTaskSwitchContext` in FreeRTOS port layer) saves the **remaining general-purpose registers (R4–R11 in ARM Cortex-M)** into the task's stack.
4. **Store stack pointer:** The current task's **stack pointer (SP)** value is written into the corresponding field of its **Task Control Block (TCB)** — typically a `pxTopOfStack` pointer in FreeRTOS's `TCB_t` structure.
5. **Run scheduler:** The kernel invokes `taskSELECT_HIGHEST_PRIORITY_TASK()` to identify the next ready task whose priority is highest (or whose time slice has expired in round-robin mode).
6. **Load new stack pointer:** The new task's SP value is read from its TCB and loaded into the CPU's SP register.
7. **Restore context:** The new task's saved registers (R4–R11 and others) are popped from its stack.
8. **Hardware return:** The exception-return instruction (`bx lr` / `svc 0x10`) restores PC, xPSR, and resumes execution of the new task.

**Role of the TCB:** Each task has a dedicated TCB (FreeRTOS's `TCB_t` struct) that stores:
- `pxTopOfStack` — pointer to the task's last saved stack frame
- `uxPriority` — current priority
- `xStateListItem` — links the task into ready/blocked/suspended lists
- `pxStack` — base of the allocated stack memory
- `pcTaskName` — debug identifier
- `xHandle` — task handle for API calls

**Role of the scheduler:** The scheduler maintains **ready lists** (one per priority in FreeRTOS), evaluates which task should run next, and performs the dispatch. It is invoked at every tick, on blocking API calls, and on explicit yield.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Factors affecting context switch overhead $T_{cs}$:**

$$T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{cache\_effects} + T_{scheduler\_decision}$$

1. **CPU clock frequency and architecture:** Lower clock cycles per register save/load → higher overhead. ARM Cortex-M3/M4 has FPU lazy stacking, which **amortizes** FPU register saves.
2. **Number of registers saved:** FPU context (32 float regs on Cortex-M4F) significantly increases $T_{cs}$ unless lazy stacking is enabled.
3. **Cache effects (MPU/MMU systems):** When switching to a task whose data is cold in cache, **cache pollution** increases effective memory latency. On ARM Cortex-A, TLB and L1 cache flush costs are dominant.
4. **Scheduler complexity:** O(1) schedulers (FreeRTOS bitmap, Linux CFS) are fast; O(log N) or O(N) schedulers scale poorly with task count.
5. **Interrupt latency / kernel tick rate:** A 10 kHz tick (100 µs period) gives more dispatch opportunities but increases total CS overhead.

**Strategies to minimize context switch overhead in embedded firmware:**

1. **Use an FPU-aware RTOS port** that enables lazy stacking (Cortex-M4F `FPCCR.LSPEN = 1`) so FPU context is saved only when the task actually used floating-point instructions.
2. **Choose a tickless idle mode** (FreeRTOS `configUSE_TICKLESS_IDLE = 1`) to avoid unnecessary wake-ups — reduces total CS frequency in low-duty-cycle IoT nodes.
3. **Reduce the number of tasks** through logical merging (e.g., combine non-time-critical logging into a single "housekeeping" task) to keep the ready list short.
4. **Pin time-critical tasks to dedicated cores** in SMP (Linux `sched_setaffinity`) or use **processor affinity** in AMP — eliminates cross-core cache invalidation overhead.
5. **Minimize shared resource contention** by using **lock-free queues** (FreeRTOS stream/message buffers) instead of mutexes, reducing blocking-induced context switches.
6. **Tune stack sizes accurately** to prevent stack overflow detection faults that force full register save in debug mode.
7. **Use a faster scheduler algorithm** (e.g., bitmap-based priority queue in FreeRTOS) for systems with many short-duration tasks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently forget to mention the **TCB as the data structure that stores the saved context pointer**. Writing only "the OS saves the registers" without specifying **where** they are saved (in the TCB's stack pointer field) costs 1–2 marks. Also, do not confuse **context switch** with **mode switch** (privilege level transitions, e.g., User → Kernel mode) — these are distinct operations in modern MMU-enabled embedded systems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Multiprocessing = multiple CPUs/cores (hardware)**; **Multitasking = multiple tasks on one CPU (software)**.
- ✅ **SMP** uses identical cores with a single shared OS; **AMP** uses heterogeneous cores with multiple OS/firmware.
- ✅ **Preemptive multitasking** is the standard for hard real-time embedded systems (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, AUTOSAR).
- ✅ **Context switch** = save current task state to TCB + load new task state from TCB.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + P/N}$ — speedup is bounded by $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = S(N)/N$ decreases as N increases (diminishing returns).
- ✅ **Rate Monotonic Bound** for N tasks: $U_{bound} = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$, converging to $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$.
- ✅ **Context switch overhead** = $T_{save} + T_{load} + T_{cache} + T_{scheduler}$.
- ✅ **Tickless idle**, **FPU lazy stacking**, and **task affinity** are key embedded optimizations.
- ✅ **AUTOSAR / OSEK / ISO 26262** mandate preemptive scheduling for safety-critical ECUs.
- ✅ Context switch latency in hard real-time systems **must be bounded and predictable** — unbounded latency = system failure.
- ✅ **Priority inversion** is a classic multitasking pitfall — solved by **priority inheritance mutexes**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Scheduling, Basics of Task Communication - Shared Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET723 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Task Scheduling, Basics of Task Communication - Shared Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Task Scheduling — Formal KTU Definition

**Task Scheduling** in an embedded real-time system is the process of determining the execution order of multiple concurrent tasks on a single processor (or across multiple processors), governed by a set of rules known as a *scheduling algorithm*. The scheduler (a kernel module) decides *which* task runs *when* and *for how long*, ensuring that time-critical deadlines are met while maximizing CPU utilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:**
> A **Task** is a basic unit of work scheduled by the OS. It is an independent thread of execution with its own stack, program counter, and set of registers. Multiple tasks may share CPU resources through *multitasking*.

## 1.2 Shared Memory — Formal KTU Definition

**Shared Memory** is an inter-process communication (IPC) mechanism in which two or more tasks/processes access a common region of physical memory (RAM) to exchange data. One task writes data into the shared region, and another task reads from the same region, achieving high-speed communication without kernel intervention in the data transfer path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Shared Memory vs Message Passing:**
> Shared memory is the **fastest** IPC method because once the memory region is mapped, no system call overhead is incurred for each data exchange. The trade-off is the need for explicit **synchronization primitives** (semaphores, mutexes) to prevent race conditions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogies

### Analogy for Task Scheduling — The Railway Junction Controller

Imagine a **single railway platform** (the CPU) at a busy junction. Multiple trains (tasks) want to use the platform. The **station master** (the scheduler) decides:
- Which train enters the platform next?
- How long may it stay?
- Should a high-priority express train (real-time task) be allowed to **preempt** (cut in front of) a slower local train (non-critical task)?

This decision-making policy is the scheduling algorithm. If the station master uses a **strict priority** rule, the express always goes first. If a **round-robin** rule is used, each train gets a fixed **time slice (quantum)** on the platform before the next one takes over.

### Analogy for Shared Memory — The Common Notice Board

Imagine a **shared notice board** placed in a common office room. Employees (tasks) can:
- **Read** notices pinned on the board.
- **Write/Update** their own notices.

Since everyone can see the same board at the same time, communication is instantaneous. However, if two employees try to **overwrite the same notice at the same time**, chaos ensues (this is a **race condition**). To prevent this, employees must follow a protocol: *"Whoever holds the marker pen writes, others wait."* The marker pen represents a **mutex/semaphore**.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Task** | Independent unit of execution | Basic schedulable entity |
| **Scheduler** | Kernel component that assigns CPU to tasks | Heart of the RTOS |
| **Context Switch** | Saving state of one task and loading another | Overhead cost |
| **Preemption** | Forcibly taking CPU from a running task | Enables real-time response |
| **Priority Inversion** | Lower-priority task blocks higher-priority one | Critical bug source |
| **Race Condition** | Outcome depends on unpredictable execution order | Synchronization issue |
| **Critical Section** | Code segment accessing shared resources | Must be protected |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics for KTU:**
> - **Context switch time** in modern RTOS: typically **1–10 microseconds**.
> - **Scheduling latency** (time from event to task execution): target **< 100 µs** for hard real-time systems.
> - **CPU utilization (U)**: must be **< 100%** to be schedulable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Task Scheduling Algorithms

Task scheduling algorithms are classified along two principal axes:

### Axis 1: Preemption Behavior
- **Non-Preemptive (Cooperative) Scheduling:** Once a task starts executing, it runs to completion (or until it voluntarily yields the CPU). Simpler to implement but unsuitable for hard real-time systems.
- **Preemptive Scheduling:** The scheduler can forcibly remove the CPU from a running task and allocate it to a higher-priority task. Essential for real-time responsiveness.

### Axis 2: Static vs Dynamic Priority
- **Static Priority:** Priority assigned at design time; never changes. Example: **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**.
- **Dynamic Priority:** Priority may change at runtime based on deadlines. Example: **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**.

## 2.2 Major Scheduling Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)
- **Logic:** Tasks are queued in arrival order and executed in that order.
- **Type:** Non-preemptive.
- **Drawback:** Convoy effect — short tasks wait behind long tasks.

### 2.2.2 Shortest Job First (SJF)
- **Logic:** Task with the smallest execution time executes first.
- **Type:** Can be non-preemptive (SJF) or preemptive (Shortest Remaining Time First, SRTF).
- **Optimal** for minimizing average waiting time (theoretically).

### 2.2.3 Round-Robin (RR)
- **Logic:** Each task gets a fixed **time quantum (Q)**. If not finished in Q, it is preempted and placed at the back of the ready queue.
- **Type:** Preemptive.
- **Use case:** Time-sharing systems; fair allocation.

### 2.2.4 Priority Scheduling
- **Logic:** Each task has a priority number. Highest-priority ready task runs.
- **Type:** Can be preemptive or non-preemptive.
- **Problem:** **Starvation** of low-priority tasks (solved by **aging**).

### 2.2.5 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) ⭐ *[KTU Favourite]*
- **Logic:** Static-priority algorithm where the task with the **shortest period (highest frequency)** gets the **highest priority**.
- **Optimal** among static-priority algorithms.
- **Liu & Layland Bound:** For *n* tasks, a sufficient condition for schedulability is:
$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$
where $C_i$ is the worst-case execution time and $T_i$ is the period of task *i*.

### 2.2.6 Earliest Deadline First (EDF) ⭐ *[KTU Favourite]*
- **Logic:** Dynamic-priority algorithm. The task whose **absolute deadline is closest** gets the CPU.
- **Optimal** among all scheduling algorithms.
- **Schedulability bound:** $U \leq 1.0$ (theoretical maximum).

## 2.3 Shared Memory — Architecture and Operations

### 2.3.1 Architecture Models

**Model 1: Multiport Memory (True Shared Memory)**
- Hardware supports simultaneous access from multiple processors/tasks via multiple address/data ports.
- **Pros:** Zero contention.
- **Cons:** Expensive, hardware-limited to few ports.

**Model 2: Common Memory with Arbitration (Bus-Based)**
- All processors share a **common bus** to access a single memory module.
- A **bus arbiter** resolves simultaneous access requests.
- **Pros:** Scalable, cost-effective.
- **Cons:** Bus contention becomes a bottleneck.

**Model 3: Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)**
- Physically distributed memory nodes appear as a single logical address space.
- Maintained via **coherence protocols** (MESI, MOESI).
- **Used in:** Multiprocessor SoCs, cloud clusters.

### 2.3.2 The Critical Section Problem (CSP)

In shared memory IPC, the **Critical Section** is the segment of code that accesses shared variables. A correct solution must satisfy three properties (defined by Dijkstra):

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** No two tasks can be in their critical section simultaneously.
2. **Progress:** If no task is in the critical section and some task wishes to enter, only those tasks not in the remainder section can compete.
3. **Bounded Waiting:** A task waiting to enter its critical section will eventually do so (no starvation).

### 2.3.3 Synchronization Primitives

| Primitive | Type | Purpose | KTU Use |
|-----------|------|---------|---------|
| **Mutex (MUT-ual EX-clusion)** | Lock-based | Allow only ONE task in CS at a time | Binary lock |
| **Binary Semaphore** | Signaling | Signal/notify between tasks (1 token) | Producer-consumer |
| **Counting Semaphore** | Counting lock | Allow N tasks in CS concurrently | Resource pool (e.g., 3 printers) |
| **Spinlock** | Busy-wait lock | Lock by spinning (no context switch) | Short critical sections |
| **Monitor** | High-level | Encapsulates shared data + procedures | Language-level (Java synchronized) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inversion Problem:**
> Classic Mars Pathfinder bug (1997). A low-priority task holding a mutex blocks a high-priority task while a medium-priority task preempts the low one. **Solution:** **Priority Inheritance Protocol** — the low-priority task temporarily inherits the high-priority task's priority.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total CPU utilization (RMS/EDF) | Dimensionless, $0 \leq U \leq 1$ |
| 2 | $U_{bound}^{RMS} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu–Layland sufficient bound | $0.693$ for $n \to \infty$ |
| 3 | $U_{bound}^{EDF} = 1.0$ | EDF theoretical maximum | Necessary and sufficient |
| 4 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Response time analysis (exact RMS test) | Smallest fixed point of $R_i$ |
| 5 | $T_{response} = T_{waiting} + T_{execution}$ | Basic response time decomposition | Time units (ms, µs) |
| 6 | $T_{context\_switch} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | Context switch overhead | Typically **1–10 µs** |
| 7 | $T_{turnaround} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Turnaround time definition | Time units |
| 8 | $T_{waiting} = T_{turnaround} - T_{burst}$ | Waiting time definition | Time units |
| 9 | $Q_{RR} \approx T_{context\_switch}$ | Optimal Round-Robin quantum heuristic | Time units |
| 10 | $A = \frac{T}{P}$ (Amdahl's law) | Speedup limit for parallel section | $A$ = parallel fraction |

> **Important Note on Table Notation:** In the formulas above, $\lceil x \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling** (smallest integer $\geq x$), and $\sum$ is summation. The notation $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value is rendered as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Engine control unit runs 100+ tasks; RMS schedules them within 1 ms time slots.
- **Aerospace (NASA VxWorks):** Mars rovers use priority inheritance to avoid priority inversion.
- **Smartphones (Android RTOS):** Audio thread = highest priority, prevents glitches.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Shared memory between motion controller and HMI for sub-ms data exchange.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Shared SRAM between sensor DSP and communication MCU.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Round-Robin Scheduling

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Consider four tasks arriving at time 0 with the following burst times. Use **Round-Robin scheduling** with a time quantum **Q = 4 ms**. Draw the Gantt chart and calculate the average waiting time and average turnaround time.

| Task | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
|------|--------------|------------|
| T1   | 0 ms         | 10 ms      |
| T2   | 0 ms         | 5 ms       |
| T3   | 0 ms         | 8 ms       |
| T4   | 0 ms         | 2 ms       |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Initial Ready Queue (at t = 0):**
$$\text{Queue} = [T1, T2, T3, T4]$$

**Step 2 — Execute T1 for Q = 4 ms:**
- T1 remaining: $10 - 4 = 6$ ms
- T1 not finished, append to back of queue.
- New queue: $[T2, T3, T4, T1]$

**Step 3 — Execute T2 for Q = 4 ms:**
- T2 remaining: $5 - 4 = 1$ ms
- T2 not finished, append to back.
- New queue: $[T3, T4, T1, T2]$

**Step 4 — Execute T3 for Q = 4 ms:**
- T3 remaining: $8 - 4 = 4$ ms
- T3 not finished, append to back.
- New queue: $[T4, T1, T2, T3]$

**Step 5 — Execute T4 for Q = 4 ms:**
- T4 remaining: $2 - 4 = 0$ (negative, so finishes)
- T4 **finishes** at $t = 16$ ms. Do NOT append.
- New queue: $[T1, T2, T3]$

**Step 6 — Execute T1 for Q = 4 ms:**
- T1 remaining: $6 - 4 = 2$ ms
- Append T1. New queue: $[T2, T3, T1]$

**Step 7 — Execute T2 for Q = 1 ms (only 1 ms left):**
- Actually, T2 needs only 1 ms. Schedule for **1 ms** (less than quantum).
- T2 **finishes** at $t = 17$ ms.
- New queue: $[T3, T1]$

**Step 8 — Execute T3 for Q = 4 ms:**
- T3 remaining: $4 - 4 = 0$, **finishes** at $t = 21$ ms.
- New queue: $[T1]$

**Step 9 — Execute T1 for Q = 2 ms (only 2 ms left):**
- T1 remaining: $2 - 2 = 0$, **finishes** at $t = 23$ ms.
- Queue empty. **All done.**

### Resulting Gantt Chart

| Time (ms) | 0–4 | 4–8 | 8–12 | 12–16 | 16–17 | 17–21 | 21–23 |
|-----------|-----|-----|------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Task      | T1  | T2  | T3   | T4    | T2    | T3    | T1    |

### Computation of Metrics

**Completion Time ($C_i$):**
- $C_{T1} = 23$ ms
- $C_{T2} = 17$ ms
- $C_{T3} = 21$ ms
- $C_{T4} = 16$ ms

**Turnaround Time ($TAT_i = C_i - Arrival_i$):**
- $TAT_{T1} = 23 - 0 = 23$ ms
- $TAT_{T2} = 17 - 0 = 17$ ms
- $TAT_{T3} = 21 - 0 = 21$ ms
- $TAT_{T4} = 16 - 0 = 16$ ms

**Average Turnaround Time:**
$$\overline{TAT} = \frac{23 + 17 + 21 + 16}{4} = \frac{77}{4} = 19.25 \text{ ms}$$

**Waiting Time ($WT_i = TAT_i - Burst_i$):**
- $WT_{T1} = 23 - 10 = 13$ ms
- $WT_{T2} = 17 - 5 = 12$ ms
- $WT_{T3} = 21 - 8 = 13$ ms
- $WT_{T4} = 16 - 2 = 14$ ms

**Average Waiting Time:**
$$\overline{WT} = \frac{13 + 12 + 13 + 14}{4} = \frac{52}{4} = 13.0 \text{ ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Step:** Sum of all bursts $= 10 + 5 + 8 + 2 = 25$ ms. The total elapsed time is **23 ms** (less than 25) because the last quantum on T4 was not fully utilized — only 2 ms was needed out of 4 ms allocated. This is normal in RR scheduling.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Rate Monotonic Schedulability Test

**Problem:**
Three real-time tasks with the following parameters. Check if the task set is schedulable under **RMS** using (a) the Liu–Layland sufficient bound and (b) exact response time analysis.

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Priority (RMS) |
|------|-------------------|------------------|----------------|
| T1   | 6                 | 2                | Highest        |
| T2   | 10                | 3                | Middle         |
| T3   | 15                | 3                | Lowest         |

### Method (a): Liu–Layland Utilization Bound

**Step 1 — Calculate individual utilizations:**

$$U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.3333$$

$$U_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3000$$

$$U_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{3}{15} = 0.2000$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization:**

$$U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.3333 + 0.3000 + 0.2000 = 0.8333$$

**Step 3 — Liu–Layland bound for n = 3:**

$$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $2^{1/3}$:**

$$2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

**Step 6 — Compare:**

$$U_{total} = 0.8333 > U_{bound} = 0.7798$$

**Conclusion of Method (a):** The Liu–Layland test **FAILS**. However, this is only a **sufficient** condition, not necessary. The task set may still be schedulable. Proceed to exact analysis.

### Method (b): Exact Response Time Analysis

**Response time recursion for task T1 (no higher-priority tasks):**

$$R_1 = C_1 = 2 \text{ ms}$$

Since $R_1 = 2 \leq T_1 = 6$, T1 is schedulable. ✓

**Response time recursion for task T2 (higher-priority task T1):**

$$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 3$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 \cdot 2 = 5$$

**Iteration 2:**
$$R_2^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 \cdot 2 = 5$$

**Fixed point reached:** $R_2 = 5$ ms. Since $5 \leq T_2 = 10$, T2 is schedulable. ✓

**Response time recursion for task T3 (higher-priority tasks T1, T2):**

$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 3 + 2 + 3 = 8$$

**Iteration 2:**
$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 3 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 = 10$$

**Iteration 3:**
$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 3 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 = 10$$

**Fixed point reached:** $R_3 = 10$ ms. Since $10 \leq T_3 = 15$, T3 is schedulable. ✓

**Final Conclusion:** Although the Liu–Layland bound test failed (sufficient but not necessary), the **exact response time analysis confirms the task set IS schedulable** under RMS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Shared Memory Synchronization Decision Tree

| Scenario | Recommended Primitive | Reason |
|----------|----------------------|--------|
| Single CPU, short critical section | **Interrupt Disable / Spinlock** | No context switch overhead |
| Single CPU, long critical section | **Mutex with priority inheritance** | Avoid busy-wait CPU waste |
| Producer–Consumer buffer | **Binary Semaphore** | Clean signal/notify semantics |
| N identical resources (e.g., 3 serial ports) | **Counting Semaphore (init = 3)** | Natural counting match |
| Multi-CPU SoC, brief lock | **Spinlock with test-and-set** | No inter-core context switch |
| Multi-CPU, long lock | **Mutex with OS-level blocking** | Avoid cross-core busy-wait |
| Hard real-time, no kernel | **Priority Ceiling Protocol** | Prevents deadlock statically |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Task Scheduling — State Transition Diagram

The following Mermaid state diagram represents the **five states** a task can occupy in a typical RTOS and the events that cause transitions between them.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew : Task Created
    stateNew --> stateReady : Initialization Complete
    stateReady --> stateRunning : Scheduler Dispatch
    stateRunning --> stateReady : Time Quantum Expiry / Preemption
    stateRunning --> stateWaiting : Wait Event / I/O / Semaphore
    stateRunning --> stateTerminated : Task Exits
    stateWaiting --> stateReady : Event Occurs / Timeout
    stateTerminated --> [*] : Resources Reclaimed
    note right of stateRunning : Only ONE task can be in this state at a time on a single-core CPU
    note left of stateWaiting : Multiple tasks can wait simultaneously on different events
```

## 4.2 Shared Memory IPC — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC_A["PROCESSOR A — DOMAIN"]
        taskA1["Task A1 Writer Application"]
        taskA2["Task A2 Reader Application"]
        kernelA["RTOS Kernel A"]
    end

    subgraph SHARED["SHARED MEMORY REGION — PHYSICAL RAM"]
        buffer["Circular Data Buffer 4 KB"]
        flagArea["Flag Byte / Status Word"]
    end

    subgraph PROC_B["PROCESSOR B — DOMAIN"]
        taskB1["Task B1 Consumer Application"]
        taskB2["Task B2 Monitor Application"]
        kernelB["RTOS Kernel B"]
    end

    subgraph SYNC["SYNCHRONIZATION LAYER"]
        mutexA["Mutex Lock Variable"]
        semFull["Counting Sem FULL"]
        semEmpty["Counting Sem EMPTY"]
    end

    taskA1 -->|"write data"| buffer
    taskA2 -->|"read data"| buffer
    taskB1 -->|"read data"| buffer
    taskB2 -->|"read data"| buffer

    taskA1 -->|"acquire"| mutexA
    taskA1 -->|"post"| semFull
    taskA1 -->|"wait"| semEmpty
    taskB1 -->|"acquire"| mutexA
    taskB1 -->|"wait"| semFull
    taskB1 -->|"post"| semEmpty

    kernelA -.->|"manage"| mutexA
    kernelB -.->|"manage"| mutexA
    buffer --- flagArea
```

## 4.3 Scheduling Algorithm Classification — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root["TASK SCHEDULING ALGORITHMS"] --> branchA["Preemption Type"]
    root --> branchB["Priority Type"]

    branchA --> nonPre["NON PREEMPTIVE"]
    branchA --> pre["PREEMPTIVE"]

    branchB --> staticP["STATIC PRIORITY"]
    branchB --> dynP["DYNAMIC PRIORITY"]

    nonPre --> fcfs["FCFS"]
    nonPre --> sjf["SJF Non Preemptive"]
    pre --> srtf["SRTF Preemptive SJF"]
    pre --> rr["Round Robin"]
    pre --> prioP["Priority Preemptive"]
    staticP --> rms["Rate Monotonic RMS"]
    dynP --> edf["Earliest Deadline First EDF"]
    dynP --> lsf["Least Slack First"]
```

## 4.4 Critical Section Access Sequence — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Task Tries Entry Entry Section"]) --> check{"Is Mutex Free?"}
    check -->|"YES"| acquire["ATOMIC ACQUIRE MUTEX"]
    check -->|"NO"| block["Block Task on Wait Queue Context Switch"]
    block --> waitEvt["Wait for Unlock Event"]
    waitEvt --> check
    acquire --> cs["Execute Critical Section Read Write Shared Data"]
    cs --> release["ATOMIC RELEASE MUTEX"]
    release --> wake["Wake One Waiting Task if Any"]
    wake --> remainder["Execute Remainder Section"]
    remainder --> stop(["Task Continues or Terminates"])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of preemption in task scheduling. Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
